question_html: Show that {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
question_text: Show that {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} is an increasing function in {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
explanation: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} For critical points let f '(x)=0 {tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} = 0{/tex} cosx (1 - tanx) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx = 0, or 1 - tanx = 0 since {tex}{ \tan x < 1~~\forall \ x\in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} {/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) < 0~~\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} Hence f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
solution: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} For critical points let f '(x)=0 {tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} = 0{/tex} cosx (1 - tanx) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx = 0, or 1 - tanx = 0 since {tex}{ \tan x < 1~~\forall \ x\in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} {/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) < 0~~\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} Hence f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex}
{tex}f'\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}.\left( {\cos x - \sin x} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
For critical points let  f '(x)=0
{tex}\frac{{\cos x\left( {1 - \tan x} \right)}}{{1 + {{\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right)}^2}}} = 0{/tex}
cosx (1 - tanx) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx = 0, or 1 - tanx = 0 
since {tex}{ \tan x < 1~~\forall \ x\in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} {/tex}
{tex}f'\left( x \right) < 0~~\forall x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}
Hence f(x) is strictly increasing on {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}

question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function given by f(x) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 10 } x ^ { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 5 } x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 36 x } { 5 } + 11{/tex} is

  1. strictly increasing
  2. strictly decreasing
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function given by f(x) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 10 } x ^ { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 5 } x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 36 x } { 5 } + 11{/tex} is strictly increasing strictly decreasing
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} x 4 - {tex}\frac{4}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - 3x 2 + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} x + 11 f'(x) = {tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex} x 2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} If we put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex} x 2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 3 - 2x 2 - 5x + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x + 2)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -2, 3 Now, we will draw these values of x on number line. From the number line, we can see that, For x {tex}\in{/tex} (−2,1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f(x) is increasing. For x {tex}\in{/tex} (− {tex}\infty{/tex} ,−2) {tex}\cup{/tex} (1,3), f(x) is decreasing.
solution: f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} x 4 - {tex}\frac{4}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - 3x 2 + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} x + 11 f'(x) = {tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex} x 2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} If we put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex} x 3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex} x 2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 3 - 2x 2 - 5x + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x + 2)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -2, 3 Now, we will draw these values of x on number line. From the number line, we can see that, For x {tex}\in{/tex} (−2,1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f(x) is increasing. For x {tex}\in{/tex} (− {tex}\infty{/tex} ,−2) {tex}\cup{/tex} (1,3), f(x) is decreasing.
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{{10}}{/tex}x4 - {tex}\frac{4}{{5}}{/tex}x3 - 3x2 + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex}x + 11
f'(x) = {tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex}x3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex}x2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex}
If we put f'(x) = 0
{tex}\frac{6}{{5}}{/tex}x3 - {tex}\frac{12}{{5}}{/tex}x2 - 6x + {tex}\frac{36}{{5}}{/tex} = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x3 - 2x2 - 5x + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x + 2)(x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, -2, 3
Now, we will draw these values of x on number line.

From the number line, we can see that,
For x {tex}\in{/tex} (−2,1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (3, {tex}\infty{/tex}), f(x) is increasing.
For x {tex}\in{/tex} (−{tex}\infty{/tex},−2) {tex}\cup{/tex} (1,3), f(x) is decreasing.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by
f (x) = 2x3 – 15x2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by f (x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
explanation: We have f(x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x + 1 or f ′(x) = 6x 2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2) Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3. We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So f(1) = 2(1 3 ) – 15(1 2 ) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24 f(2) = 2(2 3 ) – 15(2 2 ) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29 f(3) = 2(3 3 ) – 15(3 2 ) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28 f(5) = 2(5 3 ) – 15(5 2 ) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56 Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.
solution: We have f(x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x + 1 or f ′(x) = 6x 2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2) Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3. We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So f(1) = 2(1 3 ) – 15(1 2 ) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24 f(2) = 2(2 3 ) – 15(2 2 ) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29 f(3) = 2(3 3 ) – 15(3 2 ) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28 f(5) = 2(5 3 ) – 15(5 2 ) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56 Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.
solution_html:
We have
f(x) = 2x3 – 15x2 + 36x + 1
or f ′(x) = 6x2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2)
Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3.
We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So
f(1) = 2(13) – 15(12) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24
f(2) = 2(23) – 15(22) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29
f(3) = 2(33) – 15(32) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28
f(5) = 2(53) – 15(52) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56
Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.

question_html:
Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1 is

  1. strictly increasing
  2. strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 is strictly increasing strictly decreasing.
explanation: Here,it is given the function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = - 6x 2 - 18x - 12 = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) = -6(x + 2)(x + 1) ... (i) f(x) is strictly increasing {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -6(x + 2)(x + 1) [from (i)] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -2 < x < -1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2 ,-1]. f(x) is strictly decreasing {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} - 6{x + 2)(x +1) < 0 [from (i)] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} [(x + 2) > 0 and (x + 1) > 0] or [(x + 2) < 0 and (x +1) < 0] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x >-2 and x > -1) or (x < -2 and x < -1) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x > -1) or.(x < -2) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x\in[-1, \infty]{/tex} or {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]{/tex} {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is strictly decreasing on {tex}[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex}
solution: Here,it is given the function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = - 6x 2 - 18x - 12 = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) = -6(x + 2)(x + 1) ... (i) f(x) is strictly increasing {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -6(x + 2)(x + 1) [from (i)] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -2 < x < -1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2 ,-1]. f(x) is strictly decreasing {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} - 6{x + 2)(x +1) < 0 [from (i)] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) > 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} [(x + 2) > 0 and (x + 1) > 0] or [(x + 2) < 0 and (x +1) < 0] {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x >-2 and x > -1) or (x < -2 and x < -1) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x > -1) or.(x < -2) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x\in[-1, \infty]{/tex} or {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]{/tex} {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is strictly decreasing on {tex}[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,it is given the function
f(x) = -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = - 6x2 - 18x - 12 = -6(x2 + 3x + 2)
= -6(x + 2)(x + 1) ... (i)

  1. f(x) is strictly increasing 
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0 
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -6(x + 2)(x + 1) [from (i)]
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) < 0
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -2 < x < -1
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2 ,-1].
  2. f(x) is strictly decreasing
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} - 6{x + 2)(x +1) < 0 [from (i)]
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x +1) > 0
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} [(x + 2) > 0 and (x + 1) > 0]
    or [(x + 2) < 0 and (x +1) < 0]
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x >-2 and x > -1) or (x < -2 and x < -1)
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x > -1) or.(x < -2) 
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x\in[-1, \infty]{/tex} or {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]{/tex}
    {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is strictly decreasing on {tex}[-\infty,-2]\cup [1-1, \infty]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A technical company is designing a rectangular solar panel installation on a roof using 300 metres of boundary material. The design includes a partition running parallel to one of the sides dividing the area (roof) into two sections.
Let the length of the side perpendicular to the partition be {tex}x{/tex} metres and with parallel to the partition be {tex}y{/tex} metres.
Based on this information, answer the following questions:

  1. Write the equation for the total boundary material used in the boundary and parallel to the partition in terms of {tex}x{/tex} and {tex}y{/tex}. (1)
  2. Write the area of the solar panel as a function of {tex}x{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the critical points of the area function. Use second derivative test to determine critical points at the maximum area. Also, find the maximum area. (2)

      OR

    2. Using first derivative test, calculate the maximum area the company can enclose with the 300 metres of boundary material, considering the parallel partition. (2)
question_text: A technical company is designing a rectangular solar panel installation on a roof using 300 metres of boundary material. The design includes a partition running parallel to one of the sides dividing the area (roof) into two sections. Let the length of the side perpendicular to the partition be {tex}x{/tex} metres and with parallel to the partition be {tex}y{/tex} metres. Based on this information, answer the following questions: Write the equation for the total boundary material used in the boundary and parallel to the partition in terms of {tex}x{/tex} and {tex}y{/tex} . (1) Write the area of the solar panel as a function of {tex}x{/tex} . (1) Find the critical points of the area function. Use second derivative test to determine critical points at the maximum area. Also, find the maximum area. (2) OR Using first derivative test, calculate the maximum area the company can enclose with the 300 metres of boundary material, considering the parallel partition. (2)
explanation: {tex}{2} \boldsymbol{x}+{3} \boldsymbol{y}={3 0 0}{/tex} {tex}A=x y=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x){/tex} {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{4}{3} \lt 0{/tex} . So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex} Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} OR {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} changes its sign from positive to negative as {tex}x{/tex} passes through {tex}x=75{/tex} from left to right, which means {tex}x=75{/tex} is the point of maximum. Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} Note: Full credit to be given if the student takes equation as {tex}2 x+2 y=300{/tex} or {tex}2 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+3 y=300{/tex} The solutions of sub-parts will differ and marks may be given accordingly.
solution: {tex}{2} \boldsymbol{x}+{3} \boldsymbol{y}={3 0 0}{/tex} {tex}A=x y=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x){/tex} {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{4}{3} \lt 0{/tex} . So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex} Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} OR {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} changes its sign from positive to negative as {tex}x{/tex} passes through {tex}x=75{/tex} from left to right, which means {tex}x=75{/tex} is the point of maximum. Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} Note: Full credit to be given if the student takes equation as {tex}2 x+2 y=300{/tex} or {tex}2 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+3 y=300{/tex} The solutions of sub-parts will differ and marks may be given accordingly.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{2} \boldsymbol{x}+{3} \boldsymbol{y}={3 0 0}{/tex}
  2. {tex}A=x y=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x){/tex}
    1. {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex}
      For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex}
      Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{4}{3} \lt 0{/tex}. So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex}
      Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex}
      For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex}
      As {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} changes its sign from positive to negative as {tex}x{/tex} passes through
      {tex}x=75{/tex} from left to right, which means {tex}x=75{/tex} is the point of maximum.
      Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex}
      Note: Full credit to be given if the student takes equation as
      {tex}2 x+2 y=300{/tex} or {tex}2 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+3 y=300{/tex}
      The solutions of sub-parts will differ and marks may be given accordingly.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The side of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex}. At what rate its area increasing when the side of the triangle is 15 cm?
question_text: The side of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} . At what rate its area increasing when the side of the triangle is 15 cm?
explanation: Let' {tex}a{/tex} 'be the side of the triangle, so {tex}\frac{d a}{d t}=3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} Now area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2} \times \frac{d a}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left.\therefore \frac{d A}{d t}\right]_{a=15 {~cm}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \times 15}{2} \times 3=\frac{45 \sqrt{3}}{2} {~cm}^{2} / {s}{/tex}
solution: Let' {tex}a{/tex} 'be the side of the triangle, so {tex}\frac{d a}{d t}=3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} Now area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2} \times \frac{d a}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left.\therefore \frac{d A}{d t}\right]_{a=15 {~cm}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \times 15}{2} \times 3=\frac{45 \sqrt{3}}{2} {~cm}^{2} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let' {tex}a{/tex} 'be the side of the triangle, so {tex}\frac{d a}{d t}=3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
Now area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2} \times \frac{d a}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\therefore \frac{d A}{d t}\right]_{a=15 {~cm}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \times 15}{2} \times 3=\frac{45 \sqrt{3}}{2} {~cm}^{2} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the intervals in which function {tex}{f}(x)=5 x^{\frac{3}{2}}-3 x^{\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} is (i) increasing (ii) decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which function {tex}{f}(x)=5 x^{\frac{3}{2}}-3 x^{\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} is (i) increasing (ii) decreasing.
explanation: {tex} f(x)=5 x^{3 / 2}-3 x^{5 / 2} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{15}{2} \sqrt{x}(1-x) {/tex} For increasing/decreasing, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=0,1 {/tex} When {tex}x \in[0,1], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} . So, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing when {tex}x \in[0,1]{/tex} (The intervals {tex}(0,1),[0,1){/tex} or {tex}(0,1]{/tex} can also be considered.) When {tex}x \in[1, \infty), f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} .So, {tex}f{/tex} is decreasing when {tex}x \in[1, \infty){/tex} (The interval( {tex}1, \infty{/tex} )can also be considered.)
solution: {tex} f(x)=5 x^{3 / 2}-3 x^{5 / 2} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{15}{2} \sqrt{x}(1-x) {/tex} For increasing/decreasing, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=0,1 {/tex} When {tex}x \in[0,1], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} . So, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing when {tex}x \in[0,1]{/tex} (The intervals {tex}(0,1),[0,1){/tex} or {tex}(0,1]{/tex} can also be considered.) When {tex}x \in[1, \infty), f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} .So, {tex}f{/tex} is decreasing when {tex}x \in[1, \infty){/tex} (The interval( {tex}1, \infty{/tex} )can also be considered.)
solution_html:
{tex} f(x)=5 x^{3 / 2}-3 x^{5 / 2} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{15}{2} \sqrt{x}(1-x) {/tex}
For increasing/decreasing, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=0,1 {/tex}

  1. When {tex}x \in[0,1], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}. So, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing when {tex}x \in[0,1]{/tex}
    (The intervals {tex}(0,1),[0,1){/tex} or {tex}(0,1]{/tex} can also be considered.)
  2. When {tex}x \in[1, \infty), f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex}.So, {tex}f{/tex} is decreasing when {tex}x \in[1, \infty){/tex}
    (The interval( {tex}1, \infty{/tex} )can also be considered.)

question_html: For the curve {tex}{y}=5 {x}-2 {x}^{3}{/tex}, if x increases at the rate of 2 units/s, then how fast is the slope of the curve changing when {tex}{x}=2{/tex}?
question_text: For the curve {tex}{y}=5 {x}-2 {x}^{3}{/tex} , if x increases at the rate of 2 units/s, then how fast is the slope of the curve changing when {tex}{x}=2{/tex} ?
explanation: {tex} y=5 x-2 x^3 {/tex} {tex} \text { Given } \frac{d x}{d t}=2 \text { units } / {s} {/tex} {tex} \text { slope of the curve }=\frac{d y}{d x}=5-6 x^2=m {/tex} {tex} \frac{d m}{d t}=-12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=-12 x(2)=-24 x {/tex} {tex} \text { at } x=2, \frac{d m}{d t}=-24(2)=-48 {/tex} Hence, slope of curve is decreasing at the rate of 48
solution: {tex} y=5 x-2 x^3 {/tex} {tex} \text { Given } \frac{d x}{d t}=2 \text { units } / {s} {/tex} {tex} \text { slope of the curve }=\frac{d y}{d x}=5-6 x^2=m {/tex} {tex} \frac{d m}{d t}=-12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=-12 x(2)=-24 x {/tex} {tex} \text { at } x=2, \frac{d m}{d t}=-24(2)=-48 {/tex} Hence, slope of curve is decreasing at the rate of 48
solution_html:
{tex} y=5 x-2 x^3 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Given } \frac{d x}{d t}=2 \text { units } / {s} {/tex}
{tex} \text { slope of the curve }=\frac{d y}{d x}=5-6 x^2=m {/tex}
{tex} \frac{d m}{d t}=-12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=-12 x(2)=-24 x {/tex}
{tex} \text { at } x=2, \frac{d m}{d t}=-24(2)=-48 {/tex}
Hence, slope of curve is decreasing at the rate of 48

question_html: If {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}, x \geq 1{/tex}, show that {tex}f{/tex} is an increasing function.
question_text: If {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}, x \geq 1{/tex} , show that {tex}f{/tex} is an increasing function.
explanation: {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}} \geq 0{/tex} for all {tex}x \geq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0 \Rightarrow f{/tex} is an increasing function.
solution: {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}} \geq 0{/tex} for all {tex}x \geq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0 \Rightarrow f{/tex} is an increasing function.
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
Now {tex}\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}} \geq 0{/tex} for all {tex}x \geq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0 \Rightarrow f{/tex} is an increasing function.

question_html: Find the least value of 'a' so that {tex}{f}({x})=2 {x}^{2}-{ax}+3{/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}[2,4]{/tex}.
question_text: Find the least value of 'a' so that {tex}{f}({x})=2 {x}^{2}-{ax}+3{/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}[2,4]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=2 x^{2}-a x+3 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x-a{/tex} Now {tex}2 \leq x \leq 4 \Rightarrow 8-a \leq 4 x-a \leq 16-a{/tex} For {tex}f{/tex} to be an increasing function, {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 8-a \geq 0 \Rightarrow a \leq 8{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Least value of {tex}a{/tex} does not exist.
solution: {tex}f(x)=2 x^{2}-a x+3 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x-a{/tex} Now {tex}2 \leq x \leq 4 \Rightarrow 8-a \leq 4 x-a \leq 16-a{/tex} For {tex}f{/tex} to be an increasing function, {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 8-a \geq 0 \Rightarrow a \leq 8{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Least value of {tex}a{/tex} does not exist.
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=2 x^{2}-a x+3 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x-a{/tex}
Now {tex}2 \leq x \leq 4 \Rightarrow 8-a \leq 4 x-a \leq 16-a{/tex}
For {tex}f{/tex} to be an increasing function, {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 8-a \geq 0 \Rightarrow a \leq 8{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Least value of {tex}a{/tex} does not exist.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A spherical medicine ball when dropped in water dissolves in such a way that the rate of decrease of volume at any instant is proportional to its surface area. Calculate the rate of decrease of its radius.
question_text: A spherical medicine ball when dropped in water dissolves in such a way that the rate of decrease of volume at any instant is proportional to its surface area. Calculate the rate of decrease of its radius.
explanation: Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' and ' {tex}S{/tex} ' be the volume and surface area of the spherical medicine ball with radius ' {tex}r{/tex} '. {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-k S, k \gt 0{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-k S=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-k\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}=-k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius decreases at a constant rate.
solution: Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' and ' {tex}S{/tex} ' be the volume and surface area of the spherical medicine ball with radius ' {tex}r{/tex} '. {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-k S, k \gt 0{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-k S=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-k\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}=-k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius decreases at a constant rate.
solution_html:
Let '{tex}V{/tex}' and '{tex}S{/tex}' be the volume and surface area of the spherical medicine ball with radius '{tex}r{/tex}'.
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-k S, k \gt 0{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-k S=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-k\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}=-k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius decreases at a constant rate.

question_html:
A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres.
Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex}. (2)
  2. Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
question_text: A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex} , where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres. Based on the above, answer the following: Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} . (2) Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
explanation: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex} For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex} , hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex} For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex} , hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution_html:
1. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex}
    For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex}, hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex}
    Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex}
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.

question_html: Find the values of 'a' for which {tex}{f}(x)=\sqrt{3} \sin x-\cos x-2 {a} x+{b}{/tex} is decreasing on {tex}\mathbb{R}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the values of 'a' for which {tex}{f}(x)=\sqrt{3} \sin x-\cos x-2 {a} x+{b}{/tex} is decreasing on {tex}\mathbb{R}{/tex} .
explanation: ' {tex}f{/tex} ' is a decreasing function iff {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0 \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left({x}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq {a}{/tex} We know that for all {tex}x \in R,-1 \leq \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}
solution: ' {tex}f{/tex} ' is a decreasing function iff {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0 \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left({x}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq {a}{/tex} We know that for all {tex}x \in R,-1 \leq \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}
solution_html:
'{tex}f{/tex}' is a decreasing function iff {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0 \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left({x}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq {a}{/tex}
We know that for all {tex}x \in R,-1 \leq \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of '{tex}a{/tex}' for which {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{3} \sin x-\cos x-2 a x+6{/tex} is decreasing in R.
question_text: Find the value of ' {tex}a{/tex} ' for which {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{3} \sin x-\cos x-2 a x+6{/tex} is decreasing in R.
explanation: Since {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} is a decreasing function {tex}\Rightarrow {f}^{\prime}({x}) \leq {0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cdot \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos x+\frac{1}{2} \sin x\right)-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq a{/tex} Since, {tex}-1 \leq \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq 1 \Rightarrow a \geq 1{/tex} i.e. {tex}a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}
solution: Since {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} is a decreasing function {tex}\Rightarrow {f}^{\prime}({x}) \leq {0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cdot \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos x+\frac{1}{2} \sin x\right)-2 a \leq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq a{/tex} Since, {tex}-1 \leq \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq 1 \Rightarrow a \geq 1{/tex} i.e. {tex}a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}
solution_html:
Since {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} is a decreasing function {tex}\Rightarrow {f}^{\prime}({x}) \leq {0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \cdot \cos x+\sin x-2 a \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos x+\frac{1}{2} \sin x\right)-2 a \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq a{/tex}
Since, {tex}-1 \leq \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \leq 1 \Rightarrow a \geq 1{/tex} i.e. {tex}a \in[1, \infty){/tex} or {tex}(1, \infty){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Surface area of a balloon (spherical), when air is blown into it, increases at a rate of {tex}5 {~mm}^{2} / {s}{/tex}. When the radius of the balloon is 8 mm, find the rate at which the volume of the balloon is increasing.
question_text: Surface area of a balloon (spherical), when air is blown into it, increases at a rate of {tex}5 {~mm}^{2} / {s}{/tex} . When the radius of the balloon is 8 mm, find the rate at which the volume of the balloon is increasing.
explanation: {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=5 {~mm}^{2} / {s}, \left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=?{/tex} {tex}{S}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=8 \pi {r} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{8 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi {r}^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{2} {r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=20 {~mm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=5 {~mm}^{2} / {s}, \left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=?{/tex} {tex}{S}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=8 \pi {r} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{8 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi {r}^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{2} {r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=20 {~mm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=5 {~mm}^{2} / {s}, \left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=?{/tex}
{tex}{S}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{dS}}{{dt}}=8 \pi {r} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{8 \pi {r}}{/tex}
{tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi {r}^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=4 \pi {r}^{2} \cdot \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}=\frac{5}{2} {r}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=8}=20 {~mm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}

question_html: A spherical balloon has a variable diameter {tex}\frac{2}{3}(2 t+1){/tex}. Find the rate of change of its volume with respect to {tex}t{/tex}.
question_text: A spherical balloon has a variable diameter {tex}\frac{2}{3}(2 t+1){/tex} . Find the rate of change of its volume with respect to {tex}t{/tex} .
explanation: Clearly radius {tex}=\frac{2 t+1}{3}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \frac{(2 t+1)^{3}}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{8 \pi}{27}(2 t+1)^{2}{/tex}
solution: Clearly radius {tex}=\frac{2 t+1}{3}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \frac{(2 t+1)^{3}}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{8 \pi}{27}(2 t+1)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Clearly radius {tex}=\frac{2 t+1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}{V}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \frac{(2 t+1)^{3}}{27}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{8 \pi}{27}(2 t+1)^{2}{/tex}

question_html: Find the interval/intervals in which the function {tex}{f}({x})=\sin 3 {x}-\cos 3 {x}{/tex}, {tex}0 \lt {x} \lt \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} is strictly increasing.
question_text: Find the interval/intervals in which the function {tex}{f}({x})=\sin 3 {x}-\cos 3 {x}{/tex} , {tex}0 \lt {x} \lt \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} is strictly increasing.
explanation: {tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})={3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { c o s }} {3} {x}+{3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {3} {x}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \sin 3 x=-\cos 3 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} For {tex}{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right), 3 \cos 3 {x}+3 \sin 3 {x} \gt 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \gt 0, f{/tex} is strictly increasing function in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
solution: {tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})={3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { c o s }} {3} {x}+{3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {3} {x}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \sin 3 x=-\cos 3 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} For {tex}{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right), 3 \cos 3 {x}+3 \sin 3 {x} \gt 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \gt 0, f{/tex} is strictly increasing function in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})={3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { c o s }} {3} {x}+{3} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {3} {x}{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \sin 3 x=-\cos 3 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
For {tex}{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right), 3 \cos 3 {x}+3 \sin 3 {x} \gt 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \gt 0, f{/tex} is strictly increasing function in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex}

question_html: Let the volume of a metallic hollow sphere be constant. If the inner radius increases at the rate of {tex}2 {~cm} / {s}{/tex}, find the rate of increase of the outer radius when the radii are 2 cm and 4 cm respectively.
question_text: Let the volume of a metallic hollow sphere be constant. If the inner radius increases at the rate of {tex}2 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} , find the rate of increase of the outer radius when the radii are 2 cm and 4 cm respectively.
explanation: {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=2 {~cm} / s_{1}, \left(\frac{{dR}}{{dt}}\right)_{{R}=4, {r}=2}=?{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(3 R^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3 {r}^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}\right){/tex} When {tex}{R} \boldsymbol{=} {4} {c m}{/tex} and {tex}{r} \boldsymbol{=} {2 c m}{/tex} , {tex}0=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left[3(4)^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3(2)^{2}(2)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d R}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=2 {~cm} / s_{1}, \left(\frac{{dR}}{{dt}}\right)_{{R}=4, {r}=2}=?{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(3 R^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3 {r}^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}\right){/tex} When {tex}{R} \boldsymbol{=} {4} {c m}{/tex} and {tex}{r} \boldsymbol{=} {2 c m}{/tex} , {tex}0=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left[3(4)^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3(2)^{2}(2)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d R}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=2 {~cm} / s_{1}, \left(\frac{{dR}}{{dt}}\right)_{{R}=4, {r}=2}=?{/tex}
{tex}{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(3 R^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3 {r}^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}\right){/tex}
When {tex}{R} \boldsymbol{=} {4} {c m}{/tex} and {tex}{r} \boldsymbol{=} {2 c m}{/tex},
{tex}0=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left[3(4)^{2} \cdot \frac{d R}{d t}-3(2)^{2}(2)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d R}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The area of an expanding rectangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}48 {cm}^{2} / {s}{/tex}. The length of the rectangle is always square of its breadth. At what rate the length of rectangle increasing at an instant, when breadth {tex}=4.5 {~cm}{/tex}?
question_text: The area of an expanding rectangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}48 {cm}^{2} / {s}{/tex} . The length of the rectangle is always square of its breadth. At what rate the length of rectangle increasing at an instant, when breadth {tex}=4.5 {~cm}{/tex} ?
explanation: Let the length and breadth of the expanding rectangle at any time be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' respectively. Then, {tex}x=y^{2},{/tex} A(Area {tex})=x y=x^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t} =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 48 =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{(4.5)^{2}} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{64}{9} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution: Let the length and breadth of the expanding rectangle at any time be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' respectively. Then, {tex}x=y^{2},{/tex} A(Area {tex})=x y=x^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t} =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 48 =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{(4.5)^{2}} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{64}{9} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the length and breadth of the expanding rectangle at any time be '{tex}x{/tex}' and '{tex}y{/tex}' respectively.
Then, {tex}x=y^{2},{/tex} A(Area{tex})=x y=x^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d A}{d t} =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 48 =\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{(4.5)^{2}} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{64}{9} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A cylindrical water container has developed a leak at the bottom. The water is leaking at the rate of {tex}5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} from the leak. If the radius of the container is 15 cm , find the rate at which the height of water is decreasing inside the container, when the height of water is 2 metres.
question_text: A cylindrical water container has developed a leak at the bottom. The water is leaking at the rate of {tex}5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} from the leak. If the radius of the container is 15 cm , find the rate at which the height of water is decreasing inside the container, when the height of water is 2 metres.
explanation: Let {tex}V, r, h{/tex} be the volume, radius and height of cylindrical container. Given {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\pi r^{2} h=\pi(15)^{2} h=225 \pi h{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d t}=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \Rightarrow-5=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}=-\frac{5}{225 \pi}=-\frac{1}{45 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Height of the water is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{45 \pi} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}V, r, h{/tex} be the volume, radius and height of cylindrical container. Given {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\pi r^{2} h=\pi(15)^{2} h=225 \pi h{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d t}=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \Rightarrow-5=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}=-\frac{5}{225 \pi}=-\frac{1}{45 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Height of the water is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{45 \pi} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}V, r, h{/tex} be the volume, radius and height of cylindrical container.
Given {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=-5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
{tex}{V}=\pi r^{2} h=\pi(15)^{2} h=225 \pi h{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d t}=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \Rightarrow-5=225 \pi \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}=-\frac{5}{225 \pi}=-\frac{1}{45 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Height of the water is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{45 \pi} {~cm} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that of all the rectangles with a fixed perimeter, the square has the greatest area.
question_text: Show that of all the rectangles with a fixed perimeter, the square has the greatest area.
explanation: Let {tex}{P}{/tex} be the perimeter of the rectangle, which is a constant. Also assume ' {tex}{x}{/tex} ' and ' {tex}{y}{/tex} ' be the length and breadth of the rectangle, then {tex}2(x+y)=P \text { and } A(\text { Area }){/tex} {tex}=x y=\frac{x}{2}(P-2 x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(P x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}(P-4 x), \therefore A^{\prime}(x)=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{P}{4}, y=\frac{P}{4}{/tex} {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(x)=-2 \lt 0 \text { at } x=\frac{P}{4},{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the rectangle is max. if it is a square. }{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{P}{/tex} be the perimeter of the rectangle, which is a constant. Also assume ' {tex}{x}{/tex} ' and ' {tex}{y}{/tex} ' be the length and breadth of the rectangle, then {tex}2(x+y)=P \text { and } A(\text { Area }){/tex} {tex}=x y=\frac{x}{2}(P-2 x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(P x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}(P-4 x), \therefore A^{\prime}(x)=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{P}{4}, y=\frac{P}{4}{/tex} {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(x)=-2 \lt 0 \text { at } x=\frac{P}{4},{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the rectangle is max. if it is a square. }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{P}{/tex} be the perimeter of the rectangle, which is a constant. Also assume '{tex}{x}{/tex}' and '{tex}{y}{/tex}' be the length and breadth of the rectangle, then
{tex}2(x+y)=P \text { and } A(\text { Area }){/tex} {tex}=x y=\frac{x}{2}(P-2 x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(P x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}A^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}(P-4 x), \therefore A^{\prime}(x)=0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{P}{4}, y=\frac{P}{4}{/tex}
{tex}A^{\prime \prime}(x)=-2 \lt 0 \text { at } x=\frac{P}{4},{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \text { Area of the rectangle is max. if it is a square. }{/tex}

{tex} {f}^{\prime}({x})=1-\frac{1}{{x}^2}=\frac{{x}^2-1}{{x}^2} {/tex}

question_html: Find the values of 'a' for which {tex}{f}(x)=x^{2}-2 {a} x+{b}{/tex} is an increasing function for {tex}x \gt 0{/tex}.
question_text: Find the values of 'a' for which {tex}{f}(x)=x^{2}-2 {a} x+{b}{/tex} is an increasing function for {tex}x \gt 0{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-2 a{/tex} {tex}0 \lt x \lt \infty \Rightarrow-2 a \lt 2 x-2 a \lt \infty {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \lt f^{\prime}(x) \lt \infty{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing if {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \in[0, \infty) \Rightarrow a \in(-\infty, 0]{/tex} or {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}
solution: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-2 a{/tex} {tex}0 \lt x \lt \infty \Rightarrow-2 a \lt 2 x-2 a \lt \infty {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \lt f^{\prime}(x) \lt \infty{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing if {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \in[0, \infty) \Rightarrow a \in(-\infty, 0]{/tex} or {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-2 a{/tex}
{tex}0 \lt x \lt \infty \Rightarrow-2 a \lt 2 x-2 a \lt \infty {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \lt f^{\prime}(x) \lt \infty{/tex}
{tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing if {tex}f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-2 a \in[0, \infty) \Rightarrow a \in(-\infty, 0]{/tex} or {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}

question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{8}{3} x^{3}-12 x^{2}+18 x+5{/tex}.
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{8}{3} x^{3}-12 x^{2}+18 x+5{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} f(x)=\frac{8}{3} x^3-12 x^2+18 x+5 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=8 x^2-24 x+18 {/tex} {tex} =2\left(4 x^2-12 x+9\right)=2(2 x-3)^2 {/tex} For critical points, Put {tex}f(x)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2(2 x-3)^2=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{3}{2} {/tex} since {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} does not change the sign as crosses {tex}x=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} from left to right, {tex}f{/tex} has no local maxima or local minima.
solution: {tex} f(x)=\frac{8}{3} x^3-12 x^2+18 x+5 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=8 x^2-24 x+18 {/tex} {tex} =2\left(4 x^2-12 x+9\right)=2(2 x-3)^2 {/tex} For critical points, Put {tex}f(x)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2(2 x-3)^2=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{3}{2} {/tex} since {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} does not change the sign as crosses {tex}x=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} from left to right, {tex}f{/tex} has no local maxima or local minima.
solution_html:
{tex} f(x)=\frac{8}{3} x^3-12 x^2+18 x+5 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=8 x^2-24 x+18 {/tex}
{tex} =2\left(4 x^2-12 x+9\right)=2(2 x-3)^2 {/tex}
For critical points, Put {tex}f(x)=0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2(2 x-3)^2=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{3}{2} {/tex}
since {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} does not change the sign as crosses {tex}x=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} from left to right, {tex}f{/tex} has no local maxima or local minima.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The radius of a cylinder is decreasing at a rate of {tex}2 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} and the altitude is increasing at the rate of {tex}3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex}. Find the rate of change of volume of this cylinder when its radius is 4 cm and altitude is 6 cm.
question_text: The radius of a cylinder is decreasing at a rate of {tex}2 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} and the altitude is increasing at the rate of {tex}3 {~cm} / {s}{/tex} . Find the rate of change of volume of this cylinder when its radius is 4 cm and altitude is 6 cm.
explanation: {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=-2 {~cm} / s, \frac{d h}{d t}=3 {~cm} / s,\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=4, {~h}=6}=?{/tex} {tex}{~V}=\pi {r}^{2} {~h} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi r \cdot \frac{d r}{d t} \cdot {~h}+\pi {r}^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} When {tex}r=4 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}h=6 {~cm}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi(4)(-2)(6)+\pi(4)^{2}(3)=-48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} Volume is decreasing at the rate of {tex}48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=-2 {~cm} / s, \frac{d h}{d t}=3 {~cm} / s,\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=4, {~h}=6}=?{/tex} {tex}{~V}=\pi {r}^{2} {~h} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi r \cdot \frac{d r}{d t} \cdot {~h}+\pi {r}^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} When {tex}r=4 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}h=6 {~cm}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi(4)(-2)(6)+\pi(4)^{2}(3)=-48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} Volume is decreasing at the rate of {tex}48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=-2 {~cm} / s, \frac{d h}{d t}=3 {~cm} / s,\left(\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right)_{{r}=4, {~h}=6}=?{/tex}
{tex}{~V}=\pi {r}^{2} {~h} \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi r \cdot \frac{d r}{d t} \cdot {~h}+\pi {r}^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
When {tex}r=4 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}h=6 {~cm}{/tex},
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi(4)(-2)(6)+\pi(4)^{2}(3)=-48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}
Volume is decreasing at the rate of {tex}48 \pi {~cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find dimensions of a rectangle of perimeter 12 cm which will generate maximum volume when swept along a circular rotation keeping the shorter side fixed as the axis.
question_text: Find dimensions of a rectangle of perimeter 12 cm which will generate maximum volume when swept along a circular rotation keeping the shorter side fixed as the axis.
explanation: Let Length and Breadth of the rectangle be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' respectively. Also ' {tex}r{/tex} ' be the radius of the cylinder then, {tex}2(x+y)=12 \Rightarrow x+y=6,2 \pi r=x{/tex} {tex}V({/tex} Volume of cylinder) {tex}=\pi r^{2} y {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V=\pi\left(\frac{x}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}(6-x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(6 x^{2}-x^{3}\right){/tex} {tex}V^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(12 x-3 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=0 \Rightarrow x=4,(\because x \neq 0){/tex} {tex}V^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}(12-6 x) \Rightarrow V^{\prime \prime}(4)=-\frac{3}{\pi} \lt 0{/tex} The volume of the cylinder obtained by the rotation will be maximum if the dimensions of the rectangle are {tex}x=4 {~cm}, y=2 {~cm}{/tex} .
solution: Let Length and Breadth of the rectangle be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' respectively. Also ' {tex}r{/tex} ' be the radius of the cylinder then, {tex}2(x+y)=12 \Rightarrow x+y=6,2 \pi r=x{/tex} {tex}V({/tex} Volume of cylinder) {tex}=\pi r^{2} y {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V=\pi\left(\frac{x}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}(6-x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(6 x^{2}-x^{3}\right){/tex} {tex}V^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(12 x-3 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=0 \Rightarrow x=4,(\because x \neq 0){/tex} {tex}V^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}(12-6 x) \Rightarrow V^{\prime \prime}(4)=-\frac{3}{\pi} \lt 0{/tex} The volume of the cylinder obtained by the rotation will be maximum if the dimensions of the rectangle are {tex}x=4 {~cm}, y=2 {~cm}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let Length and Breadth of the rectangle be '{tex}x{/tex}' and '{tex}y{/tex}' respectively. Also '{tex}r{/tex}' be the radius of the cylinder then,
{tex}2(x+y)=12 \Rightarrow x+y=6,2 \pi r=x{/tex}
{tex}V({/tex} Volume of cylinder) {tex}=\pi r^{2} y {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V=\pi\left(\frac{x}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}(6-x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(6 x^{2}-x^{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}V^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi}\left(12 x-3 x^{2}\right){/tex}{tex}=0 \Rightarrow x=4,(\because x \neq 0){/tex}
{tex}V^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi}(12-6 x) \Rightarrow V^{\prime \prime}(4)=-\frac{3}{\pi} \lt 0{/tex}
The volume of the cylinder obtained by the rotation will be maximum if the dimensions of the rectangle are {tex}x=4 {~cm}, y=2 {~cm}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle on its length. Let the rectangular part have length and breadth x and y metres respectively.
Based on the given information, answer the following questions:

  1. If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, find the relation between x and y. (1)
  2. Using the expression obtained in(i), write an expression for the area of the window as a function of {tex}x{/tex} only. (1)
    1. Find the dimensions of the rectangle that will allow maximum light through the window. (use expression obtained in(ii)) (2)

      OR

    2. If it is given that the area of the window is 50 m2, find an expression for its perimeter in terms of x. (2)
question_text: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle on its length. Let the rectangular part have length and breadth x and y metres respectively. Based on the given information, answer the following questions: If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, find the relation between x and y. (1) Using the expression obtained in(i), write an expression for the area of the window as a function of {tex}x{/tex} only. (1) Find the dimensions of the rectangle that will allow maximum light through the window. (use expression obtained in(ii)) (2) OR If it is given that the area of the window is 50 m 2 , find an expression for its perimeter in terms of x. (2)
explanation: Perimeter {tex}(P)=3 x+2 y=12{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Area}(A)=x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=x\left(\frac{12-3 x}{2}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=6 x-\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x{/tex} For maximum light, {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-3+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \lt 0 \therefore A{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Now, {tex}y=\frac{12-3 x}{2}=6-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{18-6 \sqrt{3}}{6-\sqrt{3}} {~m}{/tex} OR {tex}x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}=50{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x{/tex} Now, {tex}P=3 x+2 y{/tex} {tex}=3 x+2\left(\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x\right) m{/tex}
solution: Perimeter {tex}(P)=3 x+2 y=12{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Area}(A)=x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=x\left(\frac{12-3 x}{2}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=6 x-\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x{/tex} For maximum light, {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-3+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \lt 0 \therefore A{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Now, {tex}y=\frac{12-3 x}{2}=6-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{18-6 \sqrt{3}}{6-\sqrt{3}} {~m}{/tex} OR {tex}x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}=50{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x{/tex} Now, {tex}P=3 x+2 y{/tex} {tex}=3 x+2\left(\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x\right) m{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Perimeter {tex}(P)=3 x+2 y=12{/tex}
  2. {tex}\operatorname{Area}(A)=x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=x\left(\frac{12-3 x}{2}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=6 x-\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex}
    1. {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x{/tex}
      For maximum light, {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow 6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex}
      Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-3+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \lt 0 \therefore A{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex}
      Now, {tex}y=\frac{12-3 x}{2}=6-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{18-6 \sqrt{3}}{6-\sqrt{3}} {~m}{/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}=50{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x{/tex}
      Now, {tex}P=3 x+2 y{/tex}
      {tex}=3 x+2\left(\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x\right) m{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
The relation between the height of the plant ({tex}y {~cm}{/tex}) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the equation {tex}{y}=4 {x}-\frac{1}{2} {x}^{2}{/tex}, where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight.

  1. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (2)
  2. In how many days will the plant attain its maximum height? What is the maximum height? (3)
question_text: The relation between the height of the plant ( {tex}y {~cm}{/tex} ) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the equation {tex}{y}=4 {x}-\frac{1}{2} {x}^{2}{/tex} , where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (2) In how many days will the plant attain its maximum height? What is the maximum height? (3)
explanation: Rate of Growth of the Plant: The rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is the derivative of the height function {tex}y{/tex} with respect to time {tex}x{/tex} . The height function is given by: {tex} y=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex} Differentiating with respect to {tex}x{/tex} , we get: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex} Thus, the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex} Maximum Height: To find when the plant reaches its maximum height, we set the rate of growth {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} to zero: {tex} 4-x-0 \rightarrow x-4 . {/tex} Thus, the plant reaches its maximum height in 4 days. Now, substitute {tex}x=4{/tex} into the height equation to find the maximum height: {tex} y-4(4)-\frac{1}{2}(4)^2-16-8-8 {~cm} {/tex} Hence, the maximum height of the plant is 8 cm.
solution: Rate of Growth of the Plant: The rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is the derivative of the height function {tex}y{/tex} with respect to time {tex}x{/tex} . The height function is given by: {tex} y=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex} Differentiating with respect to {tex}x{/tex} , we get: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex} Thus, the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex} Maximum Height: To find when the plant reaches its maximum height, we set the rate of growth {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} to zero: {tex} 4-x-0 \rightarrow x-4 . {/tex} Thus, the plant reaches its maximum height in 4 days. Now, substitute {tex}x=4{/tex} into the height equation to find the maximum height: {tex} y-4(4)-\frac{1}{2}(4)^2-16-8-8 {~cm} {/tex} Hence, the maximum height of the plant is 8 cm.
solution_html:
1. Rate of Growth of the Plant: The rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is the derivative of the height function {tex}y{/tex} with respect to time {tex}x{/tex}. The height function is given by:
    {tex} y=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex}
    Differentiating with respect to {tex}x{/tex}, we get:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex}
    Thus, the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex}

  2. Maximum Height: To find when the plant reaches its maximum height, we set the rate of growth {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} to zero:
    {tex} 4-x-0 \rightarrow x-4 . {/tex}
    Thus, the plant reaches its maximum height in 4 days. Now, substitute {tex}x=4{/tex} into the height equation to find the maximum height:
    {tex} y-4(4)-\frac{1}{2}(4)^2-16-8-8 {~cm} {/tex}
    Hence, the maximum height of the plant is 8 cm.

question_html:
A ladder of fixed length '{tex}h{/tex}' is to be placed along the wall such that it is free to move along the height of the wall.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Express the distance (y) between the wall and foot of the ladder in terms of '{tex}h{/tex}' and height ( {tex}x{/tex} ) on the wall at a certain instant. Also, write an expression in terms of {tex}h{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} for the area (A) of the right triangle, as seen from the side by an observer. (1)
  2. Find the derivative of the area (A) with respect to the height on the wall (x), and find its critical point. (1)
    1. Show that the area (A) of the right triangle is maximum at the critical point. (2)

      OR

    2. If the foot of the ladder whose length is 5 m , is being pulled towards the wall such that the rate of decrease of distance (y) is {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex}, then at what rate is the height on the wall (x) increasing, when the foot of the ladder is 3 m away from the wall? (2)
question_text: A ladder of fixed length ' {tex}h{/tex} ' is to be placed along the wall such that it is free to move along the height of the wall. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Express the distance (y) between the wall and foot of the ladder in terms of ' {tex}h{/tex} ' and height ( {tex}x{/tex} ) on the wall at a certain instant. Also, write an expression in terms of {tex}h{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} for the area (A) of the right triangle, as seen from the side by an observer. (1) Find the derivative of the area (A) with respect to the height on the wall (x), and find its critical point. (1) Show that the area (A) of the right triangle is maximum at the critical point. (2) OR If the foot of the ladder whose length is 5 m , is being pulled towards the wall such that the rate of decrease of distance (y) is {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex} , then at what rate is the height on the wall (x) increasing, when the foot of the ladder is 3 m away from the wall? (2)
explanation: {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence A is maximum at critical point OR {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex} {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence A is maximum at critical point OR {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex} {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
      Hence A is maximum at critical point

      OR

    2. {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex}
      {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}

question_html: Find the maximum slope of the curve {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex}.
question_text: Find the maximum slope of the curve {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex} Slope of the curve, {tex}m=\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x^{2}+6 x+9{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d m}{d x}=-6 x+6{/tex} For maximum/ minimum slope, put {tex}\frac{d m}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=1{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} m}{d x^{2}}=-6 \lt 0 \therefore m{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=1{/tex} Maximum slope {tex}=-3(1)^{2}+6(1)+9=12{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex} Slope of the curve, {tex}m=\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x^{2}+6 x+9{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d m}{d x}=-6 x+6{/tex} For maximum/ minimum slope, put {tex}\frac{d m}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=1{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} m}{d x^{2}}=-6 \lt 0 \therefore m{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=1{/tex} Maximum slope {tex}=-3(1)^{2}+6(1)+9=12{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex}
Slope of the curve, {tex}m=\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x^{2}+6 x+9{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d m}{d x}=-6 x+6{/tex}
For maximum/ minimum slope, put {tex}\frac{d m}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=1{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} m}{d x^{2}}=-6 \lt 0 \therefore m{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=1{/tex}
Maximum slope {tex}=-3(1)^{2}+6(1)+9=12{/tex}

question_html: A ladder 13 m long is leaning against the wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground away from the wall at the rate of {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex}. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 12 m away from the wall?
question_text: A ladder 13 m long is leaning against the wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground away from the wall at the rate of {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex} . How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 12 m away from the wall?
explanation: {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=169{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. {tex}t{/tex} {tex}2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12(2)+5\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=0[\because{/tex} when {tex}x=12 m, y=5 m]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{24}{5}{/tex} Hence, the height decreases at the rate of {tex}\frac{24}{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=169{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. {tex}t{/tex} {tex}2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12(2)+5\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=0[\because{/tex} when {tex}x=12 m, y=5 m]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{24}{5}{/tex} Hence, the height decreases at the rate of {tex}\frac{24}{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=169{/tex}
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. {tex}t{/tex}
{tex}2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12(2)+5\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=0[\because{/tex} when {tex}x=12 m, y=5 m]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{24}{5}{/tex}
Hence, the height decreases at the rate of {tex}\frac{24}{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Amongst all pairs of positive integers with product as 289, find which of the two numbers add up to the least.
question_text: Amongst all pairs of positive integers with product as 289, find which of the two numbers add up to the least.
explanation: Let numbers be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' such that {tex}x y=289 \Rightarrow y=\frac{289}{x}{/tex} , ' {tex}S{/tex} ' be their sum, then {tex}S=x+y=x+\frac{289}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=1-\frac{289}{x^{2}}, \frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=17, \text { a positive integer }{/tex} {tex}\left.\left.\frac{d^{2} S}{d x^{2}}\right]_{x=17}=289\left(\frac{2}{x^{3}}\right)\right]_{x=17} \gt 0, {/tex} {tex}\therefore S \text { is minimum when } x=17, y=17{/tex}
solution: Let numbers be ' {tex}x{/tex} ' and ' {tex}y{/tex} ' such that {tex}x y=289 \Rightarrow y=\frac{289}{x}{/tex} , ' {tex}S{/tex} ' be their sum, then {tex}S=x+y=x+\frac{289}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=1-\frac{289}{x^{2}}, \frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=17, \text { a positive integer }{/tex} {tex}\left.\left.\frac{d^{2} S}{d x^{2}}\right]_{x=17}=289\left(\frac{2}{x^{3}}\right)\right]_{x=17} \gt 0, {/tex} {tex}\therefore S \text { is minimum when } x=17, y=17{/tex}
solution_html:
Let numbers be '{tex}x{/tex}' and '{tex}y{/tex}' such that {tex}x y=289 \Rightarrow y=\frac{289}{x}{/tex}, '{tex}S{/tex}' be their sum, then
{tex}S=x+y=x+\frac{289}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=1-\frac{289}{x^{2}}, \frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=17, \text { a positive integer }{/tex}
{tex}\left.\left.\frac{d^{2} S}{d x^{2}}\right]_{x=17}=289\left(\frac{2}{x^{3}}\right)\right]_{x=17} \gt 0, {/tex}
{tex}\therefore S \text { is minimum when } x=17, y=17{/tex}

question_html:
A carpenter needs to make a wooden cuboidal box, closed from all sides, which has a square base and fixed volume. Since he is short of the paint required to paint the box on completion, he wants the surface area to be minimum.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. Taking length = breadth = x m and height = y m, express the surface area (S) of the box in terms of x and its volume (V), which is constant. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find a relation between x and y such that the surface area (S) is minimum. (2)

      OR

    2. If surface area (S) is constant, the volume (V) {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right){/tex}, x being the edge of base. Show that volume ( V ) is maximum for {tex}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{{S}}{6}}{/tex}. (2)
question_text: A carpenter needs to make a wooden cuboidal box, closed from all sides, which has a square base and fixed volume. Since he is short of the paint required to paint the box on completion, he wants the surface area to be minimum. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : Taking length = breadth = x m and height = y m, express the surface area (S) of the box in terms of x and its volume (V), which is constant. (1) Find {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}{/tex} . (1) Find a relation between x and y such that the surface area (S) is minimum. (2) OR If surface area (S) is constant, the volume (V) {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right){/tex} , x being the edge of base. Show that volume ( V ) is maximum for {tex}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{{S}}{6}}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I) Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex} . OR {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I) Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex} . OR {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}

1. {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I)
    Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex}
    1. {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex}.

      OR

    2. {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex}
      {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}
      {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} has neither maxima nor minima.
question_text: Show that the function {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} has neither maxima nor minima.
explanation: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 36x + 27 = 3\left( {4{x^2} - 12x + 9} \right) = 3{\left( {2x - 3} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} (Critical point) Since {tex}f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x < \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and for all {tex}x > \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a point of inflexion i.e., neither a point of maxima nor a point of minima. {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the only critical point, and f has neither maxima nor minima.
solution: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 36x + 27 = 3\left( {4{x^2} - 12x + 9} \right) = 3{\left( {2x - 3} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} (Critical point) Since {tex}f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x < \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and for all {tex}x > \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a point of inflexion i.e., neither a point of maxima nor a point of minima. {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the only critical point, and f has neither maxima nor minima.
solution_html:
{tex}f\left( x \right) = 4{x^3} - 18{x^2} + 27x - 7{/tex}

{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 12{x^2} - 36x + 27 = 3\left( {4{x^2} - 12x + 9} \right) = 3{\left( {2x - 3} \right)^2}{/tex}

{tex}f'\left( x \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}(Critical point)

Since {tex}f'\left( x \right) > 0{/tex} for all {tex}x < \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and for all {tex}x > \frac{3}{2}{/tex}

Hence {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a point of inflexion i.e., neither a point of maxima nor a point of minima.

{tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the only critical point, and f has neither maxima nor minima.

question_html: The function f(x) = {tex}x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} is:
question_text: The function f(x) = {tex}x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} is:

options.explanation: To determine the behavior of the function {tex}f(x)=x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} , we first compute its derivative: {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+12 \text {. }{/tex} Next, we analyze the derivative to find critical points and its sign: Finding the roots of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3\left(x^2-2 x+4\right){/tex} The discriminant of the quadratic {tex}x^2-2 x+4{/tex} is: {tex} D=(-2)^2-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 4=4-16=-12{/tex} Since the discriminant is negative, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} has no real roots, which means it does not change sign. Analyzing the sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : The quadratic x 2 - 2x + 4 opens upwards (as the coefficient of x 2 is positive) and is always positive because it has no real roots. Therefore, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} > 0 for all x. Since {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} , the function {tex}f(x){/tex} is strictly increasing on the entire real line. Thus, the correct answer is strictly increasing on R

options.text: neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on R
options.html: neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on R

Thus, the correct answer is strictly increasing on R

question_html: Find local maximum value and local minimum value (whichever exists) for the function f(x) = {tex}4 x^2+\frac{1}{x}(x \neq 0){/tex}
question_text: Find local maximum value and local minimum value (whichever exists) for the function f(x) = {tex}4 x^2+\frac{1}{x}(x \neq 0){/tex}
explanation: {tex} {f}({x})=4 x^2+\frac{1}{x}({x} \neq 0) {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}({x})=8 {x}-\frac{1}{x^2}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x^3=\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(x)=8+\frac{2}{x^3}>0 \text { at } {x}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Local minimum value }={f}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 {/tex}
solution: {tex} {f}({x})=4 x^2+\frac{1}{x}({x} \neq 0) {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}({x})=8 {x}-\frac{1}{x^2}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x^3=\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(x)=8+\frac{2}{x^3}>0 \text { at } {x}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Local minimum value }={f}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 {/tex}
