subject: Mathematics
question_html: If M and m denote the local maximum and local minimum values of the function f(x) = {tex}x+\frac{1}{x}(x \neq 0) {/tex} respectively, find the value of (M - m).
question_text: If M and m denote the local maximum and local minimum values of the function f(x) = {tex}x+\frac{1}{x}(x \neq 0) {/tex} respectively, find the value of (M - m).
explanation: {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^2}=\frac{x^2-1}{x^2}=\frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{x^2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=-1,1 {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2}{x^3} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(-1)=-2<0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore-1{/tex} is a point of local maximum {tex} \text { The local maximum value }=f(-1)=-2=M {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(1)=2>0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore 1{/tex} is point of local minimum The local minimum value {tex}=f(1)=2=m{/tex} {tex} M-m=-4{/tex}
solution: {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^2}=\frac{x^2-1}{x^2}=\frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{x^2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=-1,1 {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2}{x^3} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(-1)=-2<0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore-1{/tex} is a point of local maximum {tex} \text { The local maximum value }=f(-1)=-2=M {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime \prime}(1)=2>0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore 1{/tex} is point of local minimum The local minimum value {tex}=f(1)=2=m{/tex} {tex} M-m=-4{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^2}=\frac{x^2-1}{x^2}=\frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{x^2} {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=-1,1 {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2}{x^3} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(-1)=-2<0 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore-1{/tex} is a point of local maximum
{tex} \text { The local maximum value }=f(-1)=-2=M {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime \prime}(1)=2>0 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore 1{/tex} is point of local minimum
The local minimum value {tex}=f(1)=2=m{/tex}
{tex} M-m=-4{/tex}

question_text: Determine whether the function f(x) = x 2 - 6x + 3 is increasing or decreasing in [4, 6].
explanation: {tex} {f}({x})={x}^2-6 {x}+3, x \in[4,6]{/tex} We have {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-6{/tex} For all {tex}x{/tex} such that {tex}4<{x}<6,2<2 x-6<6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in(4,6){/tex} Hence, f is increasing over {tex}[4,6]{/tex} .
solution: {tex} {f}({x})={x}^2-6 {x}+3, x \in[4,6]{/tex} We have {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-6{/tex} For all {tex}x{/tex} such that {tex}4<{x}<6,2<2 x-6<6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in(4,6){/tex} Hence, f is increasing over {tex}[4,6]{/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex} {f}({x})={x}^2-6 {x}+3, x \in[4,6]{/tex}
We have {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-6{/tex}
For all {tex}x{/tex} such that {tex}4<{x}<6,2<2 x-6<6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in(4,6){/tex}
Hence, f is increasing over {tex}[4,6]{/tex}.

question_html: If {tex}x^{30} y^{20}=(x+y)^{50} \text {, prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} .{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x^{30} y^{20}=(x+y)^{50} \text {, prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} .{/tex}
explanation: Taking log of both sides, we get {tex} 30 \log x+20 \log y=50 \log (x+y){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac{30}{x}+\frac{20}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{50}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{20 x-30 y}{y(x+y)}\right)=\frac{20 x-30 y}{x(x+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} {/tex}
solution: Taking log of both sides, we get {tex} 30 \log x+20 \log y=50 \log (x+y){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac{30}{x}+\frac{20}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{50}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{20 x-30 y}{y(x+y)}\right)=\frac{20 x-30 y}{x(x+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} {/tex}
solution_html:
Taking log of both sides, we get
{tex} 30 \log x+20 \log y=50 \log (x+y){/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac{30}{x}+\frac{20}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{50}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{20 x-30 y}{y(x+y)}\right)=\frac{20 x-30 y}{x(x+y)} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} {/tex}

question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right] \text { at } x=1{/tex} is:
question_text: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right] \text { at } x=1{/tex} is:

options.explanation: To find {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right]{/tex} at {tex}x=1{/tex} , use the chain rule: {tex} \frac{d}{d x}=-\sin \left(\log x+e^x\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}+e^x\right) .{/tex} At {tex}x=1, \log 1=0{/tex} and {tex}e^1=e{/tex} , so the expression simplifies to {tex}-\sin e \cdot(1+e){/tex} . Thus, the answer is {tex}-(1+e) \sin e{/tex} .

explanation: To find {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right]{/tex} at {tex}x=1{/tex} , use the chain rule: {tex} \frac{d}{d x}=-\sin \left(\log x+e^x\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}+e^x\right) .{/tex} At {tex}x=1, \log 1=0{/tex} and {tex}e^1=e{/tex} , so the expression simplifies to {tex}-\sin e \cdot(1+e){/tex} . Thus, the answer is {tex}-(1+e) \sin e{/tex} .

question_html:
Overspeeding increases fuel consumption and decreases fuel economy as a result of tyre rolling friction and air resistance. While vehicles reach optimal fuel economy at different speeds, fuel mileage usually decreases rapidly at speeds above 80 km/h.

The relation between fuel consumption F (l/100 km) and speed V (km/h) under some constraints is given as F = {tex} \frac{{V}^2}{500}-\frac{{V}}{4}+14{/tex}.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find F, when V = 40 km/h. (1)
  2. Find {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex}. (1)
  3.  
    1. Find the speed V for which fuel consumption F is minimum. (2)
      OR
    2. Find the quantity of fuel required to travel 600 km at the speed V at which {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex} = -0.01. (2)
question_text: Overspeeding increases fuel consumption and decreases fuel economy as a result of tyre rolling friction and air resistance. While vehicles reach optimal fuel economy at different speeds, fuel mileage usually decreases rapidly at speeds above 80 km/h. The relation between fuel consumption F (l/100 km) and speed V (km/h) under some constraints is given as F = {tex} \frac{{V}^2}{500}-\frac{{V}}{4}+14{/tex} . On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Find F, when V = 40 km/h. (1) Find {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex} . (1) Find the speed V for which fuel consumption F is minimum. (2) OR Find the quantity of fuel required to travel 600 km at the speed V at which {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex} = -0.01. (2)
explanation: When {tex}{V}=40 {~km} / {h}, {F}=36 / 5 {l} / 100 {~km}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=\frac{V}{250}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {~V}=62.5 {~km} / {h} {/tex} {tex} \frac{d^2 F}{d V^2}=\frac{1}{250}>0 \text { at } {V}=62.5 {~km} / {h} {/tex} Hence, F is minimum when {tex}{V}=62.5 {~km} / {h}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=-0.01 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{V}{250}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{-1}{100} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V=60 {~km} / {h} {/tex} {tex} F=\frac{60^2}{500}-\frac{60}{4}+14=6.2 \ell / 100 {~km} {/tex} {tex} \text { Quantity of fuel required for } 600 {~km} {/tex} {tex} =6.2 \times 6=37.2 \ell {/tex}
solution: When {tex}{V}=40 {~km} / {h}, {F}=36 / 5 {l} / 100 {~km}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=\frac{V}{250}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {~V}=62.5 {~km} / {h} {/tex} {tex} \frac{d^2 F}{d V^2}=\frac{1}{250}>0 \text { at } {V}=62.5 {~km} / {h} {/tex} Hence, F is minimum when {tex}{V}=62.5 {~km} / {h}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d F}{d V}=-0.01 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{V}{250}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{-1}{100} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V=60 {~km} / {h} {/tex} {tex} F=\frac{60^2}{500}-\frac{60}{4}+14=6.2 \ell / 100 {~km} {/tex} {tex} \text { Quantity of fuel required for } 600 {~km} {/tex} {tex} =6.2 \times 6=37.2 \ell {/tex}

question_html: Show that {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x{/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}x{/tex} in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}.
question_text: Show that {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x{/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}x{/tex} in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\text {when} x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right], \cos x \geq 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}} \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\text { Thus, } f \text { is increasing on }\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right].{/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\text {when} x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right], \cos x \geq 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}} \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\text { Thus, } f \text { is increasing on }\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right].{/tex}
solution_html:

question_html: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of {tex}15 {~cm}^{3} /{/tex} minute. The falling sand forms a cone on the ground such that the height of the cone is always onethird of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing at the instant when the height is 4 cm?
question_text: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of {tex}15 {~cm}^{3} /{/tex} minute. The falling sand forms a cone on the ground such that the height of the cone is always onethird of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing at the instant when the height is 4 cm?
explanation: Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' be the volume, ' {tex}h{/tex} ' and ' {tex}r{/tex} ' be the height and radius of the cone, {tex}\therefore h=\frac{r}{3}, \frac{d V}{d t}=15 {~cm}^{3} / {min}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=3 \pi h^{3}{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 h\right)=15 \Rightarrow 3 \pi \frac{d\left(h^3\right)}{d t}=15 \Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}\right]_{h=4}{/tex} {tex}\left.=\frac{5}{3 \pi {~h}^2}\right]_{{hm}=4}=\frac{5}{48 \pi} {~cm} / {min}{/tex}
solution: Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' be the volume, ' {tex}h{/tex} ' and ' {tex}r{/tex} ' be the height and radius of the cone, {tex}\therefore h=\frac{r}{3}, \frac{d V}{d t}=15 {~cm}^{3} / {min}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=3 \pi h^{3}{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 h\right)=15 \Rightarrow 3 \pi \frac{d\left(h^3\right)}{d t}=15 \Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}\right]_{h=4}{/tex} {tex}\left.=\frac{5}{3 \pi {~h}^2}\right]_{{hm}=4}=\frac{5}{48 \pi} {~cm} / {min}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' be the volume, '{tex}h{/tex}' and '{tex}r{/tex}' be the height and radius of the cone,
{tex}\therefore h=\frac{r}{3}, \frac{d V}{d t}=15 {~cm}^{3} / {min}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=3 \pi h^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 h\right)=15 \Rightarrow 3 \pi \frac{d\left(h^3\right)}{d t}=15 \Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}\right]_{h=4}{/tex}{tex}\left.=\frac{5}{3 \pi {~h}^2}\right]_{{hm}=4}=\frac{5}{48 \pi} {~cm} / {min}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The perimeter of a rectangular metallic sheet is 300 cm. It is rolled along one of its sides to form a cylinder. Find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet so that volume of cylinder so formed is maximum.
question_text: The perimeter of a rectangular metallic sheet is 300 cm. It is rolled along one of its sides to form a cylinder. Find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet so that volume of cylinder so formed is maximum.
explanation: To find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet that maximize the volume of the cylinder formed, we start with the given perimeter of the rectangle. The perimeter P of a rectangle is given by the formula: {tex} P=2(l+w) {/tex} where {tex}l{/tex} is the length and {tex}w{/tex} is the width. Given that the perimeter is 300 cm, we have: {tex} 2(l+w)=300 {/tex} which simplifies to: {tex} l+w=150 {/tex} When the rectangle is rolled along one of its sides, we can assume it is rolled along the width w, making the circumference of the cylinder equal to w. The formula for the circumference C of a cylinder is: {tex} C=2 \pi r {/tex} where {tex}r{/tex} is the radius. Thus, we have: {tex} w=2 \pi r {/tex} The height h of the cylinder will be equal to the length I of the rectangle. The volume V of the cylinder is given by: {tex} V=\pi r^2 h=\pi r^2 l {/tex} Substituting I from the perimeter equation, we have: l = 150 - w and substituting {tex}w{/tex} in terms of {tex}r{/tex} gives us: {tex} l=150-2 \pi r {/tex} Therefore, the volume can be expressed as: {tex} V=\pi r^2(150-2 \pi r)=150 \pi r^2-2 \pi^2 r^3 {/tex} To maximize the volume, we take the derivative of V with respect to r and set it to zero: {tex} \frac{d V}{d r}=300 \pi r-6 \pi^2 r^2=0 {/tex} Factoring out common terms gives us: {tex} 3 \pi r(100-2 \pi r)=0 {/tex} leading to two solutions: {tex}{r}=0{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{100}{2 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}{/tex} . We discard {tex}{r}=0{/tex} as it does not make sense in this context.| Now, substituting r back to find w and I: {tex}w=2 \pi r=2 \pi \cdot \frac{50}{\pi}=100{/tex} cm. {tex}l=150-w=150-100=50{/tex} cm. Thus, the dimensions of the rectangular sheet are: Length {tex}=50 {~cm}{/tex} and Width {tex}=100 {~cm}{/tex} .
solution: To find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet that maximize the volume of the cylinder formed, we start with the given perimeter of the rectangle. The perimeter P of a rectangle is given by the formula: {tex} P=2(l+w) {/tex} where {tex}l{/tex} is the length and {tex}w{/tex} is the width. Given that the perimeter is 300 cm, we have: {tex} 2(l+w)=300 {/tex} which simplifies to: {tex} l+w=150 {/tex} When the rectangle is rolled along one of its sides, we can assume it is rolled along the width w, making the circumference of the cylinder equal to w. The formula for the circumference C of a cylinder is: {tex} C=2 \pi r {/tex} where {tex}r{/tex} is the radius. Thus, we have: {tex} w=2 \pi r {/tex} The height h of the cylinder will be equal to the length I of the rectangle. The volume V of the cylinder is given by: {tex} V=\pi r^2 h=\pi r^2 l {/tex} Substituting I from the perimeter equation, we have: l = 150 - w and substituting {tex}w{/tex} in terms of {tex}r{/tex} gives us: {tex} l=150-2 \pi r {/tex} Therefore, the volume can be expressed as: {tex} V=\pi r^2(150-2 \pi r)=150 \pi r^2-2 \pi^2 r^3 {/tex} To maximize the volume, we take the derivative of V with respect to r and set it to zero: {tex} \frac{d V}{d r}=300 \pi r-6 \pi^2 r^2=0 {/tex} Factoring out common terms gives us: {tex} 3 \pi r(100-2 \pi r)=0 {/tex} leading to two solutions: {tex}{r}=0{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{100}{2 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}{/tex} . We discard {tex}{r}=0{/tex} as it does not make sense in this context.| Now, substituting r back to find w and I: {tex}w=2 \pi r=2 \pi \cdot \frac{50}{\pi}=100{/tex} cm. {tex}l=150-w=150-100=50{/tex} cm. Thus, the dimensions of the rectangular sheet are: Length {tex}=50 {~cm}{/tex} and Width {tex}=100 {~cm}{/tex} .
solution_html:
To find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet that maximize the volume of the cylinder formed, we start with the given perimeter of the rectangle. The perimeter P of a rectangle is given by the formula:
{tex} P=2(l+w) {/tex}
where {tex}l{/tex} is the length and {tex}w{/tex} is the width. Given that the perimeter is 300 cm, we have:
{tex} 2(l+w)=300 {/tex}
which simplifies to:
{tex} l+w=150 {/tex}
When the rectangle is rolled along one of its sides, we can assume it is rolled along the width w, making the circumference of the cylinder equal to w. The formula for the circumference C of a cylinder is:
{tex} C=2 \pi r {/tex}
where {tex}r{/tex} is the radius. Thus, we have:
{tex} w=2 \pi r {/tex}
The height h of the cylinder will be equal to the length I of the rectangle. The volume V of the cylinder is given by:
{tex} V=\pi r^2 h=\pi r^2 l {/tex}
Substituting I from the perimeter equation, we have:
l = 150 - w
and substituting {tex}w{/tex} in terms of {tex}r{/tex} gives us:
{tex} l=150-2 \pi r {/tex}
Therefore, the volume can be expressed as:
{tex} V=\pi r^2(150-2 \pi r)=150 \pi r^2-2 \pi^2 r^3 {/tex}
To maximize the volume, we take the derivative of V with respect to r and set it to zero:
{tex} \frac{d V}{d r}=300 \pi r-6 \pi^2 r^2=0 {/tex}
Factoring out common terms gives us:
{tex} 3 \pi r(100-2 \pi r)=0 {/tex}
leading to two solutions: {tex}{r}=0{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{100}{2 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}{/tex}. We discard {tex}{r}=0{/tex} as it does not make sense in this context.|
Now, substituting r back to find w and I:

  1. {tex}w=2 \pi r=2 \pi \cdot \frac{50}{\pi}=100{/tex} cm.
  2. {tex}l=150-w=150-100=50{/tex} cm.

Thus, the dimensions of the rectangular sheet are: Length {tex}=50 {~cm}{/tex} and Width {tex}=100 {~cm}{/tex}.

question_html: It is given that function {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex}. Find the value of '{tex}a{/tex}', hence obtain all other points where the given function {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} attains local maximum or local minimum values.
question_text: It is given that function {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex} . Find the value of ' {tex}a{/tex} ', hence obtain all other points where the given function {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} attains local maximum or local minimum values.
explanation: {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+a{/tex} as at {tex}x=1, f{/tex} attains local maximum value, {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=0 \Rightarrow a=120{/tex} now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+120={/tex} {tex}4(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x-30\right)=4(x-1)(x-5)(x+6){/tex} Critical points are {tex}x=-6,1,5{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-124{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(-6) \gt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(1) \lt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(5) \gt 0{/tex} so {tex}f{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex} and local minimum value at {tex}x=-6,5{/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+a{/tex} as at {tex}x=1, f{/tex} attains local maximum value, {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=0 \Rightarrow a=120{/tex} now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+120={/tex} {tex}4(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x-30\right)=4(x-1)(x-5)(x+6){/tex} Critical points are {tex}x=-6,1,5{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-124{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(-6) \gt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(1) \lt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(5) \gt 0{/tex} so {tex}f{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex} and local minimum value at {tex}x=-6,5{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+a{/tex}
as at {tex}x=1, f{/tex} attains local maximum value, {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=0 \Rightarrow a=120{/tex}
now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+120={/tex} {tex}4(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x-30\right)=4(x-1)(x-5)(x+6){/tex}
Critical points are {tex}x=-6,1,5{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-124{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(-6) \gt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(1) \lt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(5) \gt 0{/tex}
so {tex}f{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex} and local minimum value at {tex}x=-6,5{/tex}

question_html:
A rectangular visiting card is to contain 24 sq.cm. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be {tex}1 \frac{1}{2} {~cm}{/tex} as shown below :


On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of {tex}x{/tex}. (2)
  2. Obtain the dimensions of the card of minimum area. (2)
question_text: A rectangular visiting card is to contain 24 sq.cm. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be {tex}1 \frac{1}{2} {~cm}{/tex} as shown below : On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of {tex}x{/tex} . (2) Obtain the dimensions of the card of minimum area. (2)
explanation: Let {tex}{A}({x}){/tex} be the area of the visiting card then, {tex}\text { As } x y=24, A(x)=(x+3)(y+2)=2 x+3 y+x y+6=2 x+\frac{72}{x}+30{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime}({x})=2-\frac{72}{{x}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}({x})=\frac{144}{{x}^{3}}{/tex} , solving {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=6{/tex} is the critical point. {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(6)=\frac{144}{6} \gt 0,{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the card is minimum at } x=6, y=4{/tex} The dimension of the card with minimum area is Length {tex}=9 {~cm}{/tex} , Breadth {tex}=6 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{A}({x}){/tex} be the area of the visiting card then, {tex}\text { As } x y=24, A(x)=(x+3)(y+2)=2 x+3 y+x y+6=2 x+\frac{72}{x}+30{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime}({x})=2-\frac{72}{{x}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}({x})=\frac{144}{{x}^{3}}{/tex} , solving {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=6{/tex} is the critical point. {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(6)=\frac{144}{6} \gt 0,{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the card is minimum at } x=6, y=4{/tex} The dimension of the card with minimum area is Length {tex}=9 {~cm}{/tex} , Breadth {tex}=6 {~cm}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let {tex}{A}({x}){/tex} be the area of the visiting card then,
    {tex}\text { As } x y=24, A(x)=(x+3)(y+2)=2 x+3 y+x y+6=2 x+\frac{72}{x}+30{/tex}
  2. {tex}{A}^{\prime}({x})=2-\frac{72}{{x}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}({x})=\frac{144}{{x}^{3}}{/tex},
    solving {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=6{/tex} is the critical point. {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(6)=\frac{144}{6} \gt 0,{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the card is minimum at } x=6, y=4{/tex}
    The dimension of the card with minimum area is Length {tex}=9 {~cm}{/tex}, Breadth {tex}=6 {~cm}{/tex}

question_html: Find the absolute maximum and absolute minimum values of the function f given by {tex}{f}({x})=\frac{{x}}{2}+\frac{2}{{x}}{/tex}, on the interval {tex}[1,2]{/tex}.
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and absolute minimum values of the function f given by {tex}{f}({x})=\frac{{x}}{2}+\frac{2}{{x}}{/tex} , on the interval {tex}[1,2]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} ; x \in[1,2]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{I}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} for absolute maximum/minimum, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=4 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex} Now, {tex}f(1)=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}f(2)=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} absolute maximum value {tex}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and absolute minimum value {tex}=2{/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} ; x \in[1,2]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{I}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} for absolute maximum/minimum, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=4 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex} Now, {tex}f(1)=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}f(2)=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} absolute maximum value {tex}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and absolute minimum value {tex}=2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} ; x \in[1,2]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{I}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex}
for absolute maximum/minimum, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=4 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex}
Now, {tex}f(1)=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}f(2)=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} absolute maximum value {tex}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and absolute minimum value {tex}=2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
explanation: Given function {tex}\log {x} / {x}{/tex} {tex} f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x(1 / x)-\log x(1)}{x^2}=\frac{1-\log x}{x^2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}>0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x>0 \Rightarrow \log x<1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x<e \text { and } x>0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is increasing in ( {tex}0, e{/tex} ) {tex} f^{\prime}(x)<0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}<0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x<0 \Rightarrow \log x>1 \Rightarrow x>e{/tex} {tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is decreasing in ( {tex}e, \infty{/tex} ).
solution: Given function {tex}\log {x} / {x}{/tex} {tex} f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x(1 / x)-\log x(1)}{x^2}=\frac{1-\log x}{x^2} {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}>0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x>0 \Rightarrow \log x<1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x<e \text { and } x>0 {/tex} {tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is increasing in ( {tex}0, e{/tex} ) {tex} f^{\prime}(x)<0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}<0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x<0 \Rightarrow \log x>1 \Rightarrow x>e{/tex} {tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is decreasing in ( {tex}e, \infty{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Given function {tex}\log {x} / {x}{/tex}
{tex} f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x(1 / x)-\log x(1)}{x^2}=\frac{1-\log x}{x^2} {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}>0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x>0 \Rightarrow \log x<1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x<e \text { and } x>0 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is increasing in ({tex}0, e{/tex})
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)<0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}<0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x<0 \Rightarrow \log x>1 \Rightarrow x>e{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is decreasing in ({tex}e, \infty{/tex}).

question_text: The area of the circle is increasing at a uniform rate of {tex}2 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex} . How fast is the circumference of the circle increasing when the radius {tex}r=5 {~cm}{/tex} ?
explanation: Let {tex}{C}={2} \boldsymbol{\pi} {r}{/tex} , be the circumference of the circle, {tex}\frac{{d}\left(\pi {r}^{2}\right)}{{dt}}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{1}{\pi {r}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dt}}=2 \pi \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{2}{{r}}=\frac{2}{5} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} at {tex}{r}=5 {~cm}{/tex}

solution_html:
Let {tex}{C}={2} \boldsymbol{\pi} {r}{/tex}, be the circumference of the circle, {tex}\frac{{d}\left(\pi {r}^{2}\right)}{{dt}}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{1}{\pi {r}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dt}}=2 \pi \frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{2}{{r}}=\frac{2}{5} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} at {tex}{r}=5 {~cm}{/tex}

question_html: Show that the function f given by {tex}{f}(x)=\sin x+\cos x{/tex}, is strictly decreasing in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Show that the function f given by {tex}{f}(x)=\sin x+\cos x{/tex} , is strictly decreasing in the interval {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=\sin x+\cos x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\sin x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4} {/tex} {tex}\text { Thus,in the interval }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right) f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore f \text { is strictly decreasing function on }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=\sin x+\cos x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\sin x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4} {/tex} {tex}\text { Thus,in the interval }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right) f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore f \text { is strictly decreasing function on }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=\sin x+\cos x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\sin x{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4} {/tex} {tex}\text { Thus,in the interval }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right) f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f \text { is strictly decreasing function on }\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right){/tex}

question_html: The surface area of a cube increases at the rate of {tex}72 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex}. Find the rate of change of its volume, when the edge of the cube measures 3 cm.
question_text: The surface area of a cube increases at the rate of {tex}72 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex} . Find the rate of change of its volume, when the edge of the cube measures 3 cm.
explanation: Let edge of cube be 'x cm' {tex}S=6 x^{2}, \frac{d S}{d t}=72 {~cm}^{2} / {sec} \Rightarrow 12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=72 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{6}{x}{/tex} Volume, {tex}V=x^3 \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^2 \frac{d x}{d t}={/tex} {tex}\left.3 \times {x}^2 \times \frac{6}{{x}}=18 {x}, \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right]_{{x}=3}=54 {~cm}^3 / {sec}{/tex} ∴ Volume is increasing at the rate of {tex}{5 4} {~ c m}^{{3}} / {s e c}{/tex}
solution: Let edge of cube be 'x cm' {tex}S=6 x^{2}, \frac{d S}{d t}=72 {~cm}^{2} / {sec} \Rightarrow 12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=72 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{6}{x}{/tex} Volume, {tex}V=x^3 \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^2 \frac{d x}{d t}={/tex} {tex}\left.3 \times {x}^2 \times \frac{6}{{x}}=18 {x}, \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right]_{{x}=3}=54 {~cm}^3 / {sec}{/tex} ∴ Volume is increasing at the rate of {tex}{5 4} {~ c m}^{{3}} / {s e c}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let edge of cube be 'x cm'
{tex}S=6 x^{2}, \frac{d S}{d t}=72 {~cm}^{2} / {sec} \Rightarrow 12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=72 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{6}{x}{/tex}
Volume, {tex}V=x^3 \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^2 \frac{d x}{d t}={/tex} {tex}\left.3 \times {x}^2 \times \frac{6}{{x}}=18 {x}, \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\right]_{{x}=3}=54 {~cm}^3 / {sec}{/tex}
∴ Volume is increasing at the rate of {tex}{5 4} {~ c m}^{{3}} / {s e c}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 6 cm3/s. How fast is the surface area of cube increasing, when the length of an edge is 8 cm?
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 6 cm 3 /s. How fast is the surface area of cube increasing, when the length of an edge is 8 cm?
explanation: Given, {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6\ {cm}^{3} / {sec}{/tex} . Since, {tex}V=x^{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow 6=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{2}{x^{2}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} Now, Surface Area {tex}={S}=6 x^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=3 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6\ {cm}^{3} / {sec}{/tex} . Since, {tex}V=x^{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow 6=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{2}{x^{2}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} Now, Surface Area {tex}={S}=6 x^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=3 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6\ {cm}^{3} / {sec}{/tex}. Since, {tex}V=x^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow 6=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{2}{x^{2}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex}
Now, Surface Area {tex}={S}=6 x^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=3 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A store has been selling calculators at ₹ 350 each. A market survey indicates that a reduction in price (p) of calculator increases the number of units ({tex}x{/tex}) sold. The relation between the price and quantity sold is given by the demand function {tex}{p}=450-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex}.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Determine the number of units ({tex}x{/tex}) that should be sold to maximise the revenue {tex}{R}(x)=x {p}(x){/tex}. Also, verify the result. (2)
  2. What rebate in price of calculator should the store give to maximise the revenue? (2)
question_text: A store has been selling calculators at ₹ 350 each. A market survey indicates that a reduction in price (p) of calculator increases the number of units ( {tex}x{/tex} ) sold. The relation between the price and quantity sold is given by the demand function {tex}{p}=450-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex} . Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Determine the number of units ( {tex}x{/tex} ) that should be sold to maximise the revenue {tex}{R}(x)=x {p}(x){/tex} . Also, verify the result. (2) What rebate in price of calculator should the store give to maximise the revenue? (2)
explanation: Revenue by selling {tex}x{/tex} items {tex}=R(x)=x \cdot p(x)=450 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=450-x{/tex} For Maxima or Minima, {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=450{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d x^{2}}=-1 \lt 0{/tex} (Revenue is maximum when {tex}x=450{/tex} units are sold) At {tex}x=450, p=450-\frac{450}{2}=225{/tex} So, Rebate {tex}=350-225={/tex} Rs. 125 per calculator
solution: Revenue by selling {tex}x{/tex} items {tex}=R(x)=x \cdot p(x)=450 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=450-x{/tex} For Maxima or Minima, {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=450{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d x^{2}}=-1 \lt 0{/tex} (Revenue is maximum when {tex}x=450{/tex} units are sold) At {tex}x=450, p=450-\frac{450}{2}=225{/tex} So, Rebate {tex}=350-225={/tex} Rs. 125 per calculator
solution_html:
1. Revenue by selling {tex}x{/tex} items {tex}=R(x)=x \cdot p(x)=450 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=450-x{/tex}
    For Maxima or Minima, {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=450{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d x^{2}}=-1 \lt 0{/tex}
    (Revenue is maximum when {tex}x=450{/tex} units are sold)
  2. At {tex}x=450, p=450-\frac{450}{2}=225{/tex}
    So, Rebate {tex}=350-225={/tex} Rs. 125 per calculator

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A sandbag is dropped from a balloon at a height of 60 metres.

When the angle of elevation of the sun is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}, the position of the sandbag is given by the equation {tex}y=60-4.9 t^{2}{/tex}, where {tex}y{/tex} is the height of the sandbag above the ground and t is the time in seconds.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the relation between x and y, where x is the distance of the shadow at P from the point Q and y is the height of the sandbag above the ground. (1)
  2. After how much time will the sandbag be 35 metres above the ground? (1)
    1. Find the rate at which the shadow of the sandbag is travelling along the ground when the sandbag is at a height of 35 metres. (2)

      OR

    2. How fast is the height of the sandbag decreasing when 2 seconds have elapsed? (2)
question_text: A sandbag is dropped from a balloon at a height of 60 metres. When the angle of elevation of the sun is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} , the position of the sandbag is given by the equation {tex}y=60-4.9 t^{2}{/tex} , where {tex}y{/tex} is the height of the sandbag above the ground and t is the time in seconds. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Find the relation between x and y, where x is the distance of the shadow at P from the point Q and y is the height of the sandbag above the ground. (1) After how much time will the sandbag be 35 metres above the ground? (1) Find the rate at which the shadow of the sandbag is travelling along the ground when the sandbag is at a height of 35 metres. (2) OR How fast is the height of the sandbag decreasing when 2 seconds have elapsed? (2)
explanation: {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} or {tex}x=\sqrt{3} y{/tex} Using {tex}y=35 m \Rightarrow 60-4.9 t^2=35 \Rightarrow 4.9 t^2=25 \Rightarrow t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}{/tex} seconds. {tex}\left.x=\sqrt{3} y \Rightarrow x=60 \sqrt{3}-4.9 \sqrt{3} t^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}\right]_{t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}}{/tex} {tex}{\left.=-4.9\sqrt3(2t)\right]}_{t=\frac{5\sqrt{10}}7}=-7\sqrt{30}\;m/s{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=-9.8 {t}=-9.8 \times 2=-19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex} Height of the sandbag is decreasing at the rate of {tex}19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} or {tex}x=\sqrt{3} y{/tex} Using {tex}y=35 m \Rightarrow 60-4.9 t^2=35 \Rightarrow 4.9 t^2=25 \Rightarrow t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}{/tex} seconds. {tex}\left.x=\sqrt{3} y \Rightarrow x=60 \sqrt{3}-4.9 \sqrt{3} t^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}\right]_{t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}}{/tex} {tex}{\left.=-4.9\sqrt3(2t)\right]}_{t=\frac{5\sqrt{10}}7}=-7\sqrt{30}\;m/s{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=-9.8 {t}=-9.8 \times 2=-19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex} Height of the sandbag is decreasing at the rate of {tex}19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} or {tex}x=\sqrt{3} y{/tex}
  2. Using {tex}y=35 m \Rightarrow 60-4.9 t^2=35 \Rightarrow 4.9 t^2=25 \Rightarrow t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}{/tex} seconds.
    1. {tex}\left.x=\sqrt{3} y \Rightarrow x=60 \sqrt{3}-4.9 \sqrt{3} t^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}\right]_{t=\frac{5 \sqrt{10}}{7}}{/tex}{tex}{\left.=-4.9\sqrt3(2t)\right]}_{t=\frac{5\sqrt{10}}7}=-7\sqrt{30}\;m/s{/tex}

      Or

    2. {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=-9.8 {t}=-9.8 \times 2=-19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
      Height of the sandbag is decreasing at the rate of {tex}19.6 {~m} / {s}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the interval in which the function {tex}{f}(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the interval in which the function {tex}{f}(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing.
explanation: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-12 x^{2}=4 x^{2}(x-3){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x^{2}(x-3) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex} Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}(-\infty, 0) \cup(0,3){/tex} . OR Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left.(-\infty, 0] \cup[0,3]\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-12 x^{2}=4 x^{2}(x-3){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x^{2}(x-3) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex} Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}(-\infty, 0) \cup(0,3){/tex} . OR Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left.(-\infty, 0] \cup[0,3]\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-12 x^{2}=4 x^{2}(x-3){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 x^{2}(x-3) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0 \text { for } x \lt 3, x \neq 0{/tex}
Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}(-\infty, 0) \cup(0,3){/tex}.
OR
Thus, {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-4 x^{3}+10{/tex} is strictly decreasing on {tex}\left.(-\infty, 0] \cup[0,3]\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given that {tex}f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex}, find the point of local maximum of {tex}f(x){/tex}.
question_text: Given that {tex}f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} , find the point of local maximum of {tex}f(x){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})=\frac{1-\log {x}}{{x}^{2}},{/tex} {tex} \therefore {f}^{\prime}({x})=0 \Rightarrow \log {x}=1 \Rightarrow {x}={e}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2 x \log x-3 x}{x^{4}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(e)=-\frac{1}{e^{3}} \lt 0{/tex} i.e. {tex}x=e{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution: {tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})=\frac{1-\log {x}}{{x}^{2}},{/tex} {tex} \therefore {f}^{\prime}({x})=0 \Rightarrow \log {x}=1 \Rightarrow {x}={e}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2 x \log x-3 x}{x^{4}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(e)=-\frac{1}{e^{3}} \lt 0{/tex} i.e. {tex}x=e{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution_html:
{tex}{f}^{\prime}({x})=\frac{1-\log {x}}{{x}^{2}},{/tex}
{tex} \therefore {f}^{\prime}({x})=0 \Rightarrow \log {x}=1 \Rightarrow {x}={e}{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{2 x \log x-3 x}{x^{4}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(e)=-\frac{1}{e^{3}} \lt 0{/tex} i.e. {tex}x=e{/tex} is a point of local maximum.

question_html: Find the point on the curve y2 = 8x for which the abscissa and ordinate change at the same rate.
question_text: Find the point on the curve y 2 = 8x for which the abscissa and ordinate change at the same rate.
explanation: The given curve is, y 2 = 8x ...(1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=8 \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=8\left[\because \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{y^{2}}{8}{/tex} [From (1)] {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{10}{8}=2{/tex} So, the point is (2, 4)
solution: The given curve is, y 2 = 8x ...(1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=8 \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=8\left[\because \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{y^{2}}{8}{/tex} [From (1)] {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{10}{8}=2{/tex} So, the point is (2, 4)
solution_html:
The given curve is,
y2 = 8x ...(1)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=8 \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=8\left[\because \frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{y^{2}}{8}{/tex}[From (1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{10}{8}=2{/tex}
So, the point is (2, 4)

question_html:
A tank, as shown in the figure below, formed using a combination of a cylinder and a cone, offers better drainage as compared to a flat bottomed tank.

A tap is connected to such a tank whose conical part is full of water. Water is dripping out from a tap at the bottom at the uniform rate of 2 cm3/s. The semi-vertical angle of the conical tank is 45o.


Find the rate at which the wet surface of the conical tank is decreasing at an instant when radius r = {tex}2\sqrt 2{/tex} cm.
question_text: A tank, as shown in the figure below, formed using a combination of a cylinder and a cone, offers better drainage as compared to a flat bottomed tank. A tap is connected to such a tank whose conical part is full of water. Water is dripping out from a tap at the bottom at the uniform rate of 2 cm 3 /s. The semi-vertical angle of the conical tank is 45 o . Find the rate at which the wet surface of the conical tank is decreasing at an instant when radius r = {tex}2\sqrt 2{/tex} cm.

solution: C = {tex}\pi{/tex} rl = {tex}\pi r{/tex} {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} r = {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} 2r {tex}\frac{\text {dr}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dt}}\right)_{r=2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = -2 cm 2 /sec

{tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} 2r {tex}\frac{\text {dr}}{\text {dt}}{/tex}

question_html: Show that the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{16 \sin \text x}{4\;+\;\cos \text x}-\text x{/tex}, is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{16 \sin \text x}{4\;+\;\cos \text x}-\text x{/tex} , is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} .
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\frac{16 \sin \text x}{4\;+\;\cos \text x}-\text x{/tex} , f'(x) = {tex}\frac{16[4+\cos \text x] \cos \text x+16 \sin ^2 \text x}{(4+\cos \text x)^2}-1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\cos \text x(56-\cos \text x)}{(4+\cos \text x)^2}{/tex} in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} ,cos x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) in strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}
solution: f(x) = {tex}\frac{16 \sin \text x}{4\;+\;\cos \text x}-\text x{/tex} , f'(x) = {tex}\frac{16[4+\cos \text x] \cos \text x+16 \sin ^2 \text x}{(4+\cos \text x)^2}-1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\cos \text x(56-\cos \text x)}{(4+\cos \text x)^2}{/tex} in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex} ,cos x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) in strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}

f(x) = {tex}\frac{16 \sin \text x}{4\;+\;\cos \text x}-\text x{/tex}, 
f'(x) = {tex}\frac{16[4+\cos \text x] \cos \text x+16 \sin ^2 \text x}{(4+\cos \text x)^2}-1{/tex}

in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex},cos x < 0

question_html: Find the sub-intervals in which f(x) = log (2 + x) - {tex}\frac{\text x}{2+\text x}{/tex}, x > -2 is increasing or decreasing.
question_text: Find the sub-intervals in which f(x) = log (2 + x) - {tex}\frac{\text x}{2+\text x}{/tex} , x > -2 is increasing or decreasing.
explanation: f(x) = log (2 + x) - {tex}\frac{\text x}{2+\text x}{/tex} , x > -2 f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{2+\text x}-\frac{2}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2+x)-2}{(2+x)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text x}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex} Sign of f'(x) f(x) is decreasing in (– 2, 0) and increasing in (0, {tex}\infty{/tex} )
solution: f(x) = log (2 + x) - {tex}\frac{\text x}{2+\text x}{/tex} , x > -2 f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{2+\text x}-\frac{2}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2+x)-2}{(2+x)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text x}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex} Sign of f'(x) f(x) is decreasing in (– 2, 0) and increasing in (0, {tex}\infty{/tex} )
solution_html:
f(x) = log (2 + x) - {tex}\frac{\text x}{2+\text x}{/tex}, x > -2
f'(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{2+\text x}-\frac{2}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(2+x)-2}{(2+x)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\text x}{(2+\text x)^2}{/tex}
Sign of f'(x)

f(x) is decreasing in (– 2, 0)
and increasing in (0, {tex}\infty{/tex})

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If equal sides of an isosceles triangle with fixed base 10 cm are increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec, how fast is the area of triangle increasing at an instant when all sides become equal?
question_text: If equal sides of an isosceles triangle with fixed base 10 cm are increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec, how fast is the area of triangle increasing at an instant when all sides become equal?
explanation: Given, Side is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4 cm/s Area of isosceles triangle = {tex}\frac{b}{2}\sqrt{a^2-\frac{b^2}{4}}{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{10}{2}\sqrt{x^2-(\frac{10}{2})^2}{/tex} A = {tex}5 \sqrt{\text x^2-25}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \text x}{\sqrt{\text x^2-25}} \frac{\text {dx}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}\right]_{\text x=10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm 2 /s = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution: Given, Side is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4 cm/s Area of isosceles triangle = {tex}\frac{b}{2}\sqrt{a^2-\frac{b^2}{4}}{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{10}{2}\sqrt{x^2-(\frac{10}{2})^2}{/tex} A = {tex}5 \sqrt{\text x^2-25}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \text x}{\sqrt{\text x^2-25}} \frac{\text {dx}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}\right]_{\text x=10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm 2 /s = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution_html:
Given, Side is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec

then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4 cm/s
Area of isosceles triangle = {tex}\frac{b}{2}\sqrt{a^2-\frac{b^2}{4}}{/tex}

A = {tex}\frac{10}{2}\sqrt{x^2-(\frac{10}{2})^2}{/tex}
A = {tex}5 \sqrt{\text x^2-25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \text x}{\sqrt{\text x^2-25}} \frac{\text {dx}}{\text {dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\frac{\text {dA}}{\text {dt}}\right]_{\text x=10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm2/s
= {tex}\frac{40}{3}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} cm2/s

question_html: Find the interval in which the function f(x) = 2x3 - 3x is strictly increasing.
question_text: Find the interval in which the function f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x is strictly increasing.
explanation: Here, f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x f'(x) = 6x 2 - 3 = 3(2x 2 - 1) Now, f'(x) = 0 2x 2 - 1=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\begin{equation} \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{equation}{/tex} f(x) is strictly increasing in {tex}\begin{equation} \left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cup\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \infty\right) \end{equation}{/tex}
solution: Here, f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x f'(x) = 6x 2 - 3 = 3(2x 2 - 1) Now, f'(x) = 0 2x 2 - 1=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\begin{equation} \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{equation}{/tex} f(x) is strictly increasing in {tex}\begin{equation} \left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cup\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \infty\right) \end{equation}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, f(x) = 2x3 - 3x
f'(x) = 6x2 - 3
= 3(2x2 - 1)
Now, f'(x) = 0
2x2 - 1=0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\begin{equation} \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{equation}{/tex}
f(x) is strictly increasing in {tex}\begin{equation} \left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cup\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \infty\right) \end{equation}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The median of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} cm/s. Find the rate at which its side is increasing.
question_text: The median of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} cm/s. Find the rate at which its side is increasing.
explanation: In an equilateral triangle, median is same as altitude. Let ‘h’ denote the length of the median (or altitude) and ‘x’ be the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex} . Then, h = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text x{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{2 \text h}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} ...(i) It is given that {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\sqrt 3{/tex} So, by (i) we have {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{\text {dh}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4 Thus, the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex} is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec.
solution: In an equilateral triangle, median is same as altitude. Let ‘h’ denote the length of the median (or altitude) and ‘x’ be the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex} . Then, h = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text x{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{2 \text h}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} ...(i) It is given that {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\sqrt 3{/tex} So, by (i) we have {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{\text {dh}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4 Thus, the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex} is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec.
solution_html:
In an equilateral triangle, median is same as altitude. Let ‘h’ denote the length of the median (or altitude) and ‘x’ be the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex}.

Then, h = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text x{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{2 \text h}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} ...(i)
It is given that {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\sqrt 3{/tex} So, by (i) we have
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{\text {dh}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = 4
Thus, the side of {tex}\triangle {\text {ABC}}{/tex} is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/sec.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Sum of two numbers is 5. If the sum of the cubes of these numbers is least, then find the sum of the squares of these numbers.
question_text: Sum of two numbers is 5. If the sum of the cubes of these numbers is least, then find the sum of the squares of these numbers.
explanation: Let the two numbers be x and y. Then, x + y = 5 or y = 5 - x Let S denote the sum of the cubes of these numbers. Then S = x 3 + y 3 = x 3 + (5 - x) 3 {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 3x 2 - 3(5 - x) 2 = 15(2x - 5) Now {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0, gives x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Showing S is minimum at x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} So, the two numbers are {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = {tex}\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
solution: Let the two numbers be x and y. Then, x + y = 5 or y = 5 - x Let S denote the sum of the cubes of these numbers. Then S = x 3 + y 3 = x 3 + (5 - x) 3 {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 3x 2 - 3(5 - x) 2 = 15(2x - 5) Now {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0, gives x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Showing S is minimum at x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} So, the two numbers are {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = {tex}\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the two numbers be x and y. Then, x + y = 5 or y = 5 - x
Let S denote the sum of the cubes of these numbers. Then
S = x3 + y3 = x3 + (5 - x)3
{tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 3x2 - 3(5 - x)2 = 15(2x - 5)
Now {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0, gives x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Showing S is minimum at x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
So, the two numbers are {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = {tex}\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}

explanation: f'(x) = 4x 3 - 12x 2 + 8x = 4x(x - 1)(x - 2) f'(x) = 0 gives x = 0, 1, 2 for strictly increasing, f'(x) > 0 x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} )
solution: f'(x) = 4x 3 - 12x 2 + 8x = 4x(x - 1)(x - 2) f'(x) = 0 gives x = 0, 1, 2 for strictly increasing, f'(x) > 0 x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} )

f'(x) = 4x3 - 12x2 + 8x = 4x(x - 1)(x - 2)
f'(x) = 0 gives x = 0, 1, 2
for strictly increasing, f'(x) > 0
x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1) {tex}\cup{/tex} (2, {tex}\infty{/tex})

question_text: Find the interval in which the function f(x) = {tex}\text x^3+\frac{1}{\text x^3}{/tex} , x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 is decreasing.
explanation: f l (x) = 3x 2 - {tex}\frac{3}{\text x^4}{/tex} f l (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3\left(\text x^6-1\right)}{\text x^4}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3\left(\text x^3-1\right)\left(\text x^3+1\right)}{\text x^4}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1, 1 ( {tex}\because{/tex} x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is decreasing when x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, 1] - {0}

question_html: If f(x) = a(tan x - cot x), where a > 0, then find whether f(x) is increasing or decreasing function in its domain.
question_text: If f(x) = a(tan x - cot x), where a > 0, then find whether f(x) is increasing or decreasing function in its domain.
explanation: f'(x) = a(sec 2 x + cosec 2 x) As a > 0 and sec 2 x, cosec 2 x are squares, f'(x) > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an increasing function in its domain.
solution: f'(x) = a(sec 2 x + cosec 2 x) As a > 0 and sec 2 x, cosec 2 x are squares, f'(x) > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an increasing function in its domain.
solution_html:
f'(x) = a(sec2 x + cosec2 x)
As a > 0 and sec2 x, cosec2 x are squares, f'(x) > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an increasing function in its domain.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m3. The cost of land is ₹ 5,000 per square metre and cost of digging increases with depth and for the whole tank, it is ₹ 40,000 h2, where h is the depth of the tank in metres. x is the side of the square base of the tank in metres.
ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM

  1. Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum. (2)
    OR
    Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m 3 . The cost of land is ₹ 5,000 per square metre and cost of digging increases with depth and for the whole tank, it is ₹ 40,000 h 2 , where h is the depth of the tank in metres. x is the side of the square base of the tank in metres. ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x. (1) Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} . (1) Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum. (2) OR Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0. (2)
explanation: C = 40000h 2 + 5000x 2 as x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x 2 {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x 6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10 OR {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10). Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0
solution: C = 40000h 2 + 5000x 2 as x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x 2 {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x 6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10 OR {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10). Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0
solution_html:
1. C = 40000h2 + 5000x2
    as x2h = 250
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x2

  2. {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x

  3. For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
    showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10
    OR
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex}) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10).
    Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A housing society wants to commission a swimming pool for its residents. For this, they have to purchase a square piece of land and dig this to such a depth that its capacity is 250 cubic metres. Cost of land is ₹ 500 per square metre. The cost of digging increases with the depth and cost for the whole pool is ₹ 4000 (depth)2.

Suppose the side of the square plot is x metres and depth is h metres.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Write cost C(h) as a function in terms of h. (1)
  2. Find critical point. (1)
  3. Use second derivative test to find the value of h for which cost of constructing the pool is minimum. What is the minimum cost of construction of the pool? (2)

OR

  1. Use first derivative test to find the depth of the pool so that cost of construction is minimum. Also, find relation between x and h for minimum cost. (2)
question_text: A housing society wants to commission a swimming pool for its residents. For this, they have to purchase a square piece of land and dig this to such a depth that its capacity is 250 cubic metres. Cost of land is ₹ 500 per square metre. The cost of digging increases with the depth and cost for the whole pool is ₹ 4000 (depth) 2 . Suppose the side of the square plot is x metres and depth is h metres. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Write cost C(h) as a function in terms of h. (1) Find critical point. (1) Use second derivative test to find the value of h for which cost of constructing the pool is minimum. What is the minimum cost of construction of the pool? (2) OR Use first derivative test to find the depth of the pool so that cost of construction is minimum. Also, find relation between x and h for minimum cost. (2)
explanation: Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex} C(cost ) = 500x 2 + 4000h 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h 2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h 2 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000 {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000 OR we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0 for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0 and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0 By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Now, x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m also, x = 4h
solution: Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex} C(cost ) = 500x 2 + 4000h 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h 2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h 2 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000 {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000 OR we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0 for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0 and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0 By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Now, x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m also, x = 4h
solution_html:
1. Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x2h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex}
    C(cost ) = 500x2 + 4000h2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h2
  2. {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m
  3. {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000
    {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m
    Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000

OR

  1. we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0
    for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0
    and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0
    By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
    Now, x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m
    also, x = 4h

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
In an agricultural institute, scientists do experiments with varieties of seeds to grow them in different environments to produce healthy plants and get more yield.
A scientist observed that a particular seed grew very fast after germination. He had recorded growth of plant since germination and he said that its growth can be defined by the function
f(x) = {tex}\frac 13{/tex}x3 - 4x2 + 15x + 2, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 10
where x is the number of days the plant is exposed to sunlight.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What are the critical points of the function f(x)? (2)
  2. Using second derivative test, find the minimum value of the function. (2)
question_text: In an agricultural institute, scientists do experiments with varieties of seeds to grow them in different environments to produce healthy plants and get more yield. A scientist observed that a particular seed grew very fast after germination. He had recorded growth of plant since germination and he said that its growth can be defined by the function f(x) = {tex}\frac 13{/tex} x 3 - 4x 2 + 15x + 2, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 10 where x is the number of days the plant is exposed to sunlight. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: What are the critical points of the function f(x)? (2) Using second derivative test, find the minimum value of the function. (2)
explanation: f l (x) = x 2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5) f l (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points. Now f II (x) = 2x - 8 f ll (3) < 0 and f ll (5) > 0 so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5. min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5) 2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}
solution: f l (x) = x 2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5) f l (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points. Now f II (x) = 2x - 8 f ll (3) < 0 and f ll (5) > 0 so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5. min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5) 2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. fl(x) = x2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5)
    fl(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points.
  2. Now fII(x) = 2x - 8
    fll(3) < 0 and fll(5) > 0
    so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5.
    min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5)2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Engine displacement is the measure of the cylinder volume swept by all the pistons of a piston engine. The piston moves inside the cylinder bore

The cylinder bore in the form of circular cylinder open at the top is to be made from a metal sheet of area {tex}75\pi{/tex} cm2.

  1. If the radius of cylinder is r cm and height is h cm, then write the volume V of cylinder in terms of radius r. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the radius of cylinder when its volume is maximum. (2)
    OR
    For maximum volume, h > r. State true or false and justify. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Engine displacement is the measure of the cylinder volume swept by all the pistons of a piston engine. The piston moves inside the cylinder bore The cylinder bore in the form of circular cylinder open at the top is to be made from a metal sheet of area {tex}75\pi{/tex} cm 2 . If the radius of cylinder is r cm and height is h cm, then write the volume V of cylinder in terms of radius r. (1) Find {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} . (1) Find the radius of cylinder when its volume is maximum. (2) OR For maximum volume, h > r. State true or false and justify. (2)
explanation: {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (75r - r 3 ) {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (75 - 3r 2 ) {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 OR False, {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r
solution: {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (75r - r 3 ) {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (75 - 3r 2 ) {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 OR False, {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r
solution_html:
1. {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex}, {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}(75r - r3)
  2. {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}(75 - 3r2)
  3. {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}(-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5
    OR
    False,
    {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}(-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5
    As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{x}+7{/tex} is strictly decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} R - {0}.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{x}+7{/tex} is strictly decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} R - {0}.
explanation: The given curve is, {tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x}+7{/tex} {tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \in R, x \neq 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}>0 \Rightarrow-\frac{3}{x^{2}}<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing for {tex}x \in R, x \neq 0{/tex}
solution: The given curve is, {tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x}+7{/tex} {tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \in R, x \neq 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}>0 \Rightarrow-\frac{3}{x^{2}}<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing for {tex}x \in R, x \neq 0{/tex}
solution_html:
The given curve is,
{tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x}+7{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=\frac{3}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}x \in R, x \neq 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}>0 \Rightarrow-\frac{3}{x^{2}}<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex}
Hence, f(x) is decreasing for {tex}x \in R, x \neq 0{/tex}

question_html: The function y = x2e-x is decreasing in the interval
question_text: The function y = x 2 e -x is decreasing in the interval

options.explanation: We have, f(x) = y = x 2 e -x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x e -x + x 2 (-1)e -x = xe -x (2 - x) Now, put {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 and x = 2 The points x = 0 and x = divide the real time into three disjoint intervals i.e., ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0), (0, 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In intervals, ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f'(x) < 0 as e -x is always positive. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) or y is decreasing in ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ).

explanation: We have, f(x) = y = x 2 e -x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x e -x + x 2 (-1)e -x = xe -x (2 - x) Now, put {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 and x = 2 The points x = 0 and x = divide the real time into three disjoint intervals i.e., ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0), (0, 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In intervals, ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f'(x) < 0 as e -x is always positive. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) or y is decreasing in ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ).

question_html: The function f(x) = 2x3 - 15x2 + 36x + 6 is increasing in the interval
question_text: The function f(x) = 2x 3 - 15x 2 + 36x + 6 is increasing in the interval

options.explanation: Given, f(x) = 2x 3 - 15x 2 + 36x + 6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 30x + 36 It f'(x) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, then f(x) is increasing. So, 6x 2 - 30x + 36 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 or, x 2 - 5x + 36 > 0 or, (x - 3)(x - 2) > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 2] {tex}\cup{/tex} [3, {tex}\infty{/tex} )

So, 6x 2 - 30x + 36 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 or, x 2 - 5x + 36 > 0 or, (x - 3)(x - 2) > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 2] {tex}\cup{/tex} [3, {tex}\infty{/tex} )

question_html: A function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R is defined as f(x) = x3 + 1.Thenthe function has
question_text: A function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R is defined as f(x) = x 3 + 1.Thenthe function has

options.text: neither maximum value nor minimum value
options.html: neither maximum value nor minimum value
options.explanation: Given, f(x) = x 3 + 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 3x 2 and f"(x) = 6x Put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 At x = 0, f''(x) = 0 Thus, f(x) has neither maximum value nor minimum value.

correct_option_text: neither maximum value nor minimum value

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The absolute maximum value of the function f(x) {tex}=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex} in the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right] {/tex} is
question_text: The absolute maximum value of the function f(x) {tex}=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex} in the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right] {/tex} is

options.explanation: Given, {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{2}(2 x)=4-x{/tex} put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 Then, we evaluate the f at critical point x = 4 and at the end points of the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} . {tex}f(4)=16-\frac{1}{2}(16){/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 f(-2) {tex}=-8-\frac{1}{2}(4){/tex} = -8 - 2 = -10 {tex}f\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^2{/tex} {tex}=18-\frac{81}{8}=7.875{/tex} Thus, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is 8 occurring at x = 4.

explanation: Given, {tex}f(x)=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{2}(2 x)=4-x{/tex} put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 Then, we evaluate the f at critical point x = 4 and at the end points of the interval {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} . {tex}f(4)=16-\frac{1}{2}(16){/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 f(-2) {tex}=-8-\frac{1}{2}(4){/tex} = -8 - 2 = -10 {tex}f\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^2{/tex} {tex}=18-\frac{81}{8}=7.875{/tex} Thus, the absolute maximum value of f on {tex}\left[-2, \frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} is 8 occurring at x = 4.

question_text: In a sphere of radius r, a right circular cone of height h having maximum curved surface area is inscribed. The expression for the square of curved surface of cone is

question_html:
A factory makes an open cardboard box for a jewellery shop from a square sheet of side 18 cm by cutting off squares from each corner and folding up the flaps.
 
Based on the above information, answer any four of the following five questions, if x is the length of each square cut from corners: 4{tex}\times{/tex}1=4

  1. The volume of the open box is: (1)
    1. 4x (x2 - 18x + 81)
    2. 2x (2x2 + 36x + 162)
    3. 2x (2x2 + 36x - 162)
    4. 4x (x2 + 18x + 81)
  2. The condition for the volume (V) to be maximum is: (1)

    1. {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}<0 {/tex}

    2. {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}>0 {/tex}

    3. {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}>0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}=0 {/tex}

    4. {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}<0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}=0 {/tex}

  3. What should be the side of square to be cut off so that the volume is maximum? (1)
    1. 6 cm
    2. 9 cm
    3. 3 cm
    4. 4 cm
  4. Maximum volume of the open box is: (1)
    1. 423 cm3
    2. 432 cm3
    3. 400 cm3
    4. 216 cm3
  5. The total area of the removed squares is: (1)
    1. 324 cm2
    2. 144 cm2
    3. 36 cm2
    4. 64 cm2
question_text: A factory makes an open cardboard box for a jewellery shop from a square sheet of side 18 cm by cutting off squares from each corner and folding up the flaps. Based on the above information, answer any four of the following five questions, if x is the length of each square cut from corners: 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1=4 The volume of the open box is: (1) 4x (x 2 - 18x + 81) 2x (2x 2 + 36x + 162) 2x (2x 2 + 36x - 162) 4x (x 2 + 18x + 81) The condition for the volume (V) to be maximum is: (1) {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}<0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}>0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}>0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}=0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}<0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}=0 {/tex} What should be the side of square to be cut off so that the volume is maximum? (1) 6 cm 9 cm 3 cm 4 cm Maximum volume of the open box is: (1) 423 cm 3 432 cm 3 400 cm 3 216 cm 3 The total area of the removed squares is: (1) 324 cm 2 144 cm 2 36 cm 2 64 cm 2
solution_html:
1. (a) 4x (x2 - 18x + 81)
  2. (a) {tex} \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=0 \text { and } \frac{{d}^2 {~V}}{{dx}^2}<0 {/tex}
  3. (c) 3 cm
  4. (a) 423 cm3
  5. (c) 36 cm2
