question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = (x - 1)3 (x - 2)2 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = (x - 1) 3 (x - 2) 2 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
explanation: f(x) = (x - 1) 3 (x - 2) 2 Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = (x - 1) 3 {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1 ) ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 1) 3 .2(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 · 3(x -1) 2 = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)[2(x - 1) + 3(x - 2)] = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(2x - 2 + 3 x - 6) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x)=(x -1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) Now, put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) = 0 Either (x - 1) 2 = 0 or x - 2 = 0 or 5x - 8 = 0 {tex}\therefore x = 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2{/tex} Now, we find intervals and check in which interval f(x) is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) Sign of f'(x) x< 1 (+)(-)(-) +ve 1<x< {tex} \frac 85{/tex} (+)(-)(-) +ve {tex} \frac 85{/tex} < x < 2 (+)(-)(+) -ve x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve So, the given function f(x) is increasing on the intervals {tex} ( - \infty , 1 ) \left( 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } \right){/tex} and {tex} ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2 \right){/tex} . {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex} \left[ \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2 \right]{/tex}
solution: f(x) = (x - 1) 3 (x - 2) 2 Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = (x - 1) 3 {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1 ) ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 1) 3 .2(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 · 3(x -1) 2 = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)[2(x - 1) + 3(x - 2)] = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(2x - 2 + 3 x - 6) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x)=(x -1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) Now, put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) = 0 Either (x - 1) 2 = 0 or x - 2 = 0 or 5x - 8 = 0 {tex}\therefore x = 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2{/tex} Now, we find intervals and check in which interval f(x) is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = (x - 1) 2 (x - 2)(5x - 8) Sign of f'(x) x< 1 (+)(-)(-) +ve 1<x< {tex} \frac 85{/tex} (+)(-)(-) +ve {tex} \frac 85{/tex} < x < 2 (+)(-)(+) -ve x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve So, the given function f(x) is increasing on the intervals {tex} ( - \infty , 1 ) \left( 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } \right){/tex} and {tex} ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2 \right){/tex} . {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex} \left[ \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2 \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
f(x) = (x - 1)3(x - 2)2

Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
f'(x) = (x - 1)3{tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1 ) ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 1)3.2(x - 2) + (x - 2)2 · 3(x -1)2
= (x - 1)2 (x - 2)[2(x - 1) + 3(x - 2)]
= (x - 1)2(x - 2)(2x - 2 + 3 x - 6)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x)=(x -1)2(x - 2)(5x - 8)
Now, put f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(x - 1)2(x - 2)(5x - 8) = 0
Either (x - 1)2 = 0 or x - 2 = 0 or 5x - 8 = 0
{tex}\therefore x = 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2{/tex}
Now, we find intervals and check in which interval  f(x) is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) = (x - 1)2(x - 2)(5x - 8) Sign of f'(x)
x< 1 (+)(-)(-) +ve
1<x<{tex} \frac 85{/tex} (+)(-)(-) +ve
{tex} \frac 85{/tex}< x < 2 (+)(-)(+) -ve
x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve

 So, the given function f(x) is increasing on the intervals {tex} ( - \infty , 1 ) \left( 1 , \frac { 8 } { 5 } \right){/tex}and {tex} ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 8 } { 5 } , 2 \right){/tex}.

question_html: Find the minimum value of (ax + by), where xy = c2.
question_text: Find the minimum value of (ax + by), where xy = c 2 .
explanation: Let f(x)=ax+by Then, {tex} f ( x ) = a x + \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \operatorname { given } , x y = c ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } \right]{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex} f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put f'(x)=0 {tex} \Rightarrow a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { a } \Rightarrow x = \pm \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex} Now, {tex} f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = 0 + \frac { 2 b c^2 } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} At {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = + v e{/tex} Hence, f(x) has minimum value at {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex} At {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = - v e{/tex} Hence, f(x) has maximum value at {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex} When {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex} , then {tex} y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) } = \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } c{/tex} {tex} \because{/tex} Minimum value of {tex} f ( x ) = a \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } \cdot c + b \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } \cdot c{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { a b } \cdot c + \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex} {tex} = 2 \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex}
solution: Let f(x)=ax+by Then, {tex} f ( x ) = a x + \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \operatorname { given } , x y = c ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } \right]{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex} f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put f'(x)=0 {tex} \Rightarrow a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { a } \Rightarrow x = \pm \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex} Now, {tex} f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = 0 + \frac { 2 b c^2 } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} At {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = + v e{/tex} Hence, f(x) has minimum value at {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex} At {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = - v e{/tex} Hence, f(x) has maximum value at {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex} When {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex} , then {tex} y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) } = \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } c{/tex} {tex} \because{/tex} Minimum value of {tex} f ( x ) = a \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } \cdot c + b \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } \cdot c{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { a b } \cdot c + \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex} {tex} = 2 \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x)=ax+by

Then, {tex} f ( x ) = a x + \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \operatorname { given } , x y = c ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } \right]{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex} f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put f'(x)=0
{tex} \Rightarrow a - \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } = \frac { b c ^ { 2 } } { a } \Rightarrow x = \pm \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex}
Now, {tex} f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = 0 + \frac { 2 b c^2 } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
At {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = + v e{/tex}
Hence, f(x) has minimum value at {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex}
At {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c , f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 2 b c ^ { 2 } } { \left( - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) ^ { 3 } } = - v e{/tex}
Hence, f(x) has maximum value at {tex} x = - \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c.{/tex}
When {tex} x = \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c{/tex}, then
{tex} y = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { x } = \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } c \right) } = \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } c{/tex}
{tex} \because{/tex} Minimum value of {tex} f ( x ) = a \sqrt { \frac { b } { a } } \cdot c + b \sqrt { \frac { a } { b } } \cdot c{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { a b } \cdot c + \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \sqrt { a b } \cdot c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The least value of the function f(x) = {tex}ax + \frac{b}{x}{/tex} (a > 0, b > 0, x > 0) is ________.
question_text: The least value of the function f(x) = {tex}ax + \frac{b}{x}{/tex} (a > 0, b > 0, x > 0) is ________.
explanation: {tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=a-\frac{b}{x^{2}}{/tex} At {tex}f^{'}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a=\frac{b}{x^{2}} \text { or } x=\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}} \quad(\text { as } x>0){/tex} Thus the least value of f(x) is, {tex}f(\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}})=a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}+\frac{b}{\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}}=\sqrt{a b}+\sqrt{a b}{/tex} {tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex}
solution: {tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=a-\frac{b}{x^{2}}{/tex} At {tex}f^{'}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a=\frac{b}{x^{2}} \text { or } x=\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}} \quad(\text { as } x>0){/tex} Thus the least value of f(x) is, {tex}f(\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}})=a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}+\frac{b}{\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}}=\sqrt{a b}+\sqrt{a b}{/tex} {tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex}

{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=a-\frac{b}{x^{2}}{/tex}
At {tex}f^{'}(x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a=\frac{b}{x^{2}} \text { or } x=\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}} \quad(\text { as } x>0){/tex}
Thus the least value of f(x) is,
{tex}f(\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}})=a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}+\frac{b}{\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}}=\sqrt{a b}+\sqrt{a b}{/tex}
{tex}=2\sqrt {ab} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.
question_text: Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.
explanation: Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i) Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y. Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)] {tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum. So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder, {tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} {tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}
solution: Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i) Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y. Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)] {tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum. So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder, {tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} {tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm
{tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i)
Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y.
Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)]
{tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} 
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} 
Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} 
At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum.
So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder,
{tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} 
{tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}

question_html: Show that  f(x) = (x – 1) ex + 1 is an increasing function for all x > 0.
question_text: Show that f(x) = (x – 1) e x + 1 is an increasing function for all x > 0.
explanation: Given:- f(x) = (x – 1) e x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left((\mathrm{x}-1) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+1\right){/tex} = f'(x) = e x + (x – 1) e x = f'(x) = e x (1+ x – 1) = f'(x) = xe x as given x > 0 = e x > 0 = xe x > 0 = f'(x) > 0 Hence, the condition for f(x) to be increasing Thus, f(x) is increasing for all x > 0
solution: Given:- f(x) = (x – 1) e x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left((\mathrm{x}-1) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+1\right){/tex} = f'(x) = e x + (x – 1) e x = f'(x) = e x (1+ x – 1) = f'(x) = xe x as given x > 0 = e x > 0 = xe x > 0 = f'(x) > 0 Hence, the condition for f(x) to be increasing Thus, f(x) is increasing for all x > 0
solution_html:
Given:- f(x) = (x – 1) ex + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left((\mathrm{x}-1) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+1\right){/tex}
= f'(x) = ex + (x – 1) ex
= f'(x) = ex(1+ x – 1)
= f'(x) = xex
as given
x > 0
= ex > 0
= xex > 0
= f'(x) > 0
Hence, the condition for f(x) to be increasing
Thus, f(x) is increasing for all x > 0

question_html: If the radius of the circle is increasing at the rate of 0.5 cm/s, then the rate of increase of its circumference is ________.
question_text: If the radius of the circle is increasing at the rate of 0.5 cm/s, then the rate of increase of its circumference is ________.
explanation: The circumference of a circle, with radius r, is defined as C(r) = {tex}2\pi r{/tex} ...(i) Given that {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = 0.5cm/sec, we have to calculate {tex}\frac{d C}{d t}{/tex} Differentiating (i) with respect to t, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = {tex}\pi{/tex} cm/sec
solution: The circumference of a circle, with radius r, is defined as C(r) = {tex}2\pi r{/tex} ...(i) Given that {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = 0.5cm/sec, we have to calculate {tex}\frac{d C}{d t}{/tex} Differentiating (i) with respect to t, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = {tex}\pi{/tex} cm/sec
solution_html:
The circumference of a circle, with radius r, is defined as
C(r) = {tex}2\pi r{/tex} ...(i)
Given that {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} = 0.5cm/sec, we have to calculate {tex}\frac{d C}{d t}{/tex}
Differentiating (i) with respect to t, we get
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex}{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5
= {tex}\pi{/tex} cm/sec

question_html: Find the point on the curve y2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2,1).
question_text: Find the point on the curve y 2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2,1).
explanation: Suppose (x, y) be a point on y 2 = 4x and A(2,1) be the given point. Then, AP 2 = (x -2) 2 + (y - 1) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AP 2 = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 1) 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} y 2 = 4x {tex}\because{/tex} x = y 2 / 4] Suppose Z = Ap 2 . Then, Z is maximum or minimum according as AP is maximum or minimum. Now, Z= {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 2) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)\left(\frac{2 y}{4}\right){/tex} + 2(y -1) = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 and, {tex}\frac{d^{2} Z}{d y^{2}}=\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex} . The critical points of Z are given by {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0. {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 Clearly, {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} \mathrm{Z}}{d y^{2}}\right)_{y=2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3(2)^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 3 > 0. Therefore, z is minimum when y = 2. Putting y = 2 in y 2 = 4x, we obtain x = 1. Thus , the coordinates of P are (1,2). Thus, the point (1,2) on y 2 = 4x is nearest to the point (2,1).
solution: Suppose (x, y) be a point on y 2 = 4x and A(2,1) be the given point. Then, AP 2 = (x -2) 2 + (y - 1) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AP 2 = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 1) 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} y 2 = 4x {tex}\because{/tex} x = y 2 / 4] Suppose Z = Ap 2 . Then, Z is maximum or minimum according as AP is maximum or minimum. Now, Z= {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 2) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)\left(\frac{2 y}{4}\right){/tex} + 2(y -1) = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 and, {tex}\frac{d^{2} Z}{d y^{2}}=\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex} . The critical points of Z are given by {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0. {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 Clearly, {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} \mathrm{Z}}{d y^{2}}\right)_{y=2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3(2)^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 3 > 0. Therefore, z is minimum when y = 2. Putting y = 2 in y 2 = 4x, we obtain x = 1. Thus , the coordinates of P are (1,2). Thus, the point (1,2) on y 2 = 4x is nearest to the point (2,1).
solution_html:
Suppose (x, y) be a point on y2 = 4x and A(2,1) be the given point. Then,
AP2 = (x -2)2 + (y - 1)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AP2 = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 1)2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} y2 = 4x {tex}\because{/tex} x = y2 / 4]
Suppose  Z = Ap2. Then, Z is maximum or minimum according as AP is maximum or minimum.
Now, Z= {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 2)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)\left(\frac{2 y}{4}\right){/tex} + 2(y -1) = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 and, {tex}\frac{d^{2} Z}{d y^{2}}=\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex}.
The critical points of Z are given by {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0.
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Z}{d y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2
Clearly, {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} \mathrm{Z}}{d y^{2}}\right)_{y=2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3(2)^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 3 > 0. Therefore, z is minimum when y = 2.
Putting y = 2 in y2 = 4x, we obtain x = 1. Thus , the coordinates of P are (1,2).
Thus, the point (1,2) on y2 = 4x is nearest to the point (2,1).

question_html: Amongst all open (from the top) right circular cylindrical boxes of volume {tex}125\pi{/tex} cm3, find the dimensions of the box which has the least surface area.
question_text: Amongst all open (from the top) right circular cylindrical boxes of volume {tex}125\pi{/tex} cm 3 , find the dimensions of the box which has the least surface area.
explanation: Let radius = r & height = h {tex}\pi \text r^2 \text h {/tex} = {tex}125\pi{/tex} or h = {tex}\frac{125}{\mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} Surface Area, S = {tex}2\pi \text r \text h {/tex} + {tex}\pi \text r^2{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{250 \pi}{r}+\pi r^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{-250 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^2}+2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 5 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{dr}^2}=\frac{500 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^3}+2 \pi>0{/tex} so s is least when r = 5 cm, gives h = 5 cm
solution: Let radius = r & height = h {tex}\pi \text r^2 \text h {/tex} = {tex}125\pi{/tex} or h = {tex}\frac{125}{\mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} Surface Area, S = {tex}2\pi \text r \text h {/tex} + {tex}\pi \text r^2{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{250 \pi}{r}+\pi r^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{-250 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^2}+2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 5 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{dr}^2}=\frac{500 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^3}+2 \pi>0{/tex} so s is least when r = 5 cm, gives h = 5 cm
solution_html:
Let radius = r & height = h
{tex}\pi \text r^2 \text h {/tex} = {tex}125\pi{/tex} or h = {tex}\frac{125}{\mathrm{r}^2}{/tex}
Surface Area, S = {tex}2\pi \text r \text h {/tex} + {tex}\pi \text r^2{/tex}
S = {tex}\frac{250 \pi}{r}+\pi r^2{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{-250 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^2}+2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dS}}{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 5
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{dr}^2}=\frac{500 \pi}{\mathrm{r}^3}+2 \pi>0{/tex} so s is least
when r = 5 cm, gives h = 5 cm

question_html: Show that the height of the right circular cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and radius r is one-third of the height of the cone, and the greatest volume of the cylinder is {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} times the volume of the cone.
question_text: Show that the height of the right circular cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and radius r is one-third of the height of the cone, and the greatest volume of the cylinder is {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} times the volume of the cone.
explanation: The below figure represents the cylinder inscribed in cone, Schematic diagram In a cone, radius {tex}={R}{/tex} , height {tex}={H}{/tex} Semi vertical angle {tex}=\theta{/tex} In a cylinder, radius= {tex}r{/tex} , height=h The expression of similar triangle, {tex}\triangle {ACE} \sim \triangle {FCD}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{{CE}}{{EA}}=\frac{{CD}}{{DF}}{/tex} Substitute the given values, {tex} \therefore \frac{H}{R}=\frac{H-h}{r} {/tex} {tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R} \gamma {/tex} The expression of volume of cylinder, {tex} {V}=\pi {r}^2 {~h}{/tex} Substitute the given value of height of cylinder, {tex} V=\pi r^2\left(H-\frac{H}{R} r\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore V=\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R} {/tex} Differentiate the above expression with respect to radius r and equate it to zero, {tex} \therefore \frac{{d}}{{dr}}\left(\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R}\right)=0 {/tex} {tex} \therefore 2 \pi r H-3 \pi r^2 \frac{H}{R}=0 {/tex} {tex} \left.\therefore r=\frac{2 R}{3} \text { (because radius }>0\right) {/tex} Second differentiate at the given value of radius is negative. So, it gives maxima. Substitute the value of radius in the height of cylinder. {tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R}\left(\frac{2 R}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore h=\frac{H}{3} {/tex} Substitute the calculated value of radius and height in the expression of volume of cylinder. {tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\pi\left(\frac{2 {R}}{3}\right)^2\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\pi\left(\frac{4 {R}^2}{9}\right)\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4}{9}\left(\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}\right) {/tex} The expression for volume of cone, {tex} {V}_{{c}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}{/tex} Substitute the volume of cone in the expression of maximum volume of cylinder. {tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\frac{4}{9} {~V}_{{c}}{/tex} Therefore, the greatest volume of cylinder that can be inscribed in cone has {tex}4 / 9{/tex} volume of cone. That have proved.
solution: The below figure represents the cylinder inscribed in cone, Schematic diagram In a cone, radius {tex}={R}{/tex} , height {tex}={H}{/tex} Semi vertical angle {tex}=\theta{/tex} In a cylinder, radius= {tex}r{/tex} , height=h The expression of similar triangle, {tex}\triangle {ACE} \sim \triangle {FCD}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{{CE}}{{EA}}=\frac{{CD}}{{DF}}{/tex} Substitute the given values, {tex} \therefore \frac{H}{R}=\frac{H-h}{r} {/tex} {tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R} \gamma {/tex} The expression of volume of cylinder, {tex} {V}=\pi {r}^2 {~h}{/tex} Substitute the given value of height of cylinder, {tex} V=\pi r^2\left(H-\frac{H}{R} r\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore V=\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R} {/tex} Differentiate the above expression with respect to radius r and equate it to zero, {tex} \therefore \frac{{d}}{{dr}}\left(\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R}\right)=0 {/tex} {tex} \therefore 2 \pi r H-3 \pi r^2 \frac{H}{R}=0 {/tex} {tex} \left.\therefore r=\frac{2 R}{3} \text { (because radius }>0\right) {/tex} Second differentiate at the given value of radius is negative. So, it gives maxima. Substitute the value of radius in the height of cylinder. {tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R}\left(\frac{2 R}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore h=\frac{H}{3} {/tex} Substitute the calculated value of radius and height in the expression of volume of cylinder. {tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\pi\left(\frac{2 {R}}{3}\right)^2\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\pi\left(\frac{4 {R}^2}{9}\right)\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4}{9}\left(\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}\right) {/tex} The expression for volume of cone, {tex} {V}_{{c}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}{/tex} Substitute the volume of cone in the expression of maximum volume of cylinder. {tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\frac{4}{9} {~V}_{{c}}{/tex} Therefore, the greatest volume of cylinder that can be inscribed in cone has {tex}4 / 9{/tex} volume of cone. That have proved.
solution_html:
The below figure represents the cylinder inscribed in cone,

Schematic diagram
In a cone, radius {tex}={R}{/tex}, height {tex}={H}{/tex}
Semi vertical angle {tex}=\theta{/tex}
In a cylinder, radius= {tex}r{/tex}, height=h
The expression of similar triangle,
{tex}\triangle {ACE} \sim \triangle {FCD}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{{CE}}{{EA}}=\frac{{CD}}{{DF}}{/tex}
Substitute the given values,
{tex} \therefore \frac{H}{R}=\frac{H-h}{r} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R} \gamma {/tex}
The expression of volume of cylinder,
{tex} {V}=\pi {r}^2 {~h}{/tex}
Substitute the given value of height of cylinder,
{tex} V=\pi r^2\left(H-\frac{H}{R} r\right) {/tex}
{tex} \therefore V=\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R} {/tex}
Differentiate the above expression with respect to radius r and equate it to zero,
{tex} \therefore \frac{{d}}{{dr}}\left(\pi r^2 H-\pi r^3 \frac{H}{R}\right)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \therefore 2 \pi r H-3 \pi r^2 \frac{H}{R}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \left.\therefore r=\frac{2 R}{3} \text { (because radius }>0\right) {/tex}
Second differentiate at the given value of radius is negative. So, it gives maxima.
Substitute the value of radius in the height of cylinder.
{tex} \therefore h=H-\frac{H}{R}\left(\frac{2 R}{3}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \therefore h=\frac{H}{3} {/tex}
Substitute the calculated value of radius and height in the expression of volume of cylinder.
{tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\pi\left(\frac{2 {R}}{3}\right)^2\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\pi\left(\frac{4 {R}^2}{9}\right)\left(\frac{{H}}{3}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{4}{9}\left(\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}\right) {/tex}
The expression for volume of cone,
{tex} {V}_{{c}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi {R}^2 {H}{/tex}
Substitute the volume of cone in the expression of maximum volume of cylinder.
{tex} \therefore {V}_{\max }=\frac{4}{9} {~V}_{{c}}{/tex}
Therefore, the greatest volume of cylinder that can be inscribed in cone has {tex}4 / 9{/tex} volume of cone. That have proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The sum of the perimeter of a circle and square is k, where k is some constant. Prove that the sum of their area is least when the side of square is double the radius of the circle.
question_text: The sum of the perimeter of a circle and square is k, where k is some constant. Prove that the sum of their area is least when the side of square is double the radius of the circle.
explanation: r is the radius of circle and x be side of sq. {tex}2\pi r + 4x = K{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {x^2}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r + 2\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right).\left( {0 - \frac{{2\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {K - 2\pi r} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} = 2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {0 - 2\pi } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2\pi + \frac{{2{\pi ^2}}}{4}{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}\left( {k - 2\pi r} \right) = 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}k - 2\pi r = 8r{/tex} {tex}k = 8r + 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{K}{{2(4 + \pi )}} = r{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} > 0{/tex} Area is minimum at {tex}r = \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} {tex}2\pi r + 4x = k{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi r}}{4}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}}}{4}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4k + k\pi - k\pi }}{{4(4 + \pi )}} = \frac{{4k}}{{4(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} x = 2r
solution: r is the radius of circle and x be side of sq. {tex}2\pi r + 4x = K{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {x^2}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r + 2\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right).\left( {0 - \frac{{2\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {K - 2\pi r} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} = 2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {0 - 2\pi } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2\pi + \frac{{2{\pi ^2}}}{4}{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}\left( {k - 2\pi r} \right) = 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}k - 2\pi r = 8r{/tex} {tex}k = 8r + 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{K}{{2(4 + \pi )}} = r{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} > 0{/tex} Area is minimum at {tex}r = \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} {tex}2\pi r + 4x = k{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi r}}{4}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}}}{4}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4k + k\pi - k\pi }}{{4(4 + \pi )}} = \frac{{4k}}{{4(4 + \pi )}}{/tex} x = 2r
solution_html:
r is the radius of circle and x be side of sq.

{tex}2\pi r + 4x = K{/tex}

{tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {x^2}{/tex}

{tex}s = \pi {r^2} + {\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r + 2\left( {\frac{{K - 2\pi r}}{4}} \right).\left( {0 - \frac{{2\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 2\pi r - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {K - 2\pi r} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} = 2\pi - \frac{\pi }{4}\left( {0 - 2\pi } \right){/tex}

{tex} = 2\pi + \frac{{2{\pi ^2}}}{4}{/tex}

For maximum/minimum

{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\frac{\pi }{4}\left( {k - 2\pi r} \right) = 2\pi r{/tex}

{tex}k - 2\pi r = 8r{/tex}

{tex}k = 8r + 2\pi r{/tex}

{tex}\frac{K}{{2(4 + \pi )}} = r{/tex}

Now {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{r^2}}} > 0{/tex}

Area is minimum at  {tex}r = \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}{/tex}

{tex}2\pi r + 4x = k{/tex}

{tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi r}}{4}{/tex}

{tex}x = \frac{{k - 2\pi \frac{k}{{2(4 + \pi )}}}}{4}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{4k + k\pi - k\pi }}{{4(4 + \pi )}} = \frac{{4k}}{{4(4 + \pi )}}{/tex}

x = 2r

question_html: A tank with rectangular base and rectangular sides, open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m3. If building of tank costs Rs 70 per sq.metres for the base and Rs 45 per sq. metre for sides. What is the cost of least expensive tank?
question_text: A tank with rectangular base and rectangular sides, open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m 3 . If building of tank costs Rs 70 per sq.metres for the base and Rs 45 per sq. metre for sides. What is the cost of least expensive tank?
explanation: Let x and y be the length and width of rectangular base, v be the volume. v = 8 (Given) v = 2xy 8 = 2xy {tex}y = \frac{4}{x}{/tex} {tex}s = (xy) \times 70 + 2(x + y) \times 45{/tex} {tex} = x \times \frac{4}{x} \times 70 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 280 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 + 90\left( {1 - \frac{4}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = 90\left( {0 + \frac{8}{{{x^3}}}} \right){/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} x = 2 {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 2}}=\frac{{720}}{{{2^3}}} > 0{/tex} s is Minimum at x = 2 Minimum cost is given by {tex}s = 280 + 90\left( {2 + \frac{4}{2}} \right){/tex} = 280 + 90 (4) = 280 + 360 = 640
solution: Let x and y be the length and width of rectangular base, v be the volume. v = 8 (Given) v = 2xy 8 = 2xy {tex}y = \frac{4}{x}{/tex} {tex}s = (xy) \times 70 + 2(x + y) \times 45{/tex} {tex} = x \times \frac{4}{x} \times 70 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 280 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 + 90\left( {1 - \frac{4}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = 90\left( {0 + \frac{8}{{{x^3}}}} \right){/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} x = 2 {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 2}}=\frac{{720}}{{{2^3}}} > 0{/tex} s is Minimum at x = 2 Minimum cost is given by {tex}s = 280 + 90\left( {2 + \frac{4}{2}} \right){/tex} = 280 + 90 (4) = 280 + 360 = 640
solution_html:
Let x and y be the length and width of rectangular base, v be the volume.
v = 8 (Given)
v = 2xy
8 = 2xy
{tex}y = \frac{4}{x}{/tex}
{tex}s = (xy) \times 70 + 2(x + y) \times 45{/tex}
{tex} = x \times \frac{4}{x} \times 70 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 280 + 90\left( {x + \frac{4}{x}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 + 90\left( {1 - \frac{4}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex}
={tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = 90\left( {0 + \frac{8}{{{x^3}}}} \right){/tex}
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}
x = 2
{tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 2}}=\frac{{720}}{{{2^3}}} > 0{/tex}
s is Minimum at x = 2
Minimum cost is given by
{tex}s = 280 + 90\left( {2 + \frac{4}{2}} \right){/tex}
= 280 + 90 (4)
= 280 + 360
= 640

question_html: Show that the right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is such that its height is equal to the diameter of the base.
question_text: Show that the right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is such that its height is equal to the diameter of the base.
explanation: Let radius of the cylinder = r Height of the cylinder = h| {tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex} (1) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} From equ (1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} Hence h = 2r
solution: Let radius of the cylinder = r Height of the cylinder = h| {tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex} (1) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} From equ (1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} Hence h = 2r
solution_html:
Let radius of the cylinder = r
Height of the cylinder = h|

{tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex}     (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} 
Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
From equ (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} 
Hence h = 2r

question_html: Show that the height of a cylinder, which is open at the top, having a given surface area and greatest volume, is equal to the radius of its base.
question_text: Show that the height of a cylinder, which is open at the top, having a given surface area and greatest volume, is equal to the radius of its base.
explanation: Let Given surface area of open cylinder be S. Then S = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh + {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} Volume V = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 h {tex}\mathrm{V}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\left[\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\operatorname{Sr}-\pi r^{3}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{S}-3 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right]{/tex} For maximum or minimum value, we have {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{S-3 \pi r^{2}}{2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S=3 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh + {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 3 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}=-3 \pi \mathrm{r}<0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For volume to be maximum, height = radius
solution: Let Given surface area of open cylinder be S. Then S = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh + {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} Volume V = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 h {tex}\mathrm{V}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\left[\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\operatorname{Sr}-\pi r^{3}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{S}-3 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right]{/tex} For maximum or minimum value, we have {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{S-3 \pi r^{2}}{2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S=3 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh + {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 3 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rh = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}=-3 \pi \mathrm{r}<0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For volume to be maximum, height = radius
solution_html:
Let Given surface area of open cylinder be S.
Then S = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh + {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
Volume V = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2h
{tex}\mathrm{V}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\left[\frac{\mathrm{S}-\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\operatorname{Sr}-\pi r^{3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{S}-3 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right]{/tex}
For maximum or minimum value, we have
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{S-3 \pi r^{2}}{2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S=3 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh + {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = 3{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dr}^{2}}=-3 \pi \mathrm{r}<0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For volume to be maximum, height = radius

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the height of the cylinder of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{{2R}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Also find the maximum volume.
question_text: Show that the height of the cylinder of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{{2R}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Also find the maximum volume.
explanation: {tex}V = \pi {r^2}.2x{/tex} {tex} [\because OL = x , LM = 2x ]{/tex} {tex} = \pi .({a^2} - {x^2}).2x{/tex} {tex}V = 2\pi ({a^2}x - {x^3}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi ({a^2} - 3{x^2}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi \left[ {0 - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 12\pi x{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}2\pi [{a^2} - 3{x^2}] = 0{/tex} {tex}{a^2} = 3{x^2} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} = x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = - 12\pi .\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} = negative maximum Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Height of cylinder of maximum volume is = 2x {tex} = 2 \times \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V = \pi {r^2}.2x{/tex} {tex} [\because OL = x , LM = 2x ]{/tex} {tex} = \pi .({a^2} - {x^2}).2x{/tex} {tex}V = 2\pi ({a^2}x - {x^3}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi ({a^2} - 3{x^2}){/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi \left[ {0 - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 12\pi x{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}2\pi [{a^2} - 3{x^2}] = 0{/tex} {tex}{a^2} = 3{x^2} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} = x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = - 12\pi .\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} = negative maximum Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Height of cylinder of maximum volume is = 2x {tex} = 2 \times \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}V = \pi {r^2}.2x{/tex} {tex} [\because OL = x , LM = 2x ]{/tex} 
{tex} = \pi .({a^2} - {x^2}).2x{/tex} 
{tex}V = 2\pi ({a^2}x - {x^3}){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 2\pi ({a^2} - 3{x^2}){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2\pi \left[ {0 - 6x} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = - 12\pi x{/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}2\pi [{a^2} - 3{x^2}] = 0{/tex} 
{tex}{a^2} = 3{x^2} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} = x{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = - 12\pi .\frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
= negative maximum
Volume is maximum at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
Height of cylinder of maximum volume is
= 2x
{tex} = 2 \times \frac{a}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

question_html: Prove that the radius of the right circular cylinder of greatest curved surface area, which can be inscribed in a given cone, is half of that of the cone.
question_text: Prove that the radius of the right circular cylinder of greatest curved surface area, which can be inscribed in a given cone, is half of that of the cone.
explanation: Let OC = r be the radius of the cone and OA = h be its height. Let a cylinder with radius OE = x inscribed in the given cone The height QE of the cylinder is given by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{EC}}{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} (since {tex}\Delta{/tex} QEC {tex} \sim {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOC) or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{h}=\frac{r-x}{r}{/tex} or QE = {tex}\frac{h(r-x)}{r}{/tex} Let S be the curved surface area of the given cylinder. Then S ≡ S( {tex}x{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{2 \pi x h(r-x)}{r}=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}\left(r x-x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~~ {S^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}(r-2 x)} \\ \Rightarrow~~{S^{{\prime}{\prime}}(x)=\frac{-4 \pi h}{r}}{/tex} Now S' ( {tex}x{/tex} ) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} . Since S″( {tex}x{/tex} ) < 0 for all x, S'' {tex}\left(\frac{r}{2}\right){/tex} < 0. So, {tex}x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} is a point of maxima of S. Hence, the radius of the cylinder of greatest curved surface area which can be inscribed in a given cone is half of that of the cone.
solution: Let OC = r be the radius of the cone and OA = h be its height. Let a cylinder with radius OE = x inscribed in the given cone The height QE of the cylinder is given by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{EC}}{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} (since {tex}\Delta{/tex} QEC {tex} \sim {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOC) or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{h}=\frac{r-x}{r}{/tex} or QE = {tex}\frac{h(r-x)}{r}{/tex} Let S be the curved surface area of the given cylinder. Then S ≡ S( {tex}x{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{2 \pi x h(r-x)}{r}=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}\left(r x-x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~~ {S^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}(r-2 x)} \\ \Rightarrow~~{S^{{\prime}{\prime}}(x)=\frac{-4 \pi h}{r}}{/tex} Now S' ( {tex}x{/tex} ) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} . Since S″( {tex}x{/tex} ) < 0 for all x, S'' {tex}\left(\frac{r}{2}\right){/tex} < 0. So, {tex}x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} is a point of maxima of S. Hence, the radius of the cylinder of greatest curved surface area which can be inscribed in a given cone is half of that of the cone.
solution_html:
Let OC = r be the radius of the cone and OA = h be its height. Let a cylinder with radius OE = x inscribed in the given cone

The height QE of the cylinder is given by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{EC}}{\mathrm{OC}}{/tex} (since {tex}\Delta{/tex}QEC {tex} \sim {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}AOC)
or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{h}=\frac{r-x}{r}{/tex}
or QE = {tex}\frac{h(r-x)}{r}{/tex}
Let S be the curved surface area of the given cylinder. Then
S ≡ S({tex}x{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{2 \pi x h(r-x)}{r}=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}\left(r x-x^{2}\right){/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow~~ {S^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2 \pi h}{r}(r-2 x)} \\ \Rightarrow~~{S^{{\prime}{\prime}}(x)=\frac{-4 \pi h}{r}}{/tex}
Now S' ({tex}x{/tex}) = 0 gives x = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}. 
Since S″({tex}x{/tex}) < 0 for all x, S''{tex}\left(\frac{r}{2}\right){/tex} < 0.
So, {tex}x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} is a point of maxima of S. Hence, the radius of the cylinder of greatest curved surface area which can be inscribed in a given cone is half of that of the cone.

question_html: Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex}.
question_text: Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex} .
explanation: Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig. The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say) Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum. Maximum area {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.
solution: Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig. The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say) Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum. Maximum area {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig.

The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say)
Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} 
Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} 
Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum.
Maximum area  {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.

question_html: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex}. Also show that the maximum volume of the cone is {tex}\frac{8}{27}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
question_text: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex} . Also show that the maximum volume of the cone is {tex}\frac{8}{27}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
explanation: Let R be the radius of cone. Let OA = OB = r (radius of sphere) AC = r + x ...(i) (height of cone) Let V be the volume of cone To Prove: AC = {tex}\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex} Now, V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(\mathrm{AC}){/tex} (from the figure) {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(r+x){/tex} ......(ii) Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex} OBC, (OB) 2 = (OC) 2 + (BC) 2 r 2 = x 2 + (BC) 2 r 2 - x 2 = (BC) 2 ...(iii) {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(r^{2}-x^{2}\right)(r+x){/tex} Differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left[(r+x)^{2}(-1)+(r-x) \cdot 2(r+x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)[-r-x+2 r-2 x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x){/tex} For maximum or minimum volume, {tex}\frac{d V}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=r \ or\ x=\frac{r}{3}{/tex} x = r is not possible. Now again differentiating w.r.t. x 1 , we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[(r+x)(-3)+(r-3 x)(1)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[-3 r-3 x+r-3 x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-2 r-6 x){/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right|_{x=\frac{r}{3}}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(-2 r-\frac{6 r}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-4 \pi r}{3}<0{/tex} So, Volume of cone is maximum when x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} Now, put x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} in (i) AC = r + x = r + {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} AC = {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex} Maximum volume {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \pi \cdot\left\{-\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^3+2\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^2 r\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{27} \cdot\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{27}{/tex} (volume of sphere)
solution: Let R be the radius of cone. Let OA = OB = r (radius of sphere) AC = r + x ...(i) (height of cone) Let V be the volume of cone To Prove: AC = {tex}\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex} Now, V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(\mathrm{AC}){/tex} (from the figure) {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(r+x){/tex} ......(ii) Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex} OBC, (OB) 2 = (OC) 2 + (BC) 2 r 2 = x 2 + (BC) 2 r 2 - x 2 = (BC) 2 ...(iii) {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(r^{2}-x^{2}\right)(r+x){/tex} Differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left[(r+x)^{2}(-1)+(r-x) \cdot 2(r+x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)[-r-x+2 r-2 x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x){/tex} For maximum or minimum volume, {tex}\frac{d V}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=r \ or\ x=\frac{r}{3}{/tex} x = r is not possible. Now again differentiating w.r.t. x 1 , we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[(r+x)(-3)+(r-3 x)(1)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[-3 r-3 x+r-3 x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-2 r-6 x){/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right|_{x=\frac{r}{3}}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(-2 r-\frac{6 r}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-4 \pi r}{3}<0{/tex} So, Volume of cone is maximum when x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} Now, put x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} in (i) AC = r + x = r + {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} AC = {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex} Maximum volume {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \pi \cdot\left\{-\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^3+2\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^2 r\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{27} \cdot\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{27}{/tex} (volume of sphere)
solution_html:
Let R be the radius of cone. 
Let OA = OB = r (radius of sphere)
AC = r + x ...(i) (height of cone)
Let V be the volume of cone

To Prove: AC = {tex}\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex}
Now, V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(\mathrm{AC}){/tex} (from the figure)
{tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}(\mathrm{BC})^{2}(r+x){/tex}......(ii)
Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}OBC,
(OB)2 = (OC)2 + (BC)2
r2 = x2 + (BC)2
r2 - x2 = (BC)2 ...(iii)
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(r^{2}-x^{2}\right)(r+x){/tex}
Differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left[(r+x)^{2}(-1)+(r-x) \cdot 2(r+x)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)[-r-x+2 r-2 x]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x){/tex}
For maximum or minimum volume,
{tex}\frac{d V}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi}{3}(r+x)(r-3 x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=r \ or\ x=\frac{r}{3}{/tex}
x = r is not possible.
Now again differentiating w.r.t. x1, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[(r+x)(-3)+(r-3 x)(1)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}[-3 r-3 x+r-3 x]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-2 r-6 x){/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right|_{x=\frac{r}{3}}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(-2 r-\frac{6 r}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-4 \pi r}{3}<0{/tex}
So, Volume of cone is maximum when x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex}
Now, put x = {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex} in (i)
AC = r + x
= r + {tex}\frac{r}{3}{/tex}
AC = {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex}
Maximum volume
{tex}=\frac{1}{3} \pi \cdot\left\{-\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^3+2\left(\frac{4 r}{3}\right)^2 r\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{27} \cdot\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{27}{/tex} (volume of sphere)

question_html: An isosceles triangle of vertical angle 2{tex}\theta{/tex} is inscribed in a circle of radius a. Show that the area of the triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}.
question_text: An isosceles triangle of vertical angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} is inscribed in a circle of radius a. Show that the area of the triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} .
explanation: Let ABC be an isosceles triangle inscribed in the circle with radius a such that AB = AC. {tex}AD = AO + OD = a + a\cos 2\theta{/tex} and {tex}BC = 2BD = 2a\sin 2\theta{/tex} (see fig.) Therefore, area of the triangle ABC i.e. {tex}\triangle = \frac{1}{2}BC.AD{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}2a\sin 2\theta .\left( {a + a\cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= {a^2}\sin 2\theta \left( {1 + \cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \triangle = {a^2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{1}{2}{a^2}\sin 4\theta {/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 2{a^2}\cos 2\theta + 2{a^2}\cos 4\theta {/tex} {tex} = 2{a^2}\left( {\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = - \cos 4\theta = \cos \left( {\pi - 4\theta } \right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}2\theta = \pi - 4\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}\Delta }}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 2{a^2}\left( { - 2\sin 2\theta - 4\sin 4\theta } \right) < 0\left( {at\ \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex} Therefore, Area of triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta = \frac{\pi }{6}.{/tex}
solution: Let ABC be an isosceles triangle inscribed in the circle with radius a such that AB = AC. {tex}AD = AO + OD = a + a\cos 2\theta{/tex} and {tex}BC = 2BD = 2a\sin 2\theta{/tex} (see fig.) Therefore, area of the triangle ABC i.e. {tex}\triangle = \frac{1}{2}BC.AD{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}2a\sin 2\theta .\left( {a + a\cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= {a^2}\sin 2\theta \left( {1 + \cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \triangle = {a^2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{1}{2}{a^2}\sin 4\theta {/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 2{a^2}\cos 2\theta + 2{a^2}\cos 4\theta {/tex} {tex} = 2{a^2}\left( {\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = - \cos 4\theta = \cos \left( {\pi - 4\theta } \right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}2\theta = \pi - 4\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}\Delta }}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 2{a^2}\left( { - 2\sin 2\theta - 4\sin 4\theta } \right) < 0\left( {at\ \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex} Therefore, Area of triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta = \frac{\pi }{6}.{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABC be an isosceles triangle inscribed in the circle with radius a such that AB = AC.
{tex}AD = AO + OD = a + a\cos 2\theta{/tex}and {tex}BC = 2BD = 2a\sin 2\theta{/tex} (see fig.)

Therefore, area of the triangle ABC i.e. {tex}\triangle = \frac{1}{2}BC.AD{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}2a\sin 2\theta .\left( {a + a\cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex}= {a^2}\sin 2\theta \left( {1 + \cos 2\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \triangle = {a^2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{1}{2}{a^2}\sin 4\theta {/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 2{a^2}\cos 2\theta + 2{a^2}\cos 4\theta {/tex} 
{tex} = 2{a^2}\left( {\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta } \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{d\Delta }}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = - \cos 4\theta = \cos \left( {\pi - 4\theta } \right){/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}2\theta = \pi - 4\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}\Delta }}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 2{a^2}\left( { - 2\sin 2\theta - 4\sin 4\theta } \right) < 0\left( {at\ \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}} \right){/tex}
Therefore, Area of triangle is maximum when {tex}\theta = \frac{\pi }{6}.{/tex}

question_html: Show that {tex}f(x)=\cos (2 x + \frac{\pi } 4){/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}(\frac{3 \pi } 8, \frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}
question_text: Show that {tex}f(x)=\cos (2 x + \frac{\pi } 4){/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}(\frac{3 \pi } 8, \frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}f(x)=\cos (2 x+ \frac{\pi } 4){/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin (2 x+ \frac{\pi }4){/tex} Now, {tex}x \in(\frac {3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 \pi } 8<x<\frac{7 \pi } 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ 3 \pi } 4<2 x< \frac{7 \pi } 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ \pi } 4 + \frac{3 \pi } 4 < 2 x + \frac{\pi } 4 < \frac{7 \pi }4 + \frac{\pi } 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi } 4<2 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)<0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-2 \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} Hence, f(x) is increasing on ( {tex}\frac{3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}f(x)=\cos (2 x+ \frac{\pi } 4){/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin (2 x+ \frac{\pi }4){/tex} Now, {tex}x \in(\frac {3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 \pi } 8<x<\frac{7 \pi } 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ 3 \pi } 4<2 x< \frac{7 \pi } 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ \pi } 4 + \frac{3 \pi } 4 < 2 x + \frac{\pi } 4 < \frac{7 \pi }4 + \frac{\pi } 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi } 4<2 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)<0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-2 \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} Hence, f(x) is increasing on ( {tex}\frac{3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f(x)=\cos (2 x+ \frac{\pi } 4){/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin (2 x+ \frac{\pi }4){/tex}
Now, 
{tex}x \in(\frac {3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 \pi } 8<x<\frac{7 \pi } 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ 3 \pi } 4<2 x< \frac{7 \pi } 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{ \pi } 4 + \frac{3 \pi } 4 < 2 x + \frac{\pi } 4 < \frac{7 \pi }4 + \frac{\pi } 4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi } 4<2 \pi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-2 \sin (2 x+\frac{\pi } 4)>0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex}
Hence, f(x) is increasing on ({tex}\frac{3 \pi } 8,\frac{7 \pi } 8){/tex}

question_html: A ladder 13 m long is leaning against a vertical wall. The bottom of the ladder is dragged away from the wall along the ground at the rate of 2 cm/sec. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 5 m away from the wall?
question_text: A ladder 13 m long is leaning against a vertical wall. The bottom of the ladder is dragged away from the wall along the ground at the rate of 2 cm/sec. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 5 m away from the wall?
explanation: Let y m be the height of the wall at which the ladder touches. Also, let the foot of the ladder be x m away from the wall. Then, by Pythagoras theorem, we have: {tex} x^2+y^2=169 {/tex} .. [length of the ladder is 13 m] {tex} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{169-x^2} {/tex} Then, the rate of change of height {tex}(y){/tex} with respect to time {tex}(t){/tex} is given by, {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{-{x}}{\sqrt{169-{x}^2}} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} Now, when {tex}x=5 {~m}{/tex} , we have: {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{-2 \times 5}{\sqrt{169-5^2}}=\frac{-10}{\sqrt{144}}=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex} Hence, the height of the ladder on the wall is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{5}{6} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} .
solution: Let y m be the height of the wall at which the ladder touches. Also, let the foot of the ladder be x m away from the wall. Then, by Pythagoras theorem, we have: {tex} x^2+y^2=169 {/tex} .. [length of the ladder is 13 m] {tex} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{169-x^2} {/tex} Then, the rate of change of height {tex}(y){/tex} with respect to time {tex}(t){/tex} is given by, {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{-{x}}{\sqrt{169-{x}^2}} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} Now, when {tex}x=5 {~m}{/tex} , we have: {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{-2 \times 5}{\sqrt{169-5^2}}=\frac{-10}{\sqrt{144}}=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex} Hence, the height of the ladder on the wall is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{5}{6} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let y m be the height of the wall at which the ladder touches. Also, let the foot of the ladder be x m away from the wall.
Then, by Pythagoras theorem, we have:

{tex} x^2+y^2=169 {/tex} .. [length of the ladder is 13 m]
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{169-x^2} {/tex}

Then, the rate of change of height {tex}(y){/tex} with respect to time {tex}(t){/tex} is given by,

{tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{-{x}}{\sqrt{169-{x}^2}} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

Now, when {tex}x=5 {~m}{/tex}, we have:

{tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{-2 \times 5}{\sqrt{169-5^2}}=\frac{-10}{\sqrt{144}}=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex}

Hence, the height of the ladder on the wall is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{5}{6} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm3/sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm 3 /sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
explanation: Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} {tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8 {tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 12 cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution: Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} {tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8 {tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 12 cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t

Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex}

{tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8             

{tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} 

{tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)]

{tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex}

Putting x = 12 cm (given),

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex}

Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex}  is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}

question_html: Find the point on the curve y2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2, -8).
question_text: Find the point on the curve y 2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2, -8).
explanation: Suppose P (x,y) be a point on y 2 = 4x ...(i) Let S be the square of the distance between A(2, -8) and P(x,y) {tex}\because{/tex} S = (x - 2) 2 + (y + 8) 2 ...(ii) Using (i), S = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y + 8) 2 {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{DS}{DY}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right) \times \frac{y}{2}{/tex} + 2 (y + 8) = {tex}\frac{y^{3}-8 y}{4}{/tex} +2y + 16 = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16 For maxima and minima,we have {tex}\frac{DY}{DS}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 4 Now,we have {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex} At y = - 4, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = 12 > 0 {tex}\because{/tex} y = -4 is the point of local minima From (i) x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 4 Thus, the required point is (4, -4) nearest to (2, -8).
solution: Suppose P (x,y) be a point on y 2 = 4x ...(i) Let S be the square of the distance between A(2, -8) and P(x,y) {tex}\because{/tex} S = (x - 2) 2 + (y + 8) 2 ...(ii) Using (i), S = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y + 8) 2 {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{DS}{DY}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right) \times \frac{y}{2}{/tex} + 2 (y + 8) = {tex}\frac{y^{3}-8 y}{4}{/tex} +2y + 16 = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16 For maxima and minima,we have {tex}\frac{DY}{DS}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 4 Now,we have {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex} At y = - 4, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = 12 > 0 {tex}\because{/tex} y = -4 is the point of local minima From (i) x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 4 Thus, the required point is (4, -4) nearest to (2, -8).
solution_html:
Suppose P (x,y) be a point on
y2 = 4x ...(i)
Let S be the square of the distance between A(2, -8) and P(x,y)
{tex}\because{/tex} S = (x - 2)2 + (y + 8)2 ...(ii)
Using (i),
S = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y + 8)2
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{DS}{DY}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right) \times \frac{y}{2}{/tex} + 2 (y + 8)
= {tex}\frac{y^{3}-8 y}{4}{/tex} +2y + 16
= {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16
For maxima and minima,we have
{tex}\frac{DY}{DS}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{4}{/tex} + 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 4
Now,we have
{tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex}
At y = - 4, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = 12 > 0
{tex}\because{/tex} y = -4 is the point of local minima
From (i)
x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{4}{/tex} = 4
Thus, the required point is (4, -4) nearest to (2, -8).

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 4x3 - 6x2 - 72x + 30 is (a) strictly increasing, (b) strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 4x 3 - 6x 2 - 72x + 30 is (a) strictly increasing, (b) strictly decreasing.
explanation: {tex}f(x) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = 12{x^2} - 12x - 72{/tex} {tex} = 12({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} {tex} = 12[{x^2} - 3x + 2x - 6]{/tex} {tex} = 12[x(x - 3) + 2(x - 3)]{/tex} {tex} = 12(x - 3)(x + 2){/tex} Put f'(x) = 0 x = -2, 3 int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} + ve Increase (-2, 3) - ve Decrease {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} + ve Increase Hence function is increasing in {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} decreasing in (-2, 3)
solution: {tex}f(x) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = 12{x^2} - 12x - 72{/tex} {tex} = 12({x^2} - x - 6){/tex} {tex} = 12[{x^2} - 3x + 2x - 6]{/tex} {tex} = 12[x(x - 3) + 2(x - 3)]{/tex} {tex} = 12(x - 3)(x + 2){/tex} Put f'(x) = 0 x = -2, 3 int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} + ve Increase (-2, 3) - ve Decrease {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} + ve Increase Hence function is increasing in {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} decreasing in (-2, 3)
solution_html:
{tex}f(x) = 4{x^3} - 6{x^2} - 72x + 30{/tex}
{tex}f'(x) = 12{x^2} - 12x - 72{/tex}
{tex} = 12({x^2} - x - 6){/tex}
{tex} = 12[{x^2} - 3x + 2x - 6]{/tex}
{tex} = 12[x(x - 3) + 2(x - 3)]{/tex}
{tex} = 12(x - 3)(x + 2){/tex}
Put f'(x) = 0
x = -2, 3

int Sign of f’(x) Result
{tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex} + ve Increase
(-2, 3) - ve Decrease
{tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} + ve Increase

Hence function is

  1. increasing in {tex}( - \infty , - 2){/tex}
  2. {tex}(3,\infty ){/tex} decreasing in (-2, 3)

question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima, if any, of the following function. Also find the local maximum and the local minimum values, as the case may be: f(x) = {tex}\sin \mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 \mathrm{x}, 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima, if any, of the following function. Also find the local maximum and the local minimum values, as the case may be: f(x) = {tex}\sin \mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 \mathrm{x}, 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin 2x f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx - 2 sin x cosx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx (1 - 2 sinx) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} f''(x) = -sin x - 2cos 2x f'' {tex}(\frac{\pi}{6}){/tex} < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {6}{/tex} is a local maxima. Local Max. Value = {tex}f(\frac{\pi}{6}) = \frac {3} {4}{/tex} Local extreme values do exist at end points x = 0, x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}
solution: f(x) = {tex}\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin 2x f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx - 2 sin x cosx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx (1 - 2 sinx) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} f''(x) = -sin x - 2cos 2x f'' {tex}(\frac{\pi}{6}){/tex} < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {6}{/tex} is a local maxima. Local Max. Value = {tex}f(\frac{\pi}{6}) = \frac {3} {4}{/tex} Local extreme values do exist at end points x = 0, x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex}\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x - sin 2x
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx - 2 sin x cosx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosx (1 - 2 sinx) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
f''(x) = -sin x - 2cos 2x
f''{tex}(\frac{\pi}{6}){/tex} < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {6}{/tex} is a local maxima.
Local Max. Value = {tex}f(\frac{\pi}{6}) = \frac {3} {4}{/tex}
Local extreme values do exist at end points x = 0, x = {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}
