question_html: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac {4r} {3}{/tex}. Also find the maximum volume of cone.
question_text: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac {4r} {3}{/tex} . Also find the maximum volume of cone.
explanation: Correct Figure r 2 = x 2 + R2 Now, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi R^2 \mathrm{H}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r 2 - x 2 ) (r + x) = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r + x) 2 (r - x) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} [(r + x) 2 (-1) + (r - x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2(r + x)] = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r + x) (r - 3x) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -r or x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex} (Rejected) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} [(r + x)(-3) + (r - 3x)] = {tex}-\pi{/tex} H < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V is maximum when x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex} . H = r + x = {tex}r+\frac{r}{3}=\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex} Maximum volume V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(r+\frac{r}{3}\right)^2\left(r-\frac{r}{3}\right)=\frac{32}{81} \pi r^3{/tex}
solution: Correct Figure r 2 = x 2 + R2 Now, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi R^2 \mathrm{H}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r 2 - x 2 ) (r + x) = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r + x) 2 (r - x) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} [(r + x) 2 (-1) + (r - x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2(r + x)] = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} (r + x) (r - 3x) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -r or x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex} (Rejected) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex} [(r + x)(-3) + (r - 3x)] = {tex}-\pi{/tex} H < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V is maximum when x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex} . H = r + x = {tex}r+\frac{r}{3}=\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex} Maximum volume V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(r+\frac{r}{3}\right)^2\left(r-\frac{r}{3}\right)=\frac{32}{81} \pi r^3{/tex}
solution_html:
Correct Figure

r2 = x2 + R2
Now, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi R^2 \mathrm{H}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex}(r2 - x2) (r + x)
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex}(r + x)2 (r - x)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex}[(r + x)2 (-1) + (r - x){tex}\cdot{/tex}2(r + x)]
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex}(r + x) (r - 3x)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -r or x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex}
(Rejected)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}=\frac{1}{3} \pi{/tex}[(r + x)(-3) + (r - 3x)] = {tex}-\pi{/tex}H < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V is maximum when x = {tex}\frac {r} {3}{/tex}.
H = r + x = {tex}r+\frac{r}{3}=\frac{4 r}{3}{/tex}
Maximum volume V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(r+\frac{r}{3}\right)^2\left(r-\frac{r}{3}\right)=\frac{32}{81} \pi r^3{/tex}

question_html: An Apache helicopter of enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7), wants to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him. Find the nearest distance.
question_text: An Apache helicopter of enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x 2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7), wants to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him. Find the nearest distance.
explanation: For each value of x, the position of the Helicopter is at point {tex}(x, x^2 + 7){/tex} . Therefore, the distance between the helicopter and the soldier placed at (3,7) is given by {tex}|(x, x^2+7)-(3, 7)|=\sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+\left(x^{2}+7-7\right)^{2}}= \sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+x^{4}}{/tex} Let {tex}f (x) = (x – 3)^2 + x^4{/tex} or {tex} f ′(x) = 2(x – 3) + 4x^3 =2(x-1)(2x^2+2x+3){/tex} Thus, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 1 or 2x 2 + 2x + 3 = 0 for which there are no real roots. Also, there are no end points of the interval to be added to the set for which f ′ is zero, i.e., there is only one point, namely, x = 1. The value of f at this point is given by f (1) = (1 – 3) 2 + (1) 4 = 5. Thus, the distance between the solider and the helicopter is {tex}\sqrt{f(1)}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} . Note that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is either a maximum value or a minimim value. Since {tex}\sqrt{f(0)}=\sqrt{(0-3)^{2}+(0)^{4}}=3>\sqrt{5}{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimim value of {tex}\sqrt{f(x)}{/tex} . Hence, {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimum distance between the soldier and the helicopter.
solution: For each value of x, the position of the Helicopter is at point {tex}(x, x^2 + 7){/tex} . Therefore, the distance between the helicopter and the soldier placed at (3,7) is given by {tex}|(x, x^2+7)-(3, 7)|=\sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+\left(x^{2}+7-7\right)^{2}}= \sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+x^{4}}{/tex} Let {tex}f (x) = (x – 3)^2 + x^4{/tex} or {tex} f ′(x) = 2(x – 3) + 4x^3 =2(x-1)(2x^2+2x+3){/tex} Thus, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 1 or 2x 2 + 2x + 3 = 0 for which there are no real roots. Also, there are no end points of the interval to be added to the set for which f ′ is zero, i.e., there is only one point, namely, x = 1. The value of f at this point is given by f (1) = (1 – 3) 2 + (1) 4 = 5. Thus, the distance between the solider and the helicopter is {tex}\sqrt{f(1)}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} . Note that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is either a maximum value or a minimim value. Since {tex}\sqrt{f(0)}=\sqrt{(0-3)^{2}+(0)^{4}}=3>\sqrt{5}{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimim value of {tex}\sqrt{f(x)}{/tex} . Hence, {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimum distance between the soldier and the helicopter.
solution_html:
For each value of x, the position of the Helicopter is at point {tex}(x, x^2 + 7){/tex}. Therefore, the distance between the helicopter and the soldier placed at (3,7) is given by
{tex}|(x, x^2+7)-(3, 7)|=\sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+\left(x^{2}+7-7\right)^{2}}= \sqrt{(x-3)^{2}+x^{4}}{/tex}
Let   {tex}f (x) = (x – 3)^2 + x^4{/tex}
or    {tex} f ′(x) = 2(x – 3) + 4x^3 =2(x-1)(2x^2+2x+3){/tex}
Thus, f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 1 or 2x2 + 2x + 3 = 0 for which there are no real roots. Also, there are no end points of the interval to be added to the set for which f ′ is zero, i.e., there is only one point, namely, x = 1. The value of f at this point is given by
f (1) = (1 – 3)2 + (1)4 = 5.
Thus, the distance between the solider and the helicopter is {tex}\sqrt{f(1)}=\sqrt{5}{/tex}.
Note that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is either a maximum value or a minimim value.
Since
{tex}\sqrt{f(0)}=\sqrt{(0-3)^{2}+(0)^{4}}=3>\sqrt{5}{/tex},
it follows that {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimim value of {tex}\sqrt{f(x)}{/tex}. Hence, {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} is the minimum distance between the soldier and the helicopter.

question_html: Find whether the function {tex}f(x)=\cos \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}; is increasing or decreasing in the interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{8}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{8}{/tex}.
question_text: Find whether the function {tex}f(x)=\cos \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} ; is increasing or decreasing in the interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{8}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{8}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=\cos \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} As {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{8}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{8} \Rightarrow \frac{3 \pi}{4}<2 x<\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=\cos \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} As {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{8}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{8} \Rightarrow \frac{3 \pi}{4}<2 x<\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=\cos \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
As {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{8}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{8} \Rightarrow \frac{3 \pi}{4}<2 x<\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \pi<2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \left(2 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)<0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}

question_html: Find the interval in which the function f given by f(x) = sin 2x + cos 2x, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} is strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the interval in which the function f given by f(x) = sin 2x + cos 2x, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} is strictly decreasing.
explanation: f(x) = sin 2x + cos 2x f'(x) = 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x f′(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}{/tex} Possible interval are {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{5 \pi}{8}, 2 \pi\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right) \Rightarrow f(x){/tex} is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution: f(x) = sin 2x + cos 2x f'(x) = 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x f′(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}{/tex} Possible interval are {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{5 \pi}{8}, 2 \pi\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right) \Rightarrow f(x){/tex} is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
f(x) = sin 2x + cos 2x
f'(x) = 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x
f′(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 cos 2x - 2 sin 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}{/tex}
Possible interval are {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right),\left(\frac{5 \pi}{8}, 2 \pi\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right) \Rightarrow f(x){/tex} is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5 \pi}{8}\right){/tex}

question_html: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the cost of the material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width.
question_text: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the cost of the material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width.
explanation: Let the length, width and height of the open tank be x, x and y units respectively. Then, its volume is x 2 y and the total surface area is x 2 + 4xy. It is given that tank can hold a given quantity of water. This means that its volume is constant. Let it be V. Then, V = x 2 y ...(i) The cost of the material will be least if the total surface area is least. Let S denote the total surface area. Then, S = x 2 + 4xy ...(ii) We have to minimize S subject to the condition that the volume V is constant. Now, S = x 2 + 4xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = x 2 + {tex}\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 V}{x^{3}}{/tex} The critical numbers of S are given by {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0. Now, {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 3 = 4v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 3 = 4x 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2y Clearly, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 v}{x^{3}}{/tex} > 0 for all x. Hence, S is minimum when x = 2y i.e. The depth (height) of the tank is half of its width.
solution: Let the length, width and height of the open tank be x, x and y units respectively. Then, its volume is x 2 y and the total surface area is x 2 + 4xy. It is given that tank can hold a given quantity of water. This means that its volume is constant. Let it be V. Then, V = x 2 y ...(i) The cost of the material will be least if the total surface area is least. Let S denote the total surface area. Then, S = x 2 + 4xy ...(ii) We have to minimize S subject to the condition that the volume V is constant. Now, S = x 2 + 4xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = x 2 + {tex}\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 V}{x^{3}}{/tex} The critical numbers of S are given by {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0. Now, {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 3 = 4v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 3 = 4x 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2y Clearly, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 v}{x^{3}}{/tex} > 0 for all x. Hence, S is minimum when x = 2y i.e. The depth (height) of the tank is half of its width.
solution_html:
Let the length, width and height of the open tank be x, x and y units respectively.
Then, its volume is x2 y and the total surface area is x2 + 4xy.
It is given that tank can hold a given quantity of water. This means that its volume is constant. Let it be V. Then,
V = x2 y ...(i)
The cost of the material will be least if the total surface area is least. Let S denote the total surface area. Then,
S = x2 + 4xy ...(ii)
We have to minimize S subject to the condition that the volume V is constant.

Now,
S = x2 + 4xy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = x2 + {tex}\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 V}{x^{3}}{/tex}
The critical numbers of S are given by {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0.
Now, {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}{/tex} = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x {tex}-\frac{4 V}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x3  = 4v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x3 = 4x2y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2y
Clearly, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\frac{8 v}{x^{3}}{/tex} > 0 for all x.
Hence, S is minimum when x = 2y i.e. The depth (height) of the tank is half of its width.

question_html: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
question_text: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
explanation: Let 1 st length = x 2 nd length = 28 - x Now circumference of circle is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} ATQ A = area of circle + area of square {tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} positive, hence minimum Therefore, 1 st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 2 nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} {tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
solution: Let 1 st length = x 2 nd length = 28 - x Now circumference of circle is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} ATQ A = area of circle + area of square {tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} positive, hence minimum Therefore, 1 st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 2 nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} {tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let 1st length = x
2nd length = 28 - x

Now circumference of circle is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
ATQ
A = area of circle + area of square
{tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
So,{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} 
positive, hence minimum
Therefore, 1st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
2nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}

question_html: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by a semicircular opening. The total perimeter of the window is 10 m. Find the dimensions of the window to admit maximum light through the whole opening.
question_text: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by a semicircular opening. The total perimeter of the window is 10 m. Find the dimensions of the window to admit maximum light through the whole opening.
explanation: Let P be the perimeter of window {tex}P = 2x + 2r + \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}10 =2x + 2r + \pi r{/tex} [P = 10] {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} Let A be area of window {tex}A = 2rx + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 2r\left[ {\frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}} \right] + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \pi {r^2} + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 10 - 4r - \pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} = - \left( {\pi + 4} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} < 0{/tex} maximum {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} Length of rectangle = 2r {tex} = \frac{{20}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} width {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex}
solution: Let P be the perimeter of window {tex}P = 2x + 2r + \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r{/tex} {tex}10 =2x + 2r + \pi r{/tex} [P = 10] {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} Let A be area of window {tex}A = 2rx + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 2r\left[ {\frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}} \right] + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \pi {r^2} + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 10 - 4r - \pi r{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} = - \left( {\pi + 4} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} < 0{/tex} maximum {tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} Length of rectangle = 2r {tex} = \frac{{20}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} width {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let P be the perimeter of window
{tex}P = 2x + 2r + \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r{/tex} 
{tex}10 =2x + 2r + \pi r{/tex} [P = 10]
{tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} 
Let A be area of window
{tex}A = 2rx + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 2r\left[ {\frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}} \right] + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \pi {r^2} + \frac{1}{2}\pi {r^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = 10r - 2{r^2} - \frac{{\pi {r^2}}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 10 - 4r - \pi r{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} = - \left( {\pi + 4} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}r = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{r^2}}} < 0{/tex} maximum
{tex}x = \frac{{10 - 2r - \pi r}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}x = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
Length of rectangle = 2r {tex} = \frac{{20}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex} 
width {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{\pi + 4}}{/tex}

question_html: A particle moves along the curve 6y = x3 + 2. Find the points on the curve at which y-coordinates is changing 2 times as fast as x - coordinates.
question_text: A particle moves along the curve 6y = x 3 + 2. Find the points on the curve at which y-coordinates is changing 2 times as fast as x - coordinates.
explanation: Given curve is, 6y = x 3 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (i) Given: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (ii) from (i) and (ii), {tex}2\left(2 \frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} = {tex}x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm 2{/tex} when x = 2, y = {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} ; when x = –2, y = -1 Therefore, Points are {tex}\left(2, \frac{5}{3}\right){/tex} and (-2, -1)
solution: Given curve is, 6y = x 3 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (i) Given: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (ii) from (i) and (ii), {tex}2\left(2 \frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} = {tex}x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm 2{/tex} when x = 2, y = {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} ; when x = –2, y = -1 Therefore, Points are {tex}\left(2, \frac{5}{3}\right){/tex} and (-2, -1)
solution_html:
Given curve is,

6y = x3 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (i)
Given: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex}={tex}2 \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} ... (ii)
from (i) and (ii), {tex}2\left(2 \frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex}={tex}x^{2} \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm 2{/tex}
when x = 2, y = {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex}; when x = –2, y = -1
Therefore, Points are {tex}\left(2, \frac{5}{3}\right){/tex} and (-2, -1)

question_html:
The total cost C(x) in Rupees, associated with the production of x units of an item is given by C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000.
Find the marginal cost when 3 units are produced, where by marginal cost we mean the instantaneous rate of change of total cost at any level of output.
question_text: The total cost C(x) in Rupees, associated with the production of x units of an item is given by C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000. Find the marginal cost when 3 units are produced, where by marginal cost we mean the instantaneous rate of change of total cost at any level of output.
explanation: We have, C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000 The marginal cost, MC(x) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} C(x) Now, {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000) = 0.005 {tex}\times{/tex} 3x 2 - 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 2x + 30 + 0 = 0.015 x 2 - 0.04x + 30 Marginal cost when 3 units are produced is MC(3) {tex}=0.015(9) - 0.04(3) + 30{/tex} {tex}= 0.135 - 0.12 + 30 \\ = 30.015{/tex}
solution: We have, C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000 The marginal cost, MC(x) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} C(x) Now, {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 5000) = 0.005 {tex}\times{/tex} 3x 2 - 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 2x + 30 + 0 = 0.015 x 2 - 0.04x + 30 Marginal cost when 3 units are produced is MC(3) {tex}=0.015(9) - 0.04(3) + 30{/tex} {tex}= 0.135 - 0.12 + 30 \\ = 30.015{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000
The marginal cost, MC(x) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}C(x)
Now, {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 5000)
= 0.005 {tex}\times{/tex}3x2 - 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex}2x + 30 + 0
= 0.015 x2 - 0.04x + 30
Marginal cost when 3 units are produced is 
MC(3) {tex}=0.015(9) - 0.04(3) + 30{/tex}
{tex}= 0.135 - 0.12 + 30 \\ = 30.015{/tex}

question_html: Find the interval in function -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
question_text: Find the interval in function -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
explanation: It is given that function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x 2 - 18x - 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) If f '(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2 So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1 Further, in interval (-2, -1) f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1
solution: It is given that function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x 2 - 18x - 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) If f '(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2 So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1 Further, in interval (-2, -1) f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x2 - 18x - 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x2 + 3x + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2)
If f '(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2
So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} 
So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex}
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0
Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1
Further, in interval (-2, -1)
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1

question_html: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the cost of material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width. If the cost is to be borne by nearby settled lower income families, for whom water will be provided, what kind of value is hidden in this question?
question_text: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the cost of material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width. If the cost is to be borne by nearby settled lower income families, for whom water will be provided, what kind of value is hidden in this question?
explanation: Let x be the length of a side of square base and y be the length of vertical side. Also, let V be the given quantity of water. Diagram: We know that, Volume of cuboid = {tex}L \times B \times H{/tex} Then, {tex}V = x^2y{/tex} .........(i) Surface area, {tex}S = 4xy + x^2{/tex} {tex}= 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} [Using Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow S = \frac { 4 V } { x } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}S ^ { \prime } = \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x{/tex} .......(ii) On putting S' = 0, we get {tex}\frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = - 2 x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } = 2 V\\ \Rightarrow x = ( 2 V ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}S ^ {'' } = \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } } + 2{/tex} and {tex}S ^ { \prime \prime } \left( ( 2 V ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) = \frac { 8 V } { 2 V } + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 > 0{/tex} at x = (2V) 1/3 Hence, S is minimum when x = (2V) {tex}\frac 13{/tex} From Eq.(i), we get minimum value of y by putting {tex}V=\frac{x^3}2{/tex} , {tex}y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 2 } \right) } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, the cost of material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width. And as the cost is minimum, the lower income families can easily afford it.
solution: Let x be the length of a side of square base and y be the length of vertical side. Also, let V be the given quantity of water. Diagram: We know that, Volume of cuboid = {tex}L \times B \times H{/tex} Then, {tex}V = x^2y{/tex} .........(i) Surface area, {tex}S = 4xy + x^2{/tex} {tex}= 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} [Using Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow S = \frac { 4 V } { x } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}S ^ { \prime } = \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x{/tex} .......(ii) On putting S' = 0, we get {tex}\frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = - 2 x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } = 2 V\\ \Rightarrow x = ( 2 V ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}S ^ {'' } = \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } } + 2{/tex} and {tex}S ^ { \prime \prime } \left( ( 2 V ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) = \frac { 8 V } { 2 V } + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 > 0{/tex} at x = (2V) 1/3 Hence, S is minimum when x = (2V) {tex}\frac 13{/tex} From Eq.(i), we get minimum value of y by putting {tex}V=\frac{x^3}2{/tex} , {tex}y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 2 } \right) } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, the cost of material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width. And as the cost is minimum, the lower income families can easily afford it.
solution_html:
Let x be the length of a side of square base and y be the length of vertical side.
Also, let V be the given quantity of water.
Diagram:

We know that, Volume of cuboid = {tex}L \times B \times H{/tex}
Then, {tex}V = x^2y{/tex}.........(i)
Surface area, {tex}S = 4xy + x^2{/tex}
{tex}= 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex}[Using Eq.(i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow S = \frac { 4 V } { x } + x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}S ^ { \prime } = \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x{/tex}.......(ii)
On putting S' = 0, we get
{tex}\frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { - 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = - 2 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 3 } = 2 V\\ \Rightarrow x = ( 2 V ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex}
Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}S ^ {'' } = \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } } + 2{/tex}
and {tex}S ^ { \prime \prime } \left( ( 2 V ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) = \frac { 8 V } { 2 V } + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 > 0{/tex} at x = (2V)1/3
Hence, S is minimum when x = (2V){tex}\frac 13{/tex}
From Eq.(i), we get minimum value of y by putting {tex}V=\frac{x^3}2{/tex},
{tex}y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 2 } \right) } { x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore, the cost of material will be least when depth of the tank is half of its width.
And as the cost is minimum, the lower income families can easily afford it.

question_html: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
question_text: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
explanation: Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute. {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute ….(i) Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute ….(ii) let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t {tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) ) {tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min. (b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t {tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /min
solution: Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute. {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute ….(i) Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute ….(ii) let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t {tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) ) {tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min. (b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t {tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /min
solution_html:
Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute.

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute  ….(i)

Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute   ….(ii)

let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) )   

{tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative.             

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min.

(b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii))

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm2/min

question_html: If the sum of lengths of the hypotenuse and a side of a right angled triangle is given, show that the area of the triangle is maximum, when the angle between them is  {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }.{/tex}
question_text: If the sum of lengths of the hypotenuse and a side of a right angled triangle is given, show that the area of the triangle is maximum, when the angle between them is {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }.{/tex}
explanation: Let us consider a right-angled triangle with base = x and hypotenuse = y. Let x + y = k, where k is a constant. Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between the base and the hypotenuse. Let A be the area of the triangle, then {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times Q R \times P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^{ 2 } \left( y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } \left[ ( k - x ) ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right] } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = k - x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 2 k x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 A \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } x - 6 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 A }{/tex} Now, for maxima or minima Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow \left( k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} Differentiating (ii) w.r.t x,we get, {tex}2 \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 A \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } - 12 k x } { 4 }{/tex} .......(iii) Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - k ^ { 2 } } { 4 A } < 0{/tex} Thus, A is maximum when {tex}x = \left( \frac { k } { 3 } \right){/tex} Now, {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \left( k - \frac { k } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 2 k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x } { y } = \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { k / 3 } { 2 k / 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} Hence, the area of triangle is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
solution: Let us consider a right-angled triangle with base = x and hypotenuse = y. Let x + y = k, where k is a constant. Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between the base and the hypotenuse. Let A be the area of the triangle, then {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times Q R \times P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^{ 2 } \left( y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } \left[ ( k - x ) ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right] } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = k - x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 2 k x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 A \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } x - 6 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 A }{/tex} Now, for maxima or minima Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow \left( k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} Differentiating (ii) w.r.t x,we get, {tex}2 \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 A \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } - 12 k x } { 4 }{/tex} .......(iii) Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - k ^ { 2 } } { 4 A } < 0{/tex} Thus, A is maximum when {tex}x = \left( \frac { k } { 3 } \right){/tex} Now, {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \left( k - \frac { k } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 2 k } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x } { y } = \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { k / 3 } { 2 k / 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} Hence, the area of triangle is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let us consider a right-angled triangle with base = x and hypotenuse = y. Let x + y = k, where k is a constant. Let {tex}\theta{/tex}  be the angle between the base and the hypotenuse. Let A be the area of the triangle, then

{tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times Q R \times P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^{ 2 } \left( y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } \left[ ( k - x ) ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right] } { 4 }{/tex}{tex}[ \because y = k - x ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A ^ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 2 k x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}.....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 A \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } x - 6 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } } { 4 A }{/tex}
Now, for maxima or minima
Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow \left( k ^ { 2 } x - 3 k x ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex} 
Differentiating (ii) w.r.t x,we get,
 {tex}2 \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 A \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } - 12 k x } { 4 }{/tex}.......(iii)
Put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} and {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (iii), we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - k ^ { 2 } } { 4 A } < 0{/tex}
Thus, A is maximum when {tex}x = \left( \frac { k } { 3 } \right){/tex}
Now, {tex}x = \frac { k } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \left( k - \frac { k } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 2 k } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x } { y } = \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { k / 3 } { 2 k / 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
Hence, the area of triangle is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.

question_html: AB is the diameter of a circle and C is any point on the circle. Show that the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is maximum, when it is an isosceles triangle.
question_text: AB is the diameter of a circle and C is any point on the circle. Show that the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is maximum, when it is an isosceles triangle.
explanation: Let AC = x, BC = y and r be the radius of circle. Also, {tex}\angle C = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} angle made in semi-circle is 90°] In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, we have (AB) 2 =(AC) 2 + (BC) 2 (2r) 2 = (x) 2 + (y) 2 4r 2 = x 2 + y 2 ...(I) We know that, Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, {tex}( A ) = _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } x \cdot y{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} Let A 2 =S Then, {tex}S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } \left( 4 r ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 4 x ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2} - x ^4 \right){/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) = 0{/tex} 8r 2 x = 4x 3 8r 2 = 4x 2 x 2 = 2r 2 x = {tex}\sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get, {tex}y ^ { 2 } = 4 r ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { 2 } = 2 r ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} Here, x = y so triangle is an isosceles. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } - 12 x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 r ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} m At {tex}x = \sqrt 2 r,\frac{{{d^2}S}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2{r^2} - 3(2{r^2})=-4r^2 < 0 {/tex} Therefore, Area of the triangle is maximum when it is an isosceles triangle.
solution: Let AC = x, BC = y and r be the radius of circle. Also, {tex}\angle C = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} angle made in semi-circle is 90°] In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, we have (AB) 2 =(AC) 2 + (BC) 2 (2r) 2 = (x) 2 + (y) 2 4r 2 = x 2 + y 2 ...(I) We know that, Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, {tex}( A ) = _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } x \cdot y{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} Let A 2 =S Then, {tex}S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } \left( 4 r ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 4 x ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2} - x ^4 \right){/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) = 0{/tex} 8r 2 x = 4x 3 8r 2 = 4x 2 x 2 = 2r 2 x = {tex}\sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get, {tex}y ^ { 2 } = 4 r ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { 2 } = 2 r ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} Here, x = y so triangle is an isosceles. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } - 12 x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 r ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} m At {tex}x = \sqrt 2 r,\frac{{{d^2}S}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2{r^2} - 3(2{r^2})=-4r^2 < 0 {/tex} Therefore, Area of the triangle is maximum when it is an isosceles triangle.
solution_html:
Let AC = x, BC = y and r be the radius of circle.
Also, {tex}\angle C = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex} angle made in semi-circle is 90°]
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, we have
(AB)2 =(AC)2 + (BC)2
(2r)2 = (x)2 + (y)2
4r2 = x2 + y2   ...(I)

We know that,
Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, {tex}( A ) = _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } x \cdot y{/tex}
On squaring both sides, we get
{tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Let  A2=S
Then, {tex}S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } \left( 4 r ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}[from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 4 x ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2} - x ^4 \right){/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) = 0{/tex}
8r2x = 4x3
8r2 = 4x2
x2 = 2r2
x = {tex}\sqrt { 2 } r{/tex}
From Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}y ^ { 2 } = 4 r ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { 2 } = 2 r ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex}
Here, x = y so triangle is an isosceles.
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } x - 4 x ^ { 3 } \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 8 r ^ { 2 } - 12 x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2 r ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}m
At {tex}x = \sqrt 2 r,\frac{{{d^2}S}}{{d{x^2}}} = 2{r^2} - 3(2{r^2})=-4r^2 < 0 {/tex} 
Therefore, Area of the triangle is maximum when it is an isosceles triangle.

question_html: Show that height of the cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and semi-vertical angle {tex}\alpha{/tex}, is one-third that of the cone. Hence find the greatest volume of the cylinder.
question_text: Show that height of the cylinder of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a right circular cone of height h and semi-vertical angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} , is one-third that of the cone. Hence find the greatest volume of the cylinder.
explanation: The given right circular cone of fixed height (h) and semi-vertical angle ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) can be drawn as: Here, a cylinder of radius R and height H is inscribed in the cone. Then, {tex}\triangle{/tex} GAO = {tex}\alpha{/tex} , OG = r, OA = h, OE = R, and CE = H. We have, r = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} Now, since {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOG is similar to {tex}\triangle{/tex} CEG, we have: {tex}\frac{A O}{O G}=\frac{C E}{E G}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{r}=\frac{H}{r-R}{/tex} [EG = OG - OE = r - h] {tex}\Rightarrow H=\frac{h}{r}(r-R)=\frac{h}{h \tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} [r = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} ] Now, the volume (V) of the cylinder is given by, {tex}V=\pi R^{2} H=\frac{\pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} {tex}=\pi R^{2} h-\frac{\pi R^{3}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d R}=2 \pi R h-\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d R}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi R h=\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} ==> R = {tex}\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex} And, for {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex} , we have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d R^{2}}=2 \pi h-\frac{6 \pi}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} h - 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} h = -2 {tex}\pi{/tex} h < 0 By the second derivative test, the volume of the cylinder is the greatest when When {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha, H=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(h \tan \alpha-\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{h \tan \alpha}{3}\right)=\frac{h}{3}{/tex} Thus, the height of the cylinder is one-third the height of the cone when the volume of the cylinder is the greatest. Now, the maximum volume of the cylinder can be obtained as: {tex}\pi\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right)^{2}\left(\frac{h}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pi\left(\frac{4 h^{2}}{9} \tan ^{2} \alpha\right)\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)=\frac{4}{27} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} Hence, the given result is proved.
solution: The given right circular cone of fixed height (h) and semi-vertical angle ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) can be drawn as: Here, a cylinder of radius R and height H is inscribed in the cone. Then, {tex}\triangle{/tex} GAO = {tex}\alpha{/tex} , OG = r, OA = h, OE = R, and CE = H. We have, r = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} Now, since {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOG is similar to {tex}\triangle{/tex} CEG, we have: {tex}\frac{A O}{O G}=\frac{C E}{E G}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{r}=\frac{H}{r-R}{/tex} [EG = OG - OE = r - h] {tex}\Rightarrow H=\frac{h}{r}(r-R)=\frac{h}{h \tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} [r = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} ] Now, the volume (V) of the cylinder is given by, {tex}V=\pi R^{2} H=\frac{\pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex} {tex}=\pi R^{2} h-\frac{\pi R^{3}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d R}=2 \pi R h-\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d R}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi R h=\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex} ==> R = {tex}\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex} And, for {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex} , we have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d R^{2}}=2 \pi h-\frac{6 \pi}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} h - 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} h = -2 {tex}\pi{/tex} h < 0 By the second derivative test, the volume of the cylinder is the greatest when When {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha, H=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(h \tan \alpha-\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{h \tan \alpha}{3}\right)=\frac{h}{3}{/tex} Thus, the height of the cylinder is one-third the height of the cone when the volume of the cylinder is the greatest. Now, the maximum volume of the cylinder can be obtained as: {tex}\pi\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right)^{2}\left(\frac{h}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pi\left(\frac{4 h^{2}}{9} \tan ^{2} \alpha\right)\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)=\frac{4}{27} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} Hence, the given result is proved.
solution_html:
The given right circular cone of fixed height (h) and semi-vertical angle ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) can be drawn as:

Here, a cylinder of radius R and height H is inscribed in the cone.
Then, {tex}\triangle{/tex}GAO = {tex}\alpha{/tex}, OG = r, OA = h, OE = R, and CE = H.
We have,
r = h tan{tex}\alpha{/tex}
Now, since {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOG is similar to {tex}\triangle{/tex}CEG, we have:
{tex}\frac{A O}{O G}=\frac{C E}{E G}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{r}=\frac{H}{r-R}{/tex} [EG = OG - OE = r - h]
{tex}\Rightarrow H=\frac{h}{r}(r-R)=\frac{h}{h \tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex}  [r = h tan{tex}\alpha{/tex}]
Now, the volume (V) of the cylinder is given by,
{tex}V=\pi R^{2} H=\frac{\pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}(h \tan \alpha-R){/tex}{tex}=\pi R^{2} h-\frac{\pi R^{3}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d V}{d R}=2 \pi R h-\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d R}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi R h=\frac{3 \pi R^{2}}{\tan \alpha}{/tex}  ==> R = {tex}\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex}
And, for {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha{/tex}, we have:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d R^{2}}=2 \pi h-\frac{6 \pi}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex}  = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}h - 4{tex}\pi{/tex}h = -2{tex}\pi{/tex}h < 0
By the second derivative test, the volume of the cylinder is the greatest when
When {tex}R=\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha, H=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(h \tan \alpha-\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right){/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{\tan \alpha}\left(\frac{h \tan \alpha}{3}\right)=\frac{h}{3}{/tex}
Thus, the height of the cylinder is one-third the height of the cone when the volume of the cylinder is the greatest. Now, the maximum volume of the cylinder can be obtained as:
{tex}\pi\left(\frac{2 h}{3} \tan \alpha\right)^{2}\left(\frac{h}{3}\right){/tex}{tex}=\pi\left(\frac{4 h^{2}}{9} \tan ^{2} \alpha\right)\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)=\frac{4}{27} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex}
Hence, the given result is proved.

question_html: A metal box with a square base and vertical sides is to contain 1024 cm3. The material for the top and bottom costs ₹ 5/cm2 and the material for the sides costs ₹ 2.50/cm2. Find the least cost of the box.
question_text: A metal box with a square base and vertical sides is to contain 1024 cm 3 . The material for the top and bottom costs ₹ 5/cm 2 and the material for the sides costs ₹ 2.50/cm 2 . Find the least cost of the box.
explanation: Since, volume of the box = 1024 cm 3 Let length of the side of square base be x cm and height of the box be y cm. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the box (V) {tex}= {x^2}.y = 1024{/tex} Since, {tex}{x^2}y = 1024 \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} Let C denotes the cost of the box. {tex}\therefore C = 2{x^2} \times 5 + 4xy \times 2.50{/tex} {tex}= 10{x^2} + 10xy = 10x\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} {tex}= 10x\left( {x + \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{10x}}{{{x^2}}}\left( {{x^3} + 1024} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C = 10{x^2} + \frac{{1024}}{x}{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 20x - 10240{\left( { x} \right)^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= 20x - \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex} \frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 20x = \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 20{x^3} = 10240{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} = 512 = {8^3} \Rightarrow x = 8{/tex} Again, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}} = 20 - 10240\left( { - 2} \right).\frac{1}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} {tex}= 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 8}} = 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{512}} = 60 > 0{/tex} For x = 8, cost is minimum and the corresponding least cost of the box {tex}C\left( 8 \right) = {10.8^2} + \frac{{10240}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Least cost = ₹ 1920
solution: Since, volume of the box = 1024 cm 3 Let length of the side of square base be x cm and height of the box be y cm. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the box (V) {tex}= {x^2}.y = 1024{/tex} Since, {tex}{x^2}y = 1024 \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} Let C denotes the cost of the box. {tex}\therefore C = 2{x^2} \times 5 + 4xy \times 2.50{/tex} {tex}= 10{x^2} + 10xy = 10x\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} {tex}= 10x\left( {x + \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{10x}}{{{x^2}}}\left( {{x^3} + 1024} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C = 10{x^2} + \frac{{1024}}{x}{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 20x - 10240{\left( { x} \right)^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= 20x - \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(ii) Now, {tex} \frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 20x = \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 20{x^3} = 10240{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} = 512 = {8^3} \Rightarrow x = 8{/tex} Again, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}} = 20 - 10240\left( { - 2} \right).\frac{1}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} {tex}= 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 8}} = 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{512}} = 60 > 0{/tex} For x = 8, cost is minimum and the corresponding least cost of the box {tex}C\left( 8 \right) = {10.8^2} + \frac{{10240}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Least cost = ₹ 1920
solution_html:
Since, volume of the box = 1024 cm3 
Let length of the side of square base be x cm and height of the box be y cm.

{tex}\therefore {/tex} Volume of the box (V) {tex}= {x^2}.y = 1024{/tex} 
Since, {tex}{x^2}y = 1024 \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} 
Let C denotes the cost of the box.
{tex}\therefore C = 2{x^2} \times 5 + 4xy \times 2.50{/tex} 
{tex}= 10{x^2} + 10xy = 10x\left( {x + y} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= 10x\left( {x + \frac{{1024}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{10x}}{{{x^2}}}\left( {{x^3} + 1024} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow C = 10{x^2} + \frac{{1024}}{x}{/tex} ...(i)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 20x - 10240{\left( { x} \right)^{ - 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}= 20x - \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}...(ii)
Now, {tex} \frac{{dC}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 20x = \frac{{10240}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow 20{x^3} = 10240{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {x^3} = 512 = {8^3} \Rightarrow x = 8{/tex} 
Again, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}} = 20 - 10240\left( { - 2} \right).\frac{1}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} 
{tex}= 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} 
{tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}C}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 8}} = 20 + \frac{{20480}}{{512}} = 60 > 0{/tex} 
For x = 8, cost is minimum and the corresponding least cost of the box
{tex}C\left( 8 \right) = {10.8^2} + \frac{{10240}}{8}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Least cost = ₹ 1920

question_html: The volume of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 8 cm3/s. Find the rate at which its surface area is increasing when the radius of the sphere is 12 cm.
question_text: The volume of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 8 cm 3 /s. Find the rate at which its surface area is increasing when the radius of the sphere is 12 cm.
explanation: Let r be the radius, V be the volume and S be the surface area of sphere Then, we have {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex} = 8 cm 3 / s To find {tex}\frac { d S } { d t }{/tex} , when r = 12 cm Since, V {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi 3 r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 8 = 4 \pi \times r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { cm } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} .........(i) Now, S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) = 4 \pi \times 2 r \cdot \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}= 8 \pi r \times \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] {tex}= \frac { 16 } { r }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) _ { r = 12 } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
solution: Let r be the radius, V be the volume and S be the surface area of sphere Then, we have {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex} = 8 cm 3 / s To find {tex}\frac { d S } { d t }{/tex} , when r = 12 cm Since, V {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi 3 r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 8 = 4 \pi \times r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { cm } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} .........(i) Now, S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) = 4 \pi \times 2 r \cdot \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}= 8 \pi r \times \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] {tex}= \frac { 16 } { r }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) _ { r = 12 } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let r be the radius, V be the volume and S be the surface area of sphere
Then, we have {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex}= 8 cm3/ s
To find {tex}\frac { d S } { d t }{/tex}, when r = 12 cm
Since, V  {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi 3 r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 8 = 4 \pi \times r ^ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { cm } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} .........(i)
Now, S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) = 4 \pi \times 2 r \cdot \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}= 8 \pi r \times \frac { 2 } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}[ From Eq(i)]
{tex}= \frac { 16 } { r }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) _ { r = 12 } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}

question_html: The volume of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 3 cubic centimeter per second. Find the rate of increase of its surface area, when the radius is 2 cm.
question_text: The volume of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 3 cubic centimeter per second. Find the rate of increase of its surface area, when the radius is 2 cm.
explanation: Let r be the radius of sphere and V be its volume. Then V = {tex}\frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} ........(i) Given, {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cm 3 /s Differentiating (i) both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex}\frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( 3 r ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \frac { 4 } { 3 } ( 3 \pi r ^2 ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(ii) Now, let S be the surface area of sphere, then S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 4 \pi ( 2 r ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 8 \pi r \left( \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} [using Eq.(ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) = \frac { 6 } { r }{/tex} when r = 2, then {tex}\frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { 6 } { 2 }{/tex} = 3 cm 2 /s Therefore,the rate of inrcrease of the surface area of sphere is 3 cm {tex}^2{/tex} /s when it's radius is 2 cm. {tex}{/tex}
solution: Let r be the radius of sphere and V be its volume. Then V = {tex}\frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} ........(i) Given, {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cm 3 /s Differentiating (i) both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex}\frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( 3 r ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \frac { 4 } { 3 } ( 3 \pi r ^2 ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(ii) Now, let S be the surface area of sphere, then S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 4 \pi ( 2 r ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 8 \pi r \left( \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} [using Eq.(ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) = \frac { 6 } { r }{/tex} when r = 2, then {tex}\frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { 6 } { 2 }{/tex} = 3 cm 2 /s Therefore,the rate of inrcrease of the surface area of sphere is 3 cm {tex}^2{/tex} /s when it's radius is 2 cm. {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let r be the radius of sphere and V be its volume.
Then V = {tex}\frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex}........(i)
Given, {tex}\frac { d V } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cm3/s

Differentiating (i) both sides w.r.t x,we get,
{tex}\frac { d V } { d t } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( 3 r ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \frac { 4 } { 3 } ( 3 \pi r ^2 ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.......(ii)
Now, let S be the surface area of sphere, then
S = {tex}4 \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 4 \pi ( 2 r ) \frac { d r } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d S } { d t } = 8 \pi r \left( \frac { 3 } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}[using Eq.(ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) = \frac { 6 } { r }{/tex}
when r = 2, then
{tex}\frac { d S } { d t } = \frac { 6 } { 2 }{/tex} = 3 cm2/s

Therefore,the rate of inrcrease of the surface area of sphere is 3 cm{tex}^2{/tex}/s when it's radius is 2 cm. {tex}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the function f(x) = x3 - 3x2 + 6x - 100 is increasing on R.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) = x 3 - 3x 2 + 6x - 100 is increasing on R.
explanation: Given, f(x) = x 3 - 3x 2 + 6x -100 Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = 3x 2 - 6x + 6 = 3x 2 - 6x + 3 + 3 = 3 (x 2 - 2x + 1) + 3 = 3 (x -1) 2 + 3 >0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f' (x) > 0 This shows that function f(x) is increasing on R.
solution: Given, f(x) = x 3 - 3x 2 + 6x -100 Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = 3x 2 - 6x + 6 = 3x 2 - 6x + 3 + 3 = 3 (x 2 - 2x + 1) + 3 = 3 (x -1) 2 + 3 >0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f' (x) > 0 This shows that function f(x) is increasing on R.
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = x3 - 3x2 + 6x -100
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
f'(x) = 3x2 - 6x + 6
= 3x2 - 6x + 3 + 3
= 3 (x2 - 2x + 1) + 3
= 3 (x -1)2 + 3 >0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f' (x) > 0
This shows that function f(x) is increasing on R.

question_html: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} times the radius of the base.
question_text: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} times the radius of the base.
explanation: Let ABC be right-circular cone having radius 'r' and height 'h'. If V and S are its volume and surface area (curved) respectively, then {tex}S = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{h^{2}+r^{2}}{/tex} ..... (i) Putting the value of h in (i), we get {tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{\frac{9 V^{2}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}+r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\pi^{2} r^{2}\left(\frac{9 V^{2}+\pi^{2} r^{6}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} \because V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h \\ h=\frac{3 V}{\pi r^{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} [Maxima or Minima is same for S or S 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\frac{9 V^{2}}{r^{2}}+\pi^{2} r^{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime}=\frac{-18 V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}{/tex} ..... (ii) [Differentiating w.r.t. 'r'] Now, (S 2 )' = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-18 \frac{V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 V^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 \times \frac{1}{9} \pi^{2} r^{4} h^{2}{/tex} [Putting value of V] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 r^{2}=h^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Differentiating (ii) w.r.t. 'r', again {tex}\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}=\frac{54 V^{2}}{r^{4}}+12 \pi^{2} r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}\right]_{r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}}>0{/tex} (for any value of r) Hence, S 2 i.e., is minimum for {tex}r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} or {tex}h=\sqrt{2} r{/tex} . i.e., For least curved surface, altitude is equal to {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} times the radius of the base.
solution: Let ABC be right-circular cone having radius 'r' and height 'h'. If V and S are its volume and surface area (curved) respectively, then {tex}S = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{h^{2}+r^{2}}{/tex} ..... (i) Putting the value of h in (i), we get {tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{\frac{9 V^{2}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}+r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\pi^{2} r^{2}\left(\frac{9 V^{2}+\pi^{2} r^{6}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} \because V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h \\ h=\frac{3 V}{\pi r^{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} [Maxima or Minima is same for S or S 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\frac{9 V^{2}}{r^{2}}+\pi^{2} r^{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime}=\frac{-18 V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}{/tex} ..... (ii) [Differentiating w.r.t. 'r'] Now, (S 2 )' = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-18 \frac{V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 V^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 \times \frac{1}{9} \pi^{2} r^{4} h^{2}{/tex} [Putting value of V] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 r^{2}=h^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Differentiating (ii) w.r.t. 'r', again {tex}\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}=\frac{54 V^{2}}{r^{4}}+12 \pi^{2} r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}\right]_{r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}}>0{/tex} (for any value of r) Hence, S 2 i.e., is minimum for {tex}r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} or {tex}h=\sqrt{2} r{/tex} . i.e., For least curved surface, altitude is equal to {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} times the radius of the base.
solution_html:
Let ABC be right-circular cone having radius 'r' and height 'h'. If V and S are its volume and surface area (curved) respectively, then
{tex}S = \pi rl{/tex}
{tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{h^{2}+r^{2}}{/tex} ..... (i)
Putting the value of h in (i), we get
{tex}S=\pi r \sqrt{\frac{9 V^{2}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}+r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\pi^{2} r^{2}\left(\frac{9 V^{2}+\pi^{2} r^{6}}{\pi^{2} r^{4}}\right){/tex}

{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} \because V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h \\ h=\frac{3 V}{\pi r^{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
[Maxima or Minima is same for S or S2]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S^{2}=\frac{9 V^{2}}{r^{2}}+\pi^{2} r^{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime}=\frac{-18 V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}{/tex} ..... (ii) [Differentiating w.r.t. 'r']
Now, (S2)' = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-18 \frac{V^{2}}{r^{3}}+4 \pi^{2} r^{3}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 V^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi^{2} r^{6}=18 \times \frac{1}{9} \pi^{2} r^{4} h^{2}{/tex} [Putting value of V]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 r^{2}=h^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Differentiating (ii) w.r.t. 'r', again
{tex}\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}=\frac{54 V^{2}}{r^{4}}+12 \pi^{2} r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \quad\left(S^{2}\right)^{\prime \prime}\right]_{r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}}>0{/tex} (for any value of r)
Hence, S2 i.e., is minimum for {tex}r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} or {tex}h=\sqrt{2} r{/tex}.
i.e., For least curved surface, altitude is equal to {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} times the radius of the base.

question_html: Show that the normal at any point {tex}\theta {/tex} to the curve {tex}x = a\cos \theta + a\theta .\sin \theta ,y = a\sin \theta - a\theta .\cos \theta {/tex}is at a constant distance from origin.
question_text: Show that the normal at any point {tex}\theta {/tex} to the curve {tex}x = a\cos \theta + a\theta .\sin \theta ,y = a\sin \theta - a\theta .\cos \theta {/tex} is at a constant distance from origin.
explanation: {tex}x = a\cos \theta + a\theta .\sin \theta ,y = a\sin \theta - a\theta .\cos \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = - a\sin \theta + a\left( {\theta .\cos \theta + \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\implies \frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = a\theta .\cos \theta{/tex} Now , {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = a\cos \theta-a(-\theta sin\theta+cos\theta){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{d\theta}=a\theta sin\theta{/tex} So , {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan \theta{/tex} Slope of normal {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{{\tan \theta }}{/tex} Equation of normal {tex}y - {y_1} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}\left( {x - {x_1}} \right){/tex} {tex}y - \left( {a\sin \theta - a.\theta \cos \theta } \right) = \frac{{ - \cos \theta }}{{\sin \theta }}\left[ {x - \left( {a\cos \theta - a.\theta \sin \theta } \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = a{/tex} Length of {tex} \bot {/tex} from origin {tex}\frac{{\left| {0\cos \theta + 0\sin \theta - a} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } }} = a{/tex} Hence proved
solution: {tex}x = a\cos \theta + a\theta .\sin \theta ,y = a\sin \theta - a\theta .\cos \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = - a\sin \theta + a\left( {\theta .\cos \theta + \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\implies \frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = a\theta .\cos \theta{/tex} Now , {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = a\cos \theta-a(-\theta sin\theta+cos\theta){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{d\theta}=a\theta sin\theta{/tex} So , {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan \theta{/tex} Slope of normal {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{{\tan \theta }}{/tex} Equation of normal {tex}y - {y_1} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}\left( {x - {x_1}} \right){/tex} {tex}y - \left( {a\sin \theta - a.\theta \cos \theta } \right) = \frac{{ - \cos \theta }}{{\sin \theta }}\left[ {x - \left( {a\cos \theta - a.\theta \sin \theta } \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = a{/tex} Length of {tex} \bot {/tex} from origin {tex}\frac{{\left| {0\cos \theta + 0\sin \theta - a} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } }} = a{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
{tex}x = a\cos \theta + a\theta .\sin \theta ,y = a\sin \theta - a\theta .\cos \theta {/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = - a\sin \theta + a\left( {\theta .\cos \theta + \sin \theta } \right){/tex}

{tex}\implies \frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = a\theta .\cos \theta{/tex}

Now ,{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = a\cos \theta-a(-\theta sin\theta+cos\theta){/tex}

{tex}\frac{dy}{d\theta}=a\theta sin\theta{/tex}

So ,{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan \theta{/tex}

Slope of normal {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{{\tan \theta }}{/tex}

Equation of normal {tex}y - {y_1} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}\left( {x - {x_1}} \right){/tex}

{tex}y - \left( {a\sin \theta - a.\theta \cos \theta } \right) = \frac{{ - \cos \theta }}{{\sin \theta }}\left[ {x - \left( {a\cos \theta - a.\theta \sin \theta } \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = a{/tex}

Length of {tex} \bot {/tex} from origin

{tex}\frac{{\left| {0\cos \theta + 0\sin \theta - a} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } }} = a{/tex} 
Hence proved
