question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x3 - 3x2 - 36x + 7 is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x 2 - 36x + 7 is increasing decreasing
explanation: It is given that function f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x 2 - 36x + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x - 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x - 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the points x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into three disjoint intervals which are as, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) > 0 Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex} . It is given that function f(x) = {tex}2x^3-3x^2-36x+7{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x - 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x - 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the point x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval (-2, 3) f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) < 0 Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly decreasing in interval (-2, 3).
solution: It is given that function f(x) = 2x 3 - 3x 2 - 36x + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x - 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x - 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the points x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into three disjoint intervals which are as, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) > 0 Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex} . It is given that function f(x) = {tex}2x^3-3x^2-36x+7{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x 2 - 6x - 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x 2 - x - 6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3 So, the point x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} So, in interval (-2, 3) f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) < 0 Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly decreasing in interval (-2, 3).
solution_html:
1. It is given that function f(x) = 2x3 - 3x2 - 36x + 7
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x2 - 6x - 36
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x2 - x - 6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3)
    Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3
    So, the points x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into three disjoint intervals which are as,{tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} 

    So, in interval {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex}
    f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) > 0
    Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}(-\infty, 2),(3, \infty){/tex}.
  2. It is given that function f(x) = {tex}2x^3-3x^2-36x+7{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6x2 - 6x - 36
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x2 - x - 6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3)
    Now for f'(x) = 0, then we get,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2, 3
    So, the point x = -2 and x = 3 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty, 2),(-2,3){/tex} and {tex}(3, \infty){/tex} 

    So, in interval (-2, 3)
    f'(x) = 6(x + 2)(x - 3) < 0
    Therefore, the given function f(x) is strictly decreasing in interval (-2, 3).

question_html: A wire of length 34 m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a rectangle whose length is twice its breadth. What should be the lengths of the two pieces, so that the combined area of the square and the rectangle is minimum?
question_text: A wire of length 34 m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a rectangle whose length is twice its breadth. What should be the lengths of the two pieces, so that the combined area of the square and the rectangle is minimum?
explanation: Let the length of one piece be x m, then length of the other piece = (34 - x) m {tex}\therefore{/tex} Side of square is {tex}\frac{x}{4} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} and perimeter of rectangle {tex}2\left(1+\frac{l}{2}\right)=34-x{/tex} {tex}l=\frac{34-x}{3}{/tex} Now, Area (A) {tex}=\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)^2+2\left(\frac{34-x}{6}\right)^2{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{x}{8}-\frac{1}{9}(34-x){/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} or x = 16 also, {tex}\frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{9}>0{/tex} so, A is minimum when x = 16 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Lengths of the two pieces are 16 m and 18 m.
solution: Let the length of one piece be x m, then length of the other piece = (34 - x) m {tex}\therefore{/tex} Side of square is {tex}\frac{x}{4} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} and perimeter of rectangle {tex}2\left(1+\frac{l}{2}\right)=34-x{/tex} {tex}l=\frac{34-x}{3}{/tex} Now, Area (A) {tex}=\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)^2+2\left(\frac{34-x}{6}\right)^2{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{x}{8}-\frac{1}{9}(34-x){/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} or x = 16 also, {tex}\frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{9}>0{/tex} so, A is minimum when x = 16 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Lengths of the two pieces are 16 m and 18 m.
solution_html:
Let the length of one piece be x m, then length of the other piece = (34 - x) m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Side of square is {tex}\frac{x}{4} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} and perimeter of rectangle
{tex}2\left(1+\frac{l}{2}\right)=34-x{/tex}
{tex}l=\frac{34-x}{3}{/tex}
Now, Area (A) {tex}=\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)^2+2\left(\frac{34-x}{6}\right)^2{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{x}{8}-\frac{1}{9}(34-x){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} or x = 16
also, {tex}\frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{9}>0{/tex}
so, A is minimum when x = 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Lengths of the two pieces are 16 m and 18 m.

question_html: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum volume and of given slant height is {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \frac{1} { \sqrt { 3 }}{/tex}
question_text: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum volume and of given slant height is {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \frac{1} { \sqrt { 3 }}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone. It is clear that {tex}\theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Let r, h and l be the radius, height and the slant height of the cone, respectively. Since, slant height of the cone is given, so consider it as constant. Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, r=l sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and h=l cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Let V be the volume of the cone. Then, {tex}V = \frac { \pi } { 3 } r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi \left( l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) ( l \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi l ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get, {tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = \frac { l ^ { 3 } \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \sin ^ { 2 } \theta ( - \sin \theta ) + \cos \theta ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( { - {{\sin }^3}\theta + 2\sin \theta {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right){/tex} Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}( - 3{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta + 2{\cos ^3}\theta - 4{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( {2{{\cos }^3}\theta - 7{{\sin }^2}\theta \cos \theta } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin^ 3 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} Now, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 } \text { then } \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} Now, we have {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}(2{\cos ^3}\theta - 14{\cos ^3}\theta ){/tex} {tex} = - 4\pi {l^3}{\cos ^3}\theta < \theta ,{\text{ for }}\theta \in \left( {0 ,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} or {tex}\theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex} Hence, for given slant height, the semi-vertical angle of the cone of maximum volume is {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} .
solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone. It is clear that {tex}\theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Let r, h and l be the radius, height and the slant height of the cone, respectively. Since, slant height of the cone is given, so consider it as constant. Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, r=l sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and h=l cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Let V be the volume of the cone. Then, {tex}V = \frac { \pi } { 3 } r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi \left( l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) ( l \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi l ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get, {tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = \frac { l ^ { 3 } \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \sin ^ { 2 } \theta ( - \sin \theta ) + \cos \theta ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( { - {{\sin }^3}\theta + 2\sin \theta {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right){/tex} Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}( - 3{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta + 2{\cos ^3}\theta - 4{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( {2{{\cos }^3}\theta - 7{{\sin }^2}\theta \cos \theta } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin^ 3 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} Now, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 } \text { then } \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} Now, we have {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}(2{\cos ^3}\theta - 14{\cos ^3}\theta ){/tex} {tex} = - 4\pi {l^3}{\cos ^3}\theta < \theta ,{\text{ for }}\theta \in \left( {0 ,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} or {tex}\theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex} Hence, for given slant height, the semi-vertical angle of the cone of maximum volume is {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex}  be the semi-vertical angle of the cone.
It is clear that {tex}\theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
Let r, h and l be the radius, height and the slant height of the cone, respectively.

Since, slant height of the cone is given, so consider it as constant.
Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, r=l sin {tex}\theta{/tex}and h=l cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
Let V be the volume of the cone.
Then, {tex}V = \frac { \pi } { 3 } r ^ { 2 } h{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi \left( l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) ( l \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi l ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get,
{tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = \frac { l ^ { 3 } \pi } { 3 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \sin ^ { 2 } \theta ( - \sin \theta ) + \cos \theta ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( { - {{\sin }^3}\theta + 2\sin \theta {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right){/tex} 
Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t  {tex}\theta{/tex},we get,
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}( - 3{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta + 2{\cos ^3}\theta - 4{\sin ^2}\theta \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}\left( {2{{\cos }^3}\theta - 7{{\sin }^2}\theta \cos \theta } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d V } { d \theta } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin^ 3 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
Now, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 } \text { then } \tan ^ { 2 } \theta = 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex}
Now, we have 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = \frac{{{l^3}\pi }}{3}(2{\cos ^3}\theta - 14{\cos ^3}\theta ){/tex}
{tex} = - 4\pi {l^3}{\cos ^3}\theta < \theta ,{\text{ for }}\theta \in \left( {0 ,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V is maximum, when {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
or {tex}\theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos \theta = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex}
Hence, for given slant height, the semi-vertical angle of the cone of maximum volume is {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}.

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = sin x - cos x, {tex}0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex} is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = sin x - cos x, {tex}0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex} is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
explanation: Given function is f(x) = sin x - cos x, {tex}0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex} . On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = cos x + sin x Therefore,on putting f'(x) = 0, we get, cos x + sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = - cos x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } = - 1\Rightarrow {/tex} tanx = -1 For, {tex}x \in [ 0,2 \pi ] , \tan x = \tan \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} or, tan x = {tex}\tan \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} or tan x=tan {tex}\frac{23 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{12}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , {tex}\frac{23 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{12}{/tex} Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x) {tex}0 < x < \frac { 3\pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2}{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} = 0 + 1 = 1 +ve {tex}\frac { 3\pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 5\pi } { 6}{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } \right) = \cos \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}= \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } ={/tex} {tex}\frac { - \sqrt { 3 } + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} -ve {tex}\frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } < x < { 2 \pi}{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac {23 \pi }{ 12 }{/tex} {tex}f \left( \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } \right) = \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } + \sin \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} {tex}= \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right) + \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex} {tex}=\cos \frac { \pi } { 12 } - \sin \frac { \pi } { 12 } > 0{/tex} +ve We know that, a function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing in an interval when f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing when f'(x) < 0. So, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}\left( 0 , \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} , {tex}\left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , 2 \pi \right){/tex} it is strictly decreasing in the interval {tex}\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} .
solution: Given function is f(x) = sin x - cos x, {tex}0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex} . On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = cos x + sin x Therefore,on putting f'(x) = 0, we get, cos x + sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = - cos x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } = - 1\Rightarrow {/tex} tanx = -1 For, {tex}x \in [ 0,2 \pi ] , \tan x = \tan \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} or, tan x = {tex}\tan \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} or tan x=tan {tex}\frac{23 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{12}{/tex} {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , {tex}\frac{23 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{12}{/tex} Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x) {tex}0 < x < \frac { 3\pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2}{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} = 0 + 1 = 1 +ve {tex}\frac { 3\pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 5\pi } { 6}{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } \right) = \cos \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}= \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } ={/tex} {tex}\frac { - \sqrt { 3 } + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} -ve {tex}\frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } < x < { 2 \pi}{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac {23 \pi }{ 12 }{/tex} {tex}f \left( \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } \right) = \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } + \sin \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} {tex}= \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right) + \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex} {tex}=\cos \frac { \pi } { 12 } - \sin \frac { \pi } { 12 } > 0{/tex} +ve We know that, a function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing in an interval when f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing when f'(x) < 0. So, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}\left( 0 , \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} , {tex}\left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , 2 \pi \right){/tex} it is strictly decreasing in the interval {tex}\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
Given function is f(x) = sin x - cos x, {tex}0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex}.
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = cos x + sin x
Therefore,on putting f'(x) = 0, we get,
cos x + sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = - cos x{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } = - 1\Rightarrow {/tex}tanx = -1
For, {tex}x \in [ 0,2 \pi ] , \tan x = \tan \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
or, tan x = {tex}\tan \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}

or tan x=tan {tex}\frac{23{tex}\pi{/tex}}{12}{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex},{tex}\frac{23{tex}\pi{/tex}}{12}{/tex}
Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x)
{tex}0 < x < \frac { 3\pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2}{/tex}

{tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}= 0 + 1 = 1 

 +ve
{tex}\frac { 3\pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 5\pi } { 6}{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } \right) = \cos \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { 5 \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
{tex}= \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= - \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } ={/tex}{tex}\frac { - \sqrt { 3 } + 1 } { 2 }{/tex}		 -ve
{tex}\frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } < x < { 2 \pi}{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac {23 \pi }{ 12 }{/tex} {tex}f \left( \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } \right) = \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 } + \sin \frac { 23 \pi } { 12 }{/tex}
{tex}= \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right) + \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex}{tex}=\cos \frac { \pi } { 12 } - \sin \frac { \pi } { 12 } > 0{/tex}		 +ve

We know that, a function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing in an interval when f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing when f'(x) < 0.
So, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing in intervals {tex}\left( 0 , \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}, {tex}\left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , 2 \pi \right){/tex} it is strictly decreasing in the interval {tex}\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}.

question_html: Prove that {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}\theta{/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
question_text: Prove that {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}\theta{/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex} , therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex} , therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} 

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex}, therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex}. 

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the altitude of the right circular cone of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius r is {tex}\frac{4r}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: Let R and h be the radius and height of the cone. r be the radius of sphere. {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}H{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}.\left( {r + x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because {R^2} = {r^2} - {x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {r + x} \right)\left( {0 - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - {x^2} - 2rx - 2{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 2rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 3rx + rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {r\left( {r - 3x} \right) + x\left( {r - 3x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {r - 3x} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} For critical points {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r -3x=0 \implies r=3x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{3} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {0 - 2r - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{r}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 2r - 6 \times \frac{r}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 4r} \right]{/tex} = - ve therefore, maximum Altitude = r + x {tex}= \frac{r}{3} + r{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4r}}{3}{/tex}
solution: Let R and h be the radius and height of the cone. r be the radius of sphere. {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}H{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}.\left( {r + x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because {R^2} = {r^2} - {x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {r + x} \right)\left( {0 - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - {x^2} - 2rx - 2{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 2rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 3rx + rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {r\left( {r - 3x} \right) + x\left( {r - 3x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {r - 3x} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} For critical points {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}r -3x=0 \implies r=3x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{3} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {0 - 2r - 6x} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{r}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 2r - 6 \times \frac{r}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 4r} \right]{/tex} = - ve therefore, maximum Altitude = r + x {tex}= \frac{r}{3} + r{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4r}}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let R and h be the radius and height of the cone.
r be the radius of sphere.
{tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}H{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {R^2}.\left( {r + x} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because {R^2} = {r^2} - {x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{r^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {r + x} \right)\left( {0 - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - {x^2} - 2rx - 2{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 2rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {{r^2} - 3rx + rx - 3{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {r\left( {r - 3x} \right) + x\left( {r - 3x} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {r - 3x} \right)\left( {r + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}
For critical points{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}r -3x=0 \implies r=3x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{r}{3} = x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {0 - 2r - 6x} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{r}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 2r - 6 \times \frac{r}{3}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ { - 4r} \right]{/tex} 
= - ve
therefore, maximum
Altitude = r + x
{tex}= \frac{r}{3} + r{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{4r}}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Find the equation of tangents to the curve y = cos(x + y), – 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex} that are parallel to the line x + 2y = 0.
question_text: Find the equation of tangents to the curve y = cos(x + y), – 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} that are parallel to the line x + 2y = 0.
explanation: Given that {tex}y = \cos \left( {x + y} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {x + y} \right)\,\left[ {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{/tex} Since tangent is parallel to x + 2y = 0, therefore slope of tangent {tex}= - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}- \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1{/tex} ...(ii) Since {tex}\cos \left( {x + y} \right) = y{/tex} and {tex}\sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) + {\sin ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) = {y^2} + 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {y^2} + 1{/tex} or y = 0. Therefore, cos x = 0 Therefore, {tex}x = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\pi }{2},n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2..{/tex} Thus, {tex}x = \pm \frac{\pi }{2}, \pm \frac{{3\pi }}{2},{/tex} but {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2},x = \frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2}{/tex} satisfy equation (ii) Hence, the points are {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} . Therefore, equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = 1\left( {x - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y - \pi = 0,{/tex} and equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {x + \frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y + 3\pi = 0{/tex}
solution: Given that {tex}y = \cos \left( {x + y} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {x + y} \right)\,\left[ {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{/tex} Since tangent is parallel to x + 2y = 0, therefore slope of tangent {tex}= - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}- \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1{/tex} ...(ii) Since {tex}\cos \left( {x + y} \right) = y{/tex} and {tex}\sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) + {\sin ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) = {y^2} + 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {y^2} + 1{/tex} or y = 0. Therefore, cos x = 0 Therefore, {tex}x = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\pi }{2},n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2..{/tex} Thus, {tex}x = \pm \frac{\pi }{2}, \pm \frac{{3\pi }}{2},{/tex} but {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2},x = \frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2}{/tex} satisfy equation (ii) Hence, the points are {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} . Therefore, equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = 1\left( {x - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y - \pi = 0,{/tex} and equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {x + \frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y + 3\pi = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}y = \cos \left( {x + y} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {x + y} \right)\,\left[ {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
or {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{/tex}
Since tangent is parallel to x + 2y = 0, therefore slope of tangent {tex}= - \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}- \frac{{\sin \left( {x + y} \right)}}{{1 + \sin \left( {x + y} \right)}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1{/tex} ...(ii)
Since {tex}\cos \left( {x + y} \right) = y{/tex} and {tex}\sin \left( {x + y} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) + {\sin ^2}\left( {x + y} \right) = {y^2} + 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {y^2} + 1{/tex} or y = 0.
Therefore, cos x = 0
Therefore, {tex}x = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\pi }{2},n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2..{/tex}
Thus, {tex}x = \pm \frac{\pi }{2}, \pm \frac{{3\pi }}{2},{/tex} but {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2},x = \frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2}{/tex} satisfy equation (ii)
Hence, the points are {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex}.
Therefore, equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = 1\left( {x - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y - \pi = 0,{/tex} and equation of tangent at {tex}\left( {\frac{{ - 3\pi }}{2},0} \right){/tex} is {tex}y = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {x + \frac{{3\pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} or {tex}2x + 4y + 3\pi = 0{/tex}

question_html: The sum of the surface areas of a cuboid with sides x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} and a sphere is given to be constant. Prove that the sum of their volumes is minimum, if  x is equal of three times the radius of sphere. Also, find the minimum value of the sum of their volumes.
question_text: The sum of the surface areas of a cuboid with sides x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} and a sphere is given to be constant. Prove that the sum of their volumes is minimum, if x is equal of three times the radius of sphere. Also, find the minimum value of the sum of their volumes.
explanation: Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} . {tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given] {tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} ......(i) Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} \sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=3r [using Eq. (i)] Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex} w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex} {tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex} Now, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex} Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere. Hence proved. Now, on putting {tex} r = \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}V _ { \mathrm { min } } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 } \left( \frac { x } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 81 } x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 2 \pi } { 27 } + 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 44 } { 189 } + 1 \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 233 } { 189 } \right) = \frac { 466 } { 567 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
solution: Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} . {tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given] {tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} ......(i) Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} \sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=3r [using Eq. (i)] Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex} w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex} {tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex} Now, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex} Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere. Hence proved. Now, on putting {tex} r = \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}V _ { \mathrm { min } } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 } \left( \frac { x } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 81 } x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 2 \pi } { 27 } + 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 44 } { 189 } + 1 \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 233 } { 189 } \right) = \frac { 466 } { 567 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and  {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex}.
{tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given]
{tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}......(i)
Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}....(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}\sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x=3r
[using Eq. (i)]
Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex}w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex}
Now, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex}
Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere.
Hence proved.
Now, on putting {tex} r = \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex}in Eq. (ii), we get 
{tex}V _ { \mathrm { min } } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 } \left( \frac { x } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 \pi } { 81 } x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 2 \pi } { 27 } + 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 44 } { 189 } + 1 \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 233 } { 189 } \right) = \frac { 466 } { 567 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that the least perimeter of an isosceles triangle in which a circle of radius r can be inscribed is {tex}6 \sqrt { 3 } r{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that the least perimeter of an isosceles triangle in which a circle of radius r can be inscribed is {tex}6 \sqrt { 3 } r{/tex} .
explanation: Let ABC be the given isosceles triangle, with AB = AC. Clearly, {tex}O D \perp B C , O F \perp A C , O E \perp A B{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact] and BD=BE, CD=CF, AE=AF [ {tex}\because{/tex} tangents from an external point to a circle are equal in length] Since, AD is an altitude of isosceles {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, Thus, BD=CD [ {tex}\because{/tex} in an isosceles triangle, altitude from common vertex of equal sides bisect the third side] Thus,BD = BE = CF = CD [ {tex}\because{/tex} BE=BD and CD=CF] Now, perimeter (P) of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC=AB+BC+AC =AE+BE+BD+DC+AF+FC =(AE+AF) + (BE+BD+DC+FC) =2AE+4BD............(i) Consider {tex}\Delta O E A{/tex} , we have , {tex}A E = \frac { O E } { \tan \theta } = \frac { r } { \tan \theta } \text { and } O A = \frac { r } { \sin \theta }{/tex} and in {tex}\Delta A D B{/tex} ,we have BD=AD {tex}\tan \theta{/tex} {tex}= ( A O + O D ) \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex} Now, {tex}P = 2 \frac { r } { \tan \theta } + 4 \cdot \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow P ( \theta ) = r ( 2 \cot \theta + 4 \sec \theta + 4 \tan \theta ){/tex} ....(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get, {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( - 2 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sec \theta \tan \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} .....(iii) {tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} Now, put {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 2 \left( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 2 + 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \sin \theta + 1 ) \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta = - 1 \text { or } 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 , \text { as } \theta \text { can't be more than } 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \sin \theta - 1 ) ( \sin \theta + 1 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} Therefore,On differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} we get {tex}P ^ { \prime \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( 4 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \cot \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 3 } \theta\right.{/tex} {tex}+ 4 \sec \theta \tan ^ { 2 } \theta + 8 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \tan \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) > 0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}P ( \theta ){/tex} is minimum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}P(\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6})= r \left[ 2 \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \sec \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= r \left( 2 \sqrt { 3 } + 4 \cdot \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } } + 4 \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= r \left( \frac { 6 + 8 + 4 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 18 } { \sqrt { 3 } } r = 6 \sqrt { 3 } r{/tex}
solution: Let ABC be the given isosceles triangle, with AB = AC. Clearly, {tex}O D \perp B C , O F \perp A C , O E \perp A B{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact] and BD=BE, CD=CF, AE=AF [ {tex}\because{/tex} tangents from an external point to a circle are equal in length] Since, AD is an altitude of isosceles {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, Thus, BD=CD [ {tex}\because{/tex} in an isosceles triangle, altitude from common vertex of equal sides bisect the third side] Thus,BD = BE = CF = CD [ {tex}\because{/tex} BE=BD and CD=CF] Now, perimeter (P) of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC=AB+BC+AC =AE+BE+BD+DC+AF+FC =(AE+AF) + (BE+BD+DC+FC) =2AE+4BD............(i) Consider {tex}\Delta O E A{/tex} , we have , {tex}A E = \frac { O E } { \tan \theta } = \frac { r } { \tan \theta } \text { and } O A = \frac { r } { \sin \theta }{/tex} and in {tex}\Delta A D B{/tex} ,we have BD=AD {tex}\tan \theta{/tex} {tex}= ( A O + O D ) \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex} Now, {tex}P = 2 \frac { r } { \tan \theta } + 4 \cdot \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow P ( \theta ) = r ( 2 \cot \theta + 4 \sec \theta + 4 \tan \theta ){/tex} ....(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get, {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( - 2 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sec \theta \tan \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} .....(iii) {tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} Now, put {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 2 \left( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 2 + 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \sin \theta + 1 ) \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta = - 1 \text { or } 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 , \text { as } \theta \text { can't be more than } 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \sin \theta - 1 ) ( \sin \theta + 1 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} Therefore,On differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} we get {tex}P ^ { \prime \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( 4 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \cot \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 3 } \theta\right.{/tex} {tex}+ 4 \sec \theta \tan ^ { 2 } \theta + 8 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \tan \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) > 0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}P ( \theta ){/tex} is minimum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}P(\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6})= r \left[ 2 \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \sec \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= r \left( 2 \sqrt { 3 } + 4 \cdot \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } } + 4 \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= r \left( \frac { 6 + 8 + 4 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 18 } { \sqrt { 3 } } r = 6 \sqrt { 3 } r{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABC be the given isosceles triangle, with AB = AC.

Clearly, {tex}O D \perp B C , O F \perp A C , O E \perp A B{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex}radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact]
and BD=BE, CD=CF, AE=AF
[{tex}\because{/tex}tangents from an external point to a circle are equal in length]
Since, AD is an altitude of isosceles {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC,
Thus, BD=CD
[{tex}\because{/tex}in an isosceles triangle, altitude from common vertex of equal sides bisect the third side]
Thus,BD = BE = CF = CD
[{tex}\because{/tex}BE=BD and CD=CF]
Now, perimeter (P) of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC=AB+BC+AC
=AE+BE+BD+DC+AF+FC
=(AE+AF) + (BE+BD+DC+FC)
=2AE+4BD............(i)
Consider {tex}\Delta O E A{/tex}, we have ,
{tex}A E = \frac { O E } { \tan \theta } = \frac { r } { \tan \theta } \text { and } O A = \frac { r } { \sin \theta }{/tex}
and in {tex}\Delta A D B{/tex} ,we have BD=AD {tex}\tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}= ( A O + O D ) \tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}= \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex}
Now, {tex}P = 2 \frac { r } { \tan \theta } + 4 \cdot \left( \frac { r } { \sin \theta } + r \right) \tan \theta{/tex}
[from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow P ( \theta ) = r ( 2 \cot \theta + 4 \sec \theta + 4 \tan \theta ){/tex}....(ii)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex}, we get,
{tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( - 2 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sec \theta \tan \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex}.....(iii)
{tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } + \frac { 4 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex}= r \left( \frac { - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex}
Now, put {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - 2 \left( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - 2 + 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 3 } \theta + 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \sin \theta + 1 ) \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta = - 1 \text { or } 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin \theta - 1 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 , \text { as } \theta \text { can't be more than } 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 2 \sin \theta - 1 ) ( \sin \theta + 1 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \theta \neq - 1 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
Therefore,On differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex}
we get
{tex}P ^ { \prime \prime } ( \theta ) = r \left( 4 cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \cot \theta + 4 \sec ^ { 3 } \theta\right.{/tex}{tex}+ 4 \sec \theta \tan ^ { 2 } \theta + 8 \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \tan \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) > 0{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}P ( \theta ){/tex}is minimum, when {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6})= r \left[ 2 \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \sec \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) + 4 \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) \right]{/tex}

{tex}= r \left( 2 \sqrt { 3 } + 4 \cdot \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } } + 4 \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}= r \left( \frac { 6 + 8 + 4 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 18 } { \sqrt { 3 } } r = 6 \sqrt { 3 } r{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = sin 3x - cos 3x, 0 < x < {tex} \pi{/tex}, is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = sin 3x - cos 3x, 0 < x < {tex} \pi{/tex} , is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
explanation: Given, f(x) = sin 3x - cos 3x, 0 < x < {tex} \pi{/tex} Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = 3cos 3x + 3sin 3x On putting f'(x) = 0, we get, sin 3x = - cos 3x {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} tan 3x = -1 {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} [ {tex} \because \tan \theta{/tex} is negative in Ilnd and IVth quadrants] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} Now, we find intervals and check in which intervals f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x) {tex} 0 < x < \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} = 3(0 + 1) = 3 +ve {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 3}{/tex} 3(cos {tex} \pi{/tex} + sin {tex} \pi{/tex} ) = 3(-1 + 0) = -3 -ve {tex} \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac {3 \pi } {4 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } + \sin \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = 3 \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) = {/tex} {tex} 3\sqrt2{/tex} +ve {tex}\frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 23\pi } {24 }{/tex} {tex}3 \left( \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { 23 } { 8 } \pi \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \left[ \cos \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) + \sin \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= 3 \left( - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) < 0{/tex} -ve Here, we see that f'(x)>0, for 0< x< {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { and } \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} , therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing in the intervals {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} and {tex} \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex} while f'(x)< 0 in {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \text { and } \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex} therefore, f(x) is strictly decreasing in the intervals {tex} \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \right) \text { and } \left( \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } , \pi \right){/tex}
solution: Given, f(x) = sin 3x - cos 3x, 0 < x < {tex} \pi{/tex} Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = 3cos 3x + 3sin 3x On putting f'(x) = 0, we get, sin 3x = - cos 3x {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} tan 3x = -1 {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} [ {tex} \because \tan \theta{/tex} is negative in Ilnd and IVth quadrants] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} Now, we find intervals and check in which intervals f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x) {tex} 0 < x < \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} = 3(0 + 1) = 3 +ve {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 3}{/tex} 3(cos {tex} \pi{/tex} + sin {tex} \pi{/tex} ) = 3(-1 + 0) = -3 -ve {tex} \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac {3 \pi } {4 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } + \sin \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = 3 \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) = {/tex} {tex} 3\sqrt2{/tex} +ve {tex}\frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 23\pi } {24 }{/tex} {tex}3 \left( \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { 23 } { 8 } \pi \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \left[ \cos \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) + \sin \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= 3 \left( - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) < 0{/tex} -ve Here, we see that f'(x)>0, for 0< x< {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { and } \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} , therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing in the intervals {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} and {tex} \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex} while f'(x)< 0 in {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \text { and } \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex} therefore, f(x) is strictly decreasing in the intervals {tex} \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \right) \text { and } \left( \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } , \pi \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = sin 3x - cos 3x, 0 < x < {tex} \pi{/tex}
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
f'(x) = 3cos 3x + 3sin 3x
On putting f'(x) = 0, we get,
sin 3x = - cos 3x
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} tan 3x = -1
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
 [{tex} \because \tan \theta{/tex} is negative in Ilnd and IVth quadrants]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex}
Now, we find intervals and check in which intervals f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval Test value f'(x) = 3(cos x + sin 3x) Sign of f'(x)
{tex} 0 < x < \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}= 3(0 + 1) = 3 +ve
{tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 3}{/tex} 3(cos {tex} \pi{/tex} + sin {tex} \pi{/tex}) = 3(-1 + 0) = -3 -ve
{tex} \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex} At x = {tex} \frac {3 \pi } {4 }{/tex} {tex} 3 \left( \cos \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } + \sin \frac { 9 \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}{tex} = 3 \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) = {/tex}{tex} 3\sqrt2{/tex} +ve
{tex}\frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac { 23\pi } {24 }{/tex} {tex}3 \left( \cos \frac { 23 \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { 23 } { 8 } \pi \right){/tex}
{tex}= 3 \left[ \cos \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) + \sin \left( 3 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) \right]{/tex}{tex}= 3 \left( - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 3 \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 8 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 8 } \right) < 0{/tex}		 -ve

Here, we see that f'(x)>0, for 0< x< {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { and } \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } < x < \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 }{/tex}, therefore, f(x) is strictly increasing in the intervals {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} and {tex} \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } , \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } \right){/tex}
while f'(x)< 0 in {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \text { and } \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } < x < \pi{/tex}
therefore, f(x) is strictly decreasing in the intervals {tex} \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { 7 \pi } { 12 } \right) \text { and } \left( \frac { 11 \pi } { 12 } , \pi \right){/tex}

question_html:
Water is running into a conical vessel, 15 cm deep and 5 cm in radius, at the rate of 0.1 cm3/sec. When the water is 6 cm deep, find at what rate is

  1. the water level rising?
  2. the water-surface area increasing?
  3. the wetted surface of the vessel increasing?
question_text: Water is running into a conical vessel, 15 cm deep and 5 cm in radius, at the rate of 0.1 cm 3 /sec. When the water is 6 cm deep, find at what rate is the water level rising? the water-surface area increasing? the wetted surface of the vessel increasing?
explanation: Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the water-cone VA'B' at any time t. Let VO' = h, O'A' = r and VA'= l. Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone. Then, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O A}{V O}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} . Also, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3r = h We have, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{27} h^{3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} 3r = h] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=0.1 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}(\text { Given })\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi h^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi(36)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} Thus, the water level is rising at a rate of {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} cm/sec. Let A be the water surface area at any time t. Then, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\pi \frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} When h = 6, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 6}{9} \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec Thus, the water-surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec. Let S be the wetted surface area of the vessel at any time t. Then, S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} . From fig., we have l 2 = VA' 2 = VO' 2 + O'A' 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l 2 = h 2 + r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l 2 = h 2 + {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{\pi}{9} \sqrt{10} h^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10} h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} Since h = 6 and, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10}}{9} \times 6 \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec. Thus, the wetted surface area of the vessel is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec.
solution: Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the water-cone VA'B' at any time t. Let VO' = h, O'A' = r and VA'= l. Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone. Then, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O A}{V O}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} . Also, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3r = h We have, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{27} h^{3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} 3r = h] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=0.1 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}(\text { Given })\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi h^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi(36)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} Thus, the water level is rising at a rate of {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} cm/sec. Let A be the water surface area at any time t. Then, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\pi \frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} When h = 6, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 6}{9} \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec Thus, the water-surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec. Let S be the wetted surface area of the vessel at any time t. Then, S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} . From fig., we have l 2 = VA' 2 = VO' 2 + O'A' 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l 2 = h 2 + r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l 2 = h 2 + {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{\pi}{9} \sqrt{10} h^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10} h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} Since h = 6 and, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10}}{9} \times 6 \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec. Thus, the wetted surface area of the vessel is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm 2 /sec.
solution_html:
Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the water-cone VA'B' at any time t. Let VO' = h, O'A' = r and VA'= l. Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone. Then,
tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O A}{V O}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}.
Also, tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3r = h

  1. We have,
    V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)^{2} h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{27} h^{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 3r = h]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{27} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=0.1 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}(\text { Given })\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi h^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.7}{3 \pi(36)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex}
    Thus, the water level is rising at a rate of {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} cm/sec.
  2. Let A be the water surface area at any time t. Then,
    A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\pi \frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
    When h = 6, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex}, we get 
    {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 6}{9} \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm2/sec
    Thus, the water-surface area is increasing at the rate of  {tex}\frac{1}{30}{/tex} cm2/sec.
  3. Let S be the wetted surface area of the vessel at any time t. Then, S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}.
    From fig., we have 
    l2 = VA'2 = VO'2 + O'A'2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l2 = h2 + r2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l2 = h2 + {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\pi\left(\frac{h}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{10} h}{3}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{\pi}{9} \sqrt{10} h^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10} h}{9} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
    Since h = 6 and, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex}. Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{10}}{9} \times 6 \times \frac{1}{40 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm2/sec.
    Thus, the wetted surface area of the vessel is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{\sqrt{10}}{30}{/tex} cm2/sec.

question_html: The radius r of a right circular cone is decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the height h is increasing at the rate of 2 cm/minute. When r = 9 cm and h = 6 cm, find the rate of change of its volume.
question_text: The radius r of a right circular cone is decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the height h is increasing at the rate of 2 cm/minute. When r = 9 cm and h = 6 cm, find the rate of change of its volume.
explanation: We know that Volume of right circular cone {tex}=\frac{\pi r^{2} h}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(2 r h \frac{\partial r}{\partial t}+r^{2} \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(108 \times-3+81 \times 2){/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-162)=-54 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{min}{/tex} Therefore Volume is decreasing at rate 54 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /min..
solution: We know that Volume of right circular cone {tex}=\frac{\pi r^{2} h}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(2 r h \frac{\partial r}{\partial t}+r^{2} \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(108 \times-3+81 \times 2){/tex} {tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-162)=-54 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{min}{/tex} Therefore Volume is decreasing at rate 54 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /min..
solution_html:
We know that Volume of right circular cone {tex}=\frac{\pi r^{2} h}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(2 r h \frac{\partial r}{\partial t}+r^{2} \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(108 \times-3+81 \times 2){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=\frac{\pi}{3}(-162)=-54 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Therefore Volume is decreasing at rate 54{tex}\pi{/tex}cm2/min..
