question_html: Find the maximum area of an isosceles triangle inscribed in the ellipse {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex}, with its vertex at one end of the major axis.
question_text: Find the maximum area of an isosceles triangle inscribed in the ellipse {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex} , with its vertex at one end of the major axis.
explanation: Given equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex} . Here, a = 5, b = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a > b So, major axis is along X-axis. Let {tex}\triangle{/tex} BTC be the isosceles triangle which is inscribed in the ellipse and OD = x, BC = 2y and TD = 5 - x. Let A denote the area of the triangle. Then, we have {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \text { base } \times \text { height } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times B C \times T D{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } 2 y ( 5 - x ) \Rightarrow A = y ( 5 - x ){/tex} Therefore, on squaring both sides, we get, {tex}A^2={/tex} {tex}y^2(5-x)^2.........(i){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1 - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} On putting value of y 2 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Let A 2 = Z Then, {tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left[ \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 ( 5 - x ) ( - 1 ) + ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( - 2 ) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5, \mathrm { } - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, when x = 5, then {tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( 25 - 25 ) ( 5 - 5 )^{2} = 0{/tex} Which is not possible. So, x = 5 is rejected. {tex}\therefore \quad x = - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac{d^2Z}{dx^2}= \frac{d}{{dx}}\left[ { - \frac{{32}}{{25}}{{(5 - x)}^2}(2x + 5)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{32}}{{25}}\left[ {{{(5 - x)}^2} \cdot 2 - (2x + 5) \cdot 2(5 - x)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{64}}{{25}}(5 - x)( - 3x) = \frac{{192x}}{{25}}(5 - x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2},{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}Z}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = - \frac{5}{2}}} < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z is maximum. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area A is maximum, when {tex}x = - \frac{5}{2}{/tex} and y = 12 Clearly, {tex}Z = {A^2} = \frac{{16}}{{25}}\left( {25 - \frac{{25}}{4}} \right){\left[ {5 + \frac{5}{2}} \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{16}}{{25}} \times \frac{{75}}{4} \times \frac{{225}}{4} = 3 \times 225{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The maximum area, {tex}A = \sqrt {3 \times 225} {/tex} {tex} = 15\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq units.
solution: Given equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex} . Here, a = 5, b = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a > b So, major axis is along X-axis. Let {tex}\triangle{/tex} BTC be the isosceles triangle which is inscribed in the ellipse and OD = x, BC = 2y and TD = 5 - x. Let A denote the area of the triangle. Then, we have {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \text { base } \times \text { height } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times B C \times T D{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } 2 y ( 5 - x ) \Rightarrow A = y ( 5 - x ){/tex} Therefore, on squaring both sides, we get, {tex}A^2={/tex} {tex}y^2(5-x)^2.........(i){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1 - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} On putting value of y 2 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Let A 2 = Z Then, {tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left[ \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 ( 5 - x ) ( - 1 ) + ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( - 2 ) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5, \mathrm { } - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, when x = 5, then {tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( 25 - 25 ) ( 5 - 5 )^{2} = 0{/tex} Which is not possible. So, x = 5 is rejected. {tex}\therefore \quad x = - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac{d^2Z}{dx^2}= \frac{d}{{dx}}\left[ { - \frac{{32}}{{25}}{{(5 - x)}^2}(2x + 5)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{32}}{{25}}\left[ {{{(5 - x)}^2} \cdot 2 - (2x + 5) \cdot 2(5 - x)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{64}}{{25}}(5 - x)( - 3x) = \frac{{192x}}{{25}}(5 - x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2},{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}Z}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = - \frac{5}{2}}} < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z is maximum. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area A is maximum, when {tex}x = - \frac{5}{2}{/tex} and y = 12 Clearly, {tex}Z = {A^2} = \frac{{16}}{{25}}\left( {25 - \frac{{25}}{4}} \right){\left[ {5 + \frac{5}{2}} \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{16}}{{25}} \times \frac{{75}}{4} \times \frac{{225}}{4} = 3 \times 225{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The maximum area, {tex}A = \sqrt {3 \times 225} {/tex} {tex} = 15\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
Given equation of ellipse is
{tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex}.
Here, a = 5, b = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a > b
So, major axis is along X-axis.
Let {tex}\triangle{/tex}BTC be the isosceles triangle which is inscribed in the ellipse and OD = x, BC = 2y and TD = 5 - x.

Let A denote the area of the triangle. Then, we have
{tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \text { base } \times \text { height } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times B C \times T D{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } 2 y ( 5 - x ) \Rightarrow A = y ( 5 - x ){/tex}
Therefore, on squaring both sides, we get,
{tex}A^2={/tex} {tex}y^2(5-x)^2.........(i){/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 16 } = 1 - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 25 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
On putting value of y2 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Let A2 = Z
Then, {tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = \frac { 16 } { 25 } \left[ \left( 25 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 ( 5 - x ) ( - 1 ) + ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}= \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( - 2 ) ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d Z } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 32 } { 25 } ( 5 - x ) ^ { 2 } ( 2 x + 5 ) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5, \mathrm { } - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex}
Now, when x = 5, then
{tex}Z = \frac { 16 } { 25 } ( 25 - 25 ) ( 5 - 5 )^{2} = 0{/tex}
Which is not possible.
So, x = 5 is rejected.
{tex}\therefore \quad x = - \frac { 5 } { 2 }{/tex}
Now, {tex} \frac{d^2Z}{dx^2}= \frac{d}{{dx}}\left[ { - \frac{{32}}{{25}}{{(5 - x)}^2}(2x + 5)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{32}}{{25}}\left[ {{{(5 - x)}^2} \cdot 2 - (2x + 5) \cdot 2(5 - x)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{{64}}{{25}}(5 - x)( - 3x) = \frac{{192x}}{{25}}(5 - x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} At {tex}x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2},{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}Z}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = - \frac{5}{2}}} < 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z is maximum.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area A is maximum, when {tex}x = - \frac{5}{2}{/tex} and y = 12
Clearly,
{tex}Z = {A^2} = \frac{{16}}{{25}}\left( {25 - \frac{{25}}{4}} \right){\left[ {5 + \frac{5}{2}} \right]^2}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{16}}{{25}} \times \frac{{75}}{4} \times \frac{{225}}{4} = 3 \times 225{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The maximum area, {tex}A = \sqrt {3 \times 225} {/tex} {tex} = 15\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq units.

question_html: Find the coordinates of a point on the parabola y = x2 + 7x + 2 which is closest to the straight line y = 3x - 3.
question_text: Find the coordinates of a point on the parabola y = x 2 + 7x + 2 which is closest to the straight line y = 3x - 3.
explanation: Given equation of curve is, y = x 2 + 7x + 2 and equation of straight line is y=3x - 3. Let P(x,y) be any point on the parabola y=x {tex}^2{/tex} +7x+2 Let D be the distance of point P from straight line then {tex}D = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 9 + 1 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left| 3 x - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 7 x + 2 \right) - 3 \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left| - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 5 \right) \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } + 2 \cdot 2 x + 2 ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad D = \frac { ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} .....(i) On differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 ( x + 2 ) + 0 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} For Extremum value of D, put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x+2)=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x= -2 Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 10 } } > 0{/tex} Thus, D is minimum when x= -2 Now, y = x 2 + 7x + 2= (- 2) 2 + 7 (- 2) + 2 =4 - 14 + 2 = - 8 Hence, point (-2, -8) is on the prabola, which is closet to the given straight line.
solution: Given equation of curve is, y = x 2 + 7x + 2 and equation of straight line is y=3x - 3. Let P(x,y) be any point on the parabola y=x {tex}^2{/tex} +7x+2 Let D be the distance of point P from straight line then {tex}D = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 9 + 1 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left| 3 x - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 7 x + 2 \right) - 3 \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left| - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 5 \right) \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } + 2 \cdot 2 x + 2 ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad D = \frac { ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} .....(i) On differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 ( x + 2 ) + 0 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex} For Extremum value of D, put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x+2)=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x= -2 Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 10 } } > 0{/tex} Thus, D is minimum when x= -2 Now, y = x 2 + 7x + 2= (- 2) 2 + 7 (- 2) + 2 =4 - 14 + 2 = - 8 Hence, point (-2, -8) is on the prabola, which is closet to the given straight line.
solution_html:
Given equation of curve is, y = x2 + 7x + 2 and equation of straight line is y=3x - 3.
Let P(x,y) be any point on the parabola  y=x{tex}^2{/tex}+7x+2
Let D be the distance of point P from straight line 
then
{tex}D = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 9 + 1 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { | 3 x - y - 3 | } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \left| 3 x - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 7 x + 2 \right) - 3 \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \left| - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 5 \right) \right| } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } + 2 \cdot 2 x + 2 ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad D = \frac { ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}.....(i)
On differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 ( x + 2 ) + 0 } { \sqrt { 10 } }{/tex}
For Extremum value of D, put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x+2)=0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x= -2
Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 10 } } > 0{/tex}
Thus, D is minimum when x= -2
Now, y = x2 + 7x + 2= (- 2)2 + 7 (- 2) + 2
=4 - 14 + 2 = - 8
Hence, point (-2, -8) is on the prabola, which is closet to the given straight line.

question_html: The side of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm/s. At what rate is its area increasing, when the side of the triangle is 20 cm?
question_text: The side of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm/s. At what rate is its area increasing, when the side of the triangle is 20 cm?
explanation: Let 'a' be the side of an equilateral triangle and 'A' be the area of an equilateral triangle. Then, {tex} \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex} = 2 cm/s We know that, area of an equilateral triangle, A = {tex} \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } a ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get {tex} \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 a \times \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 \times 20 \times 2{/tex} [given, a = 20] {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = 20 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} cm 2 /s Thus, the rate of area increasing is 20 {tex} \sqrt3{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution: Let 'a' be the side of an equilateral triangle and 'A' be the area of an equilateral triangle. Then, {tex} \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex} = 2 cm/s We know that, area of an equilateral triangle, A = {tex} \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } a ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get {tex} \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 a \times \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 \times 20 \times 2{/tex} [given, a = 20] {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = 20 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} cm 2 /s Thus, the rate of area increasing is 20 {tex} \sqrt3{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution_html:
Let 'a' be the side of an equilateral triangle and 'A' be the area of an equilateral triangle. Then, {tex} \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex}= 2 cm/s
We know that, area of an equilateral triangle, A = {tex} \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } a ^ { 2 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. t, we get
{tex} \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 a \times \frac { d a } { d t }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \times 2 \times 20 \times 2{/tex}[given, a = 20]
{tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d A } { d t } = 20 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} cm2/s
Thus, the rate of area increasing is 20{tex} \sqrt3{/tex} cm2/s

question_html: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value: f(x) = sinx - cosx, 0 < x < 2{tex} \pi{/tex}.
question_text: Find the local maxima and local minima of the function. Find also the local maximum and the local minimum value: f(x) = sinx - cosx, 0 < x < 2 {tex} \pi{/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex} f ( x ) = \sin x - \cos x , 0 < x < 2 \pi{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x ,we get, f'(x) = cosx + sinx..........(i) For local maximum and local minimum, Put f'(x) = 0, i.e. {tex} \cos x + \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = - \sin x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x = - 1 \Rightarrow x = \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { or } 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \text { or } \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get, f'' (x)=-sin x + cos x When {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } ,{/tex} then {tex}f''\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } < 0{/tex} When {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \text { then } f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } > 0{/tex} Thus, {tex} x = \frac { 3 { \pi } } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local maxima and {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local minima. Now, the local maximum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} and the local minimum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = - \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex} f ( x ) = \sin x - \cos x , 0 < x < 2 \pi{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x ,we get, f'(x) = cosx + sinx..........(i) For local maximum and local minimum, Put f'(x) = 0, i.e. {tex} \cos x + \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = - \sin x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x = - 1 \Rightarrow x = \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { or } 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \text { or } \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get, f'' (x)=-sin x + cos x When {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } ,{/tex} then {tex}f''\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } < 0{/tex} When {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \text { then } f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = - \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } > 0{/tex} Thus, {tex} x = \frac { 3 { \pi } } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local maxima and {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local minima. Now, the local maximum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} and the local minimum value, {tex} f \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = - \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex} f ( x ) = \sin x - \cos x , 0 < x < 2 \pi{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x ,we get,
f'(x) = cosx + sinx..........(i)
For local maximum and local minimum,
Put f'(x) = 0,
i.e. {tex} \cos x + \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow \cos x = - \sin x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x = - 1 \Rightarrow x = \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \text { or } 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \text { or } \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) 
w.r.t. x, we get,
f'' (x)=-sin x + cos x
When {tex}x = \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } ,{/tex}then
{tex}f''\left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } < 0{/tex}
When {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } , \text { then } f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = - \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) + \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } > 0{/tex}
Thus, {tex} x = \frac { 3 { \pi } } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local maxima and {tex} x = \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} is a point of local minima.
Now, the local maximum value,
{tex} f \left( \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \sin \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
and the local minimum value,
{tex} f \left( \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } \right) = \sin \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { 7 \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \sin \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - \cos \left( 2 \pi - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = - \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex} = - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = - \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: A point on the hypotenuse of a triangle is at distance a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is{tex}(a^{\frac{2}{3}} + b^{\frac{2}{3}})^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
question_text: A point on the hypotenuse of a triangle is at distance a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is {tex}(a^{\frac{2}{3}} + b^{\frac{2}{3}})^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}AP = a\cos ec\theta {/tex} {tex}BP = b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}l = AP + BP{/tex} {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = a\cos e{c^2}\theta + a\cos ec\theta {\cot ^2}\theta + {b^2} - {\sec ^3}\theta + b\sec \theta .{\tan ^2}\theta {/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{acos\theta}{sin^2\theta}=\frac{bsin\theta}{cos^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}{\tan ^3}\theta = \frac{a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{{a^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }},\cos \theta = \frac{{{b^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < 0{/tex} for {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} l is minimum and minimum value of l is given by, {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+cot^2\theta}+b\sqrt{1+tan^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+(\frac{b}{a})^{2/3}}+b\sqrt{1+(\frac{a}{b})^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}=a^{2/3}\sqrt{a^{2/3}+b^{2/3}}+b^{2/3}\sqrt{b^{2/3}+a^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}l = {\left( {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} \right)^{3/2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}AP = a\cos ec\theta {/tex} {tex}BP = b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}l = AP + BP{/tex} {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = a\cos e{c^2}\theta + a\cos ec\theta {\cot ^2}\theta + {b^2} - {\sec ^3}\theta + b\sec \theta .{\tan ^2}\theta {/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} {tex}\frac{acos\theta}{sin^2\theta}=\frac{bsin\theta}{cos^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}{\tan ^3}\theta = \frac{a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{{a^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }},\cos \theta = \frac{{{b^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < 0{/tex} for {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} l is minimum and minimum value of l is given by, {tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+cot^2\theta}+b\sqrt{1+tan^2\theta}{/tex} {tex}=a\sqrt{1+(\frac{b}{a})^{2/3}}+b\sqrt{1+(\frac{a}{b})^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}=a^{2/3}\sqrt{a^{2/3}+b^{2/3}}+b^{2/3}\sqrt{b^{2/3}+a^{2/3}}{/tex} {tex}l = {\left( {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} \right)^{3/2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}AP = a\cos ec\theta {/tex} 
{tex}BP = b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}l = AP + BP{/tex} 
{tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = a\cos e{c^2}\theta + a\cos ec\theta {\cot ^2}\theta + {b^2} - {\sec ^3}\theta + b\sec \theta .{\tan ^2}\theta {/tex} 
For maximum/minimum
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dl}}{{d\theta }} = -a\cos ec\theta \cot \theta + b\sec \theta .\tan \theta {/tex} 
{tex}\frac{acos\theta}{sin^2\theta}=\frac{bsin\theta}{cos^2\theta}{/tex} 
{tex}{\tan ^3}\theta = \frac{a}{b}{/tex} 
{tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{{a^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }},\cos \theta = \frac{{{b^{1/3}}}}{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}l}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < 0{/tex} for {tex}\tan \theta = {\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^{1/3}}{/tex} 
l  is minimum and minimum value of l is given by,
{tex}l = a\cos ec\theta + b\sec \theta {/tex} 
{tex}=a\sqrt{1+cot^2\theta}+b\sqrt{1+tan^2\theta}{/tex} 
{tex}=a\sqrt{1+(\frac{b}{a})^{2/3}}+b\sqrt{1+(\frac{a}{b})^{2/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}=a^{2/3}\sqrt{a^{2/3}+b^{2/3}}+b^{2/3}\sqrt{b^{2/3}+a^{2/3}}{/tex} 
{tex}l = {\left( {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} \right)^{3/2}}{/tex}

question_html: A point on the hypotenuse of a right triangle is at distances a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is (a{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} + b{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}){tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: A point on the hypotenuse of a right triangle is at distances a and b from the sides of the triangle. Show that the minimum length of the hypotenuse is (a {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} + b {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Let P be a point on the hypotenuse AC of right-angled {tex} \Delta{/tex} ABC, Such that {tex} P L \perp A B{/tex} and PL=a and {tex} P M \perp B C{/tex} and PM=b. Let {tex} \angle A P L = \angle A C B = \theta{/tex} [say] Then, {tex} A P = a \sec \theta , P C = b \ cosec \theta{/tex} Let l be the length of the hypotenuse, then l = AP+ PC {tex} \Rightarrow l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta , 0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get, {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = a \sec \theta \tan \theta - b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex} .........(i) For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a \sec \theta \tan \theta = b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { a \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \frac { b \cos \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \left( \frac { b} { a } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^2} = a \left( \sec \theta \times \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan \theta \times \sec \theta \tan \theta \right){/tex} {tex} - b \left[ \ cosec \theta \left( - \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} + \cot \theta ( - \ cosec \theta \cot \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}= a \sec \theta \left( \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} {tex}+ b \ cosec \theta \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + \cot ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} For {tex}0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} , all trigonometric ratios are positive Also, a > 0 and b > 0 {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^ { 2 } }{/tex} is positive. Thus, l is least when {tex}\tan \theta = \left( \frac { b } { a } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Least value of, {tex}l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta{/tex} {tex}= a \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } + b \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { b ^ { 1 / 3 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) = \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \therefore \text { in } \Delta E F G , \tan \theta = \frac { b ^ { 1 / 3 } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } , \sec \theta = \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } }\right.{/tex} and {tex}\left. {\cos ec\theta = \frac{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{{{b^{1/3}}}}} \right]{/tex}
solution: Let P be a point on the hypotenuse AC of right-angled {tex} \Delta{/tex} ABC, Such that {tex} P L \perp A B{/tex} and PL=a and {tex} P M \perp B C{/tex} and PM=b. Let {tex} \angle A P L = \angle A C B = \theta{/tex} [say] Then, {tex} A P = a \sec \theta , P C = b \ cosec \theta{/tex} Let l be the length of the hypotenuse, then l = AP+ PC {tex} \Rightarrow l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta , 0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get, {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = a \sec \theta \tan \theta - b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex} .........(i) For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a \sec \theta \tan \theta = b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { a \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \frac { b \cos \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \left( \frac { b} { a } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 }{/tex} Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^2} = a \left( \sec \theta \times \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan \theta \times \sec \theta \tan \theta \right){/tex} {tex} - b \left[ \ cosec \theta \left( - \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} + \cot \theta ( - \ cosec \theta \cot \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}= a \sec \theta \left( \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} {tex}+ b \ cosec \theta \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + \cot ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} For {tex}0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} , all trigonometric ratios are positive Also, a > 0 and b > 0 {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^ { 2 } }{/tex} is positive. Thus, l is least when {tex}\tan \theta = \left( \frac { b } { a } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Least value of, {tex}l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta{/tex} {tex}= a \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } + b \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { b ^ { 1 / 3 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) = \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \therefore \text { in } \Delta E F G , \tan \theta = \frac { b ^ { 1 / 3 } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } , \sec \theta = \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } }\right.{/tex} and {tex}\left. {\cos ec\theta = \frac{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{{{b^{1/3}}}}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let P be a point on the hypotenuse AC of right-angled {tex} \Delta{/tex}ABC, Such that {tex} P L \perp A B{/tex} and  PL=a and {tex} P M \perp B C{/tex} and PM=b.
Let {tex} \angle A P L = \angle A C B = \theta{/tex}[say]
Then, {tex} A P = a \sec \theta , P C = b \ cosec \theta{/tex}
Let l be the length of the hypotenuse, then
l = AP+ PC
{tex} \Rightarrow l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta , 0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex}, we get, {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = a \sec \theta \tan \theta - b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex}.........(i)
For maxima  or minima, put {tex} \frac { d l } { d \theta } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a \sec \theta \tan \theta = b \ cosec \theta \cot \theta{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { a \sin \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \frac { b \cos \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta = \left( \frac { b} { a } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 }{/tex}

Again, on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} we get
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^2} = a \left( \sec \theta \times \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan \theta \times \sec \theta \tan \theta \right){/tex}{tex} - b \left[ \ cosec \theta \left( - \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta \right)\right.{/tex}{tex} + \cot \theta ( - \ cosec \theta \cot \theta ) ]{/tex}
{tex}= a \sec \theta \left( \sec ^ { 2 } \theta + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex}{tex}+ b \ cosec \theta \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } \theta + \cot ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex}
For {tex}0 < \theta < \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} , all trigonometric ratios are positive
Also, a > 0 and b > 0
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } l } { d \theta ^ { 2 } }{/tex}is positive.
Thus, l is least when {tex}\tan \theta = \left( \frac { b } { a } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex}

{tex}\because{/tex} Least value of, 
{tex}l = a \sec \theta + b \ cosec \theta{/tex}
{tex}= a \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } + b \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { b ^ { 1 / 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) = \left( a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \therefore \text { in } \Delta E F G , \tan \theta = \frac { b ^ { 1 / 3 } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } } , \sec \theta = \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 / 3 } + b ^ { 2 / 3 } } } { a ^ { 1 / 3 } }\right.{/tex}and {tex}\left. {\cos ec\theta = \frac{{\sqrt {{a^{2/3}} + {b^{2/3}}} }}{{{b^{1/3}}}}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = cos2 x + sin x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = cos 2 x + sin x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ]
explanation: It is given that f(x) = cos 2 x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 2 cos x (-sin x) + cos x = -2 sin x cos x + cos x Now, if {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sin x cos x = cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x (2 sin x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or cos x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{x} \in[0, \pi]{/tex} Next, evaluating the value of f at critical points {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} , (i.e. at x = 0 and {tex}x=\pi{/tex} ), we get, {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}f(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\frac{5\pi}{6})+ sin(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\pi-\frac\pi6)+sin(\pi-\frac\pi6)=cos^2\frac\pi6-sin\frac\pi6=\frac54{/tex} f(0) = cos 2 0 + sin 0 = 1 + 0 = 1 {tex}f(\pi)=\cos ^{2} \pi+\sin \pi{/tex} = (-1) 2 + 0 = 1 {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+1=1{/tex} Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f is {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and the absolute minimum value of f is 1 occurring at x = 1, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\pi{/tex} .
solution: It is given that f(x) = cos 2 x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 2 cos x (-sin x) + cos x = -2 sin x cos x + cos x Now, if {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sin x cos x = cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x (2 sin x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or cos x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{x} \in[0, \pi]{/tex} Next, evaluating the value of f at critical points {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} , (i.e. at x = 0 and {tex}x=\pi{/tex} ), we get, {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}f(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\frac{5\pi}{6})+ sin(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\pi-\frac\pi6)+sin(\pi-\frac\pi6)=cos^2\frac\pi6-sin\frac\pi6=\frac54{/tex} f(0) = cos 2 0 + sin 0 = 1 + 0 = 1 {tex}f(\pi)=\cos ^{2} \pi+\sin \pi{/tex} = (-1) 2 + 0 = 1 {tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+1=1{/tex} Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f is {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and the absolute minimum value of f is 1 occurring at x = 1, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\pi{/tex} .
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = cos2 x + sin x, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} 
{tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 2 cos x (-sin x) + cos x
= -2 sin x cos x + cos x
Now, if {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sin x cos x = cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x (2 sin x - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or cos x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6},\frac{5\pi}{6}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{x} \in[0, \pi]{/tex} 
Next, evaluating the value of f at critical points {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and at the end points of the interval {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex}, (i.e. at x = 0 and {tex}x=\pi{/tex} ), we get,
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}f(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\frac{5\pi}{6})+ sin(\frac{5\pi}{6}) = cos^2(\pi-\frac\pi6)+sin(\pi-\frac\pi6)=cos^2\frac\pi6-sin\frac\pi6=\frac54{/tex}
f(0) = cos2 0 + sin 0 = 1 + 0 = 1
{tex}f(\pi)=\cos ^{2} \pi+\sin \pi{/tex} = (-1)2 + 0 = 1
{tex}f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+1=1{/tex} 
Therefore, the absolute maximum value of f is {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} occurring at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and the absolute minimum value of f is 1 occurring at x = 1, {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\pi{/tex}.

question_html: The sum of surface areas of a sphere and a cuboid with sides {tex}\frac{x}{3}{/tex}, x and 2x, is constant. Show that the sum of their volumes is minimum if x is equal to three times the radius of sphere.
question_text: The sum of surface areas of a sphere and a cuboid with sides {tex}\frac{x}{3}{/tex} , x and 2x, is constant. Show that the sum of their volumes is minimum if x is equal to three times the radius of sphere.
explanation: Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} . Therefore,we have, {tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given] {tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} ......(i) Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have, {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} \sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, we must have {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3r [using Eq. (i)] Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex} w.r.t. x, we have, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex} {tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex} Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere.
solution: Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex} . Therefore,we have, {tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given] {tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} ......(i) Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have, {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} \sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, we must have {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3r [using Eq. (i)] Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex} w.r.t. x, we have, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex} {tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex} {tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex} Now, we have, {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex} Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere.
solution_html:
Let r be the radius of the sphere and dimensions of cuboid are x, 2x and  {tex} \frac { x } { 3 }{/tex}.

Therefore,we have,
{tex} \therefore 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \left[ \frac { x } { 3 } \times x + x \times 2 x + 2 x \times \frac { x } { 3 } \right]{/tex} =k (constant) [given]
{tex} \Rightarrow 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 6 x ^ { 2 } = k{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad r ^ { 2 } = \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \Rightarrow r = \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}......(i)
Sum of the volumes, {tex} V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } + \frac { x } { 3 } \times x \times 2 x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 4 \pi r ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}....(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have,
{tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \times \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { - 12 x } { 4 \pi } \right) + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}\sqrt [ ] { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \left( \frac { - 3 x } { \pi } \right) + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, we must have {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( - 6 x ) \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } + 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = 6 x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3r
[using Eq. (i)]
Again, on differentiating {tex} \frac { d V } { d x }{/tex}w.r.t. x, we have,
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 6 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { \frac { k - 6 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi } } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{{4\pi }}} + x\cdot\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{k - 6{x^2}}}{4}} }}\left( {\frac{{ - 12x}}{{4\pi }}} \right)} \right) + 4x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6 \left( r - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r } \right) + 4 x{/tex}
{tex}= - 6r + \frac{{9{x^2}}}{{\pi r}} + 4x{/tex}
Now, we have,{tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}r}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 3r}} = - 6r + \frac{{9 \times 9{r^2}}}{\pi } + 12r = 6r + \frac{{18r}}{\pi } > 0{/tex}
Hence, V is minimum when x is equal to three times the radius of the sphere.

question_html: A tank with a rectangular base and rectangular sides open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 3 m and volume is 75 m3. If building of tank costs Rs. 100 per square metre for the base and Rs. 50 per square metres for the sides, find the cost of least expensive tank.
question_text: A tank with a rectangular base and rectangular sides open at the top is to be constructed so that its depth is 3 m and volume is 75 m 3 . If building of tank costs Rs. 100 per square metre for the base and Rs. 50 per square metres for the sides, find the cost of least expensive tank.
explanation: The given tank is in the form of cuboid. Volume of cuboid = lbh = 75 m 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb(3) = 75 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb = 25 ...(1) Cost = 100.l.b + 50(lh + bh + lh + bh) =100(l)(b) + 100(l + b)h = 2500 + 300(l + b) Minimize 2500 + 300(l + b) Cost = 2500 + 300( {tex}\frac{25}{b}{/tex} + b) Cost is minimum when {tex}\frac{d}{db}{/tex} (Cost) = 0 {tex}0+300\left(25\left(-\frac{1}{b^{2}}\right)^{}+1\right)=0{/tex} {tex}300\left(1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}b^2=25\Rightarrow b = 5{/tex} Therefore Minimum Cost = 2500 + 300(5 + 5) = 2500 + 3000 = 5500
solution: The given tank is in the form of cuboid. Volume of cuboid = lbh = 75 m 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb(3) = 75 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb = 25 ...(1) Cost = 100.l.b + 50(lh + bh + lh + bh) =100(l)(b) + 100(l + b)h = 2500 + 300(l + b) Minimize 2500 + 300(l + b) Cost = 2500 + 300( {tex}\frac{25}{b}{/tex} + b) Cost is minimum when {tex}\frac{d}{db}{/tex} (Cost) = 0 {tex}0+300\left(25\left(-\frac{1}{b^{2}}\right)^{}+1\right)=0{/tex} {tex}300\left(1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}b^2=25\Rightarrow b = 5{/tex} Therefore Minimum Cost = 2500 + 300(5 + 5) = 2500 + 3000 = 5500
solution_html:
The given tank is in the form of cuboid.
Volume of cuboid = lbh = 75 m3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb(3) = 75
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} lb = 25    ...(1) 
Cost = 100.l.b + 50(lh + bh + lh + bh)
=100(l)(b) + 100(l + b)h
= 2500 + 300(l + b)
Minimize 2500 + 300(l + b)
Cost = 2500 + 300({tex}\frac{25}{b}{/tex}+ b)
Cost is minimum when {tex}\frac{d}{db}{/tex}(Cost) = 0
{tex}0+300\left(25\left(-\frac{1}{b^{2}}\right)^{}+1\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}300\left(1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}1-\frac{25}{b^{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}b^2=25\Rightarrow b = 5{/tex}
Therefore Minimum Cost = 2500 + 300(5 + 5) = 2500 + 3000 = 5500

question_html: Find the absolute maximum and absolute minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = sin2 x – cos x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}].
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and absolute minimum values of the function f given by f(x) = sin 2 x – cos x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ].
explanation: Given f(x) = sin 2 x – cos x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2sinx cosx + sinx = sinx(2cosx + 1) For critical points, f'(x) =0 ==> sinx (2cosx + 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sinx = 0, 1 + 2cosx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} , {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] Now, f(0) = sin 2 0 - Cos 0 = -1, f {tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{3}-\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} , f( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) = sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} - cos {tex}\pi{/tex} =1 So, absolute maximum value = 5/4 and absolute minimum value = -1.
solution: Given f(x) = sin 2 x – cos x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2sinx cosx + sinx = sinx(2cosx + 1) For critical points, f'(x) =0 ==> sinx (2cosx + 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sinx = 0, 1 + 2cosx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} , {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] Now, f(0) = sin 2 0 - Cos 0 = -1, f {tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{3}-\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} , f( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) = sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} - cos {tex}\pi{/tex} =1 So, absolute maximum value = 5/4 and absolute minimum value = -1.
solution_html:
Given f(x) = sin2 x – cos x, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2sinx cosx + sinx = sinx(2cosx + 1)
For critical points, f'(x) =0  ==> sinx (2cosx + 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sinx = 0, 1 + 2cosx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex}[0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
Now, f(0) = sin2 0 - Cos 0 = -1, f{tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{3}-\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}, f({tex}\pi{/tex}) =  sin2{tex}\pi{/tex} - cos {tex}\pi{/tex} =1
So, absolute maximum value = 5/4 and absolute minimum value = -1.

question_html: Show that a cylinder of a given volume which is open at the top has minimum total surface area, when its height is equal to the radius of its base.
question_text: Show that a cylinder of a given volume which is open at the top has minimum total surface area, when its height is equal to the radius of its base.
explanation: Let r be the radius, h be the height, V be the volume and S be the total surface area of a right circular cylinder which is open at the top . Now, given that {tex} V = \pi r ^2 h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that, total surface area S is given by {tex} S = 2 \pi r h + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} [\because{/tex} Cylinder is open at the top, therefore S= curved surface area of cylinder+area of base] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = 2 \pi r \left( \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right) + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \mathrm { put }\ h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } , \text { from Eq. } ( \mathrm { i } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 2 V } { r } + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t.r, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = - \frac { \boldsymbol { 2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 { \pi r }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 2 { V } } { r^2 } + 2 \pi r = 0 \Rightarrow V = \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \pi r ^ { 2 } h = \pi r ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = \pi r ^{ 2 } h \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = r{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { d S } { d r } \right) = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { - \boldsymbol {2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 \pi r \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 V } { r ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi{/tex} On putting r=h, we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { d ^ { 2 } S } \\ { d r ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] _ { r = h } = \frac { 4 V } { h ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi > 0 \text { as } h > 0{/tex} Then, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} Thus,S is minimum. Hence, S is minimum, when h = r, i.e. when height of cylinder is equal to radius of the base.
solution: Let r be the radius, h be the height, V be the volume and S be the total surface area of a right circular cylinder which is open at the top . Now, given that {tex} V = \pi r ^2 h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that, total surface area S is given by {tex} S = 2 \pi r h + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} [\because{/tex} Cylinder is open at the top, therefore S= curved surface area of cylinder+area of base] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = 2 \pi r \left( \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right) + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \mathrm { put }\ h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } , \text { from Eq. } ( \mathrm { i } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 2 V } { r } + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t.r, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = - \frac { \boldsymbol { 2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 { \pi r }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 2 { V } } { r^2 } + 2 \pi r = 0 \Rightarrow V = \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \pi r ^ { 2 } h = \pi r ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = \pi r ^{ 2 } h \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = r{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { d S } { d r } \right) = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { - \boldsymbol {2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 \pi r \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 V } { r ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi{/tex} On putting r=h, we get {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { d ^ { 2 } S } \\ { d r ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] _ { r = h } = \frac { 4 V } { h ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi > 0 \text { as } h > 0{/tex} Then, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} Thus,S is minimum. Hence, S is minimum, when h = r, i.e. when height of cylinder is equal to radius of the base.
solution_html:
Let r be the radius, h be the height, V be the volume and S be the total surface area of a right circular cylinder which is open at the top .

Now, given that {tex} V = \pi r ^2 h{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
We know  that, total surface area S is given by
{tex} S = 2 \pi r h + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} [\because{/tex}Cylinder is open at the top, therefore S= curved surface area of cylinder+area of base]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = 2 \pi r \left( \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } \right) + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \left[ \mathrm { put }\ h = \frac { V } { \pi r ^ { 2 } } , \text { from Eq. } ( \mathrm { i } ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac { 2 V } { r } + \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t.r, we get
{tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = - \frac { \boldsymbol { 2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 { \pi r }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 2 { V } } { r^2 } + 2 \pi r = 0 \Rightarrow V = \pi r ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \pi r ^ { 2 } h = \pi r ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = \pi r ^{ 2 } h \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = r{/tex}
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { d S } { d r } \right) = \frac { d } { d r } \left( \frac { - \boldsymbol {2V } } { r ^ { 2 } } + 2 \pi r \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 V } { r ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi{/tex}
On putting r=h, we get
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { d ^ { 2 } S } \\ { d r ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] _ { r = h } = \frac { 4 V } { h ^ { 3 } } + 2 \pi > 0 \text { as } h > 0{/tex}
Then, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex}
Thus,S is minimum.
Hence, S is minimum, when h = r, i.e. when height of cylinder is equal to radius of the base.

question_html: Find the value(s) of x for which y = [x(x - 2)]2 is an increasing function.
question_text: Find the value(s) of x for which y = [x(x - 2)] 2 is an increasing function.
explanation: Given function is y = [x(x - 2)] 2 = [x 2 - 2x] 2 . Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) {/tex} = 2(x 2 - 2x) (2x - 2) = 4x(x - 2) (x - 1) Therefore,on putting {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 0, we get, 4x(x - 2)(x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 1 and 2. Now, we find interval in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x) (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0) (-)(-)(-) -ve (0, 1) (+)(-)(-) +ve (1, 2) (+)(-)(+) -ve (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) (+)(+)(+) +ve Therefore, y is strictly increasing in ( 0, 1 ) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). Also, y is a polynomial function, so it continuous at x = 0, 1 and 2. Hence, y is increasing in [ 0, 1] {tex} \cup {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ).
solution: Given function is y = [x(x - 2)] 2 = [x 2 - 2x] 2 . Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) {/tex} = 2(x 2 - 2x) (2x - 2) = 4x(x - 2) (x - 1) Therefore,on putting {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 0, we get, 4x(x - 2)(x - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0, 1 and 2. Now, we find interval in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x) (- {tex}\infty{/tex} ,0) (-)(-)(-) -ve (0, 1) (+)(-)(-) +ve (1, 2) (+)(-)(+) -ve (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) (+)(+)(+) +ve Therefore, y is strictly increasing in ( 0, 1 ) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). Also, y is a polynomial function, so it continuous at x = 0, 1 and 2. Hence, y is increasing in [ 0, 1] {tex} \cup {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Given function is y = [x(x - 2)]2= [x2 - 2x]2.
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) {/tex}
= 2(x2 - 2x) (2x - 2)
= 4x(x - 2) (x - 1)
Therefore,on putting {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= 0, we get,
4x(x - 2)(x - 1) = 0{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 0, 1 and 2.
Now, we find interval in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x)
(-{tex}\infty{/tex},0) (-)(-)(-) -ve
(0, 1) (+)(-)(-) +ve
(1, 2) (+)(-)(+) -ve
(2,{tex}\infty{/tex}) (+)(+)(+) +ve

Therefore, y is strictly increasing in ( 0, 1 ) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).
Also, y is a polynomial function, so it continuous at x = 0, 1 and 2.
 Hence, y is increasing in [ 0, 1] {tex} \cup {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty{/tex}).

question_html: If length of three sides of a trapezium other than base are equal to 10cm, then find the area of the trapezium when it is maximum.
question_text: If length of three sides of a trapezium other than base are equal to 10cm, then find the area of the trapezium when it is maximum.
explanation: Let ABCD be the given trapezium in which AD = BC = CD = 10 cm. Draw a perpendicular DP and CQ on AB. Let AP = x cm In {tex}\Delta{/tex} APD & {tex}\Delta{/tex} BQC, {tex}\angle APD=\angle BQC{/tex} [each =90 {tex}\circ{/tex} ] AD = BC [Both 10 cm] DP = CQ [Perpendicular between parallel lines are equal in length] {tex}\therefore \quad \Delta A P D \cong \Delta B Q C{/tex} [RHS Congruency] {tex}\therefore QB=AP{/tex} [CPCT] {tex}\Rightarrow QB=x \;cm{/tex} {tex}D P = \sqrt { 10 ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad{/tex} [by Pythagoras theorem] Now, area of trapezium, {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( \text { sum of parallel sides } ) \times{height}{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 2 x + 10 + 10 ) \times \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= ( x + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .....(i) We need to find the area of trapezium when it is maximum i.e. we need to maximize area. On differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = ( x + 10 ) \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} ....(ii) For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } = 0{/tex} = -2 (x 2 + 5x - 50) =0 = -2 (x+10) (x - 5) =0 x = 5 or -10 Since, x represents distance, so it cannot be negative. Therefore, we take x = 5. On diffentiating both sides of eq.(ii) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}(-2x^2-10x+100)-(-2x^2-10x+100)\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{100-x^2})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} [by using the quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)-(-2x^2-10x+100)(\frac{-2x}{2\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)+\frac{x(-2x^2-10x+100)}{\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{{100-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{({100-x^2})\cdot (-4x-10)+{x(-2x^2-10x+100})}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-400x+4x^3-1000+10x^2-2x^3-10x^2+100x}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac { 2 x ^ { 3 } - 300 x - 1000 } { \left( 100 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} When x = 5, {tex} \frac {\mathrm d ^ { 2 } A } {\mathrm d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 ( 5 ) ^ { 3 } - 300 ( 5 ) - 1000 } { \left[ 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 250 - 1500 - 1000 } { ( 100 - 25 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = \frac { - 2250 } { 75 \sqrt { 75 } } < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} It is maximum when x = 5 Thus, area of trapezium is maximum at x = 5 and maximum area is {tex}A _ { \max } = ( 5 + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} [put x = 5 in Eq. (i)] {tex}= 15 \sqrt { 100 - 25 } = 15 \sqrt { 75 } = 75 \sqrt { 3 }\; \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Let ABCD be the given trapezium in which AD = BC = CD = 10 cm. Draw a perpendicular DP and CQ on AB. Let AP = x cm In {tex}\Delta{/tex} APD & {tex}\Delta{/tex} BQC, {tex}\angle APD=\angle BQC{/tex} [each =90 {tex}\circ{/tex} ] AD = BC [Both 10 cm] DP = CQ [Perpendicular between parallel lines are equal in length] {tex}\therefore \quad \Delta A P D \cong \Delta B Q C{/tex} [RHS Congruency] {tex}\therefore QB=AP{/tex} [CPCT] {tex}\Rightarrow QB=x \;cm{/tex} {tex}D P = \sqrt { 10 ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad{/tex} [by Pythagoras theorem] Now, area of trapezium, {tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( \text { sum of parallel sides } ) \times{height}{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 2 x + 10 + 10 ) \times \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= ( x + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .....(i) We need to find the area of trapezium when it is maximum i.e. we need to maximize area. On differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = ( x + 10 ) \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} ....(ii) For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } = 0{/tex} = -2 (x 2 + 5x - 50) =0 = -2 (x+10) (x - 5) =0 x = 5 or -10 Since, x represents distance, so it cannot be negative. Therefore, we take x = 5. On diffentiating both sides of eq.(ii) w.r.t.x, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}(-2x^2-10x+100)-(-2x^2-10x+100)\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{100-x^2})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} [by using the quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)-(-2x^2-10x+100)(\frac{-2x}{2\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)+\frac{x(-2x^2-10x+100)}{\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{{100-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{({100-x^2})\cdot (-4x-10)+{x(-2x^2-10x+100})}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-400x+4x^3-1000+10x^2-2x^3-10x^2+100x}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac { 2 x ^ { 3 } - 300 x - 1000 } { \left( 100 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} When x = 5, {tex} \frac {\mathrm d ^ { 2 } A } {\mathrm d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 ( 5 ) ^ { 3 } - 300 ( 5 ) - 1000 } { \left[ 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 250 - 1500 - 1000 } { ( 100 - 25 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = \frac { - 2250 } { 75 \sqrt { 75 } } < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} It is maximum when x = 5 Thus, area of trapezium is maximum at x = 5 and maximum area is {tex}A _ { \max } = ( 5 + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} [put x = 5 in Eq. (i)] {tex}= 15 \sqrt { 100 - 25 } = 15 \sqrt { 75 } = 75 \sqrt { 3 }\; \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the given trapezium in which AD = BC = CD = 10 cm.
Draw a perpendicular DP and CQ on AB. Let AP = x cm
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}APD & {tex}\Delta{/tex}BQC, 
{tex}\angle APD=\angle BQC{/tex}  [each =90{tex}\circ{/tex}]
AD = BC [Both 10 cm]
DP = CQ [Perpendicular between parallel lines are equal in length]
{tex}\therefore \quad \Delta A P D \cong \Delta B Q C{/tex} [RHS Congruency]
{tex}\therefore QB=AP{/tex} [CPCT]
{tex}\Rightarrow QB=x \;cm{/tex}
{tex}D P = \sqrt { 10 ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad{/tex}[by Pythagoras theorem]
Now, area of trapezium,
{tex}A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( \text { sum of parallel sides } ) \times{height}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 2 x + 10 + 10 ) \times \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= ( x + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.....(i)

We need to find the area of trapezium when it is maximum i.e. we need to maximize area.
On differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = ( x + 10 ) \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}....(ii)
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 10 x + 100 } { \sqrt { 100 - x ^ { 2 } } } = 0{/tex}
= -2 (x2 + 5x - 50) =0
= -2 (x+10) (x - 5) =0
x = 5 or -10
Since, x represents distance, so it cannot be negative.
Therefore, we take x = 5.
On diffentiating both sides of eq.(ii) w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}\frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}(-2x^2-10x+100)-(-2x^2-10x+100)\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{100-x^2})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex} [by using the quotient rule of derivative]

{tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)-(-2x^2-10x+100)(\frac{-2x}{2\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{(\sqrt{100-x^2})^2}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{\sqrt{100-x^2}\cdot (-4x-10)+\frac{x(-2x^2-10x+100)}{\sqrt{100-x^2}})}{{100-x^2}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{({100-x^2})\cdot (-4x-10)+{x(-2x^2-10x+100})}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}=\frac{-400x+4x^3-1000+10x^2-2x^3-10x^2+100x}{{(100-x^2})^\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm d^2A}{\mathrm dx^2}= \frac { 2 x ^ { 3 } - 300 x - 1000 } { \left( 100 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}

When x = 5,

 {tex} \frac {\mathrm d ^ { 2 } A } {\mathrm d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 ( 5 ) ^ { 3 } - 300 ( 5 ) - 1000 } { \left[ 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 250 - 1500 - 1000 } { ( 100 - 25 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = \frac { - 2250 } { 75 \sqrt { 75 } } < 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {/tex} It is maximum when x = 5
Thus, area of trapezium is maximum at x = 5 and maximum area is
{tex}A _ { \max } = ( 5 + 10 ) \sqrt { 100 - ( 5 ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} [put x = 5 in Eq. (i)]
{tex}= 15 \sqrt { 100 - 25 } = 15 \sqrt { 75 } = 75 \sqrt { 3 }\; \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 3x4 - 4x3 - 12x2 + 5 is

  1. strictly increasing.
  2. strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 3x 4 - 4x 3 - 12x 2 + 5 is strictly increasing. strictly decreasing.
explanation: Given function is {tex}f(x) = 3x^4 - 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 5{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}f'(x) = 12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x{/tex} For strictly increasing or strictly decreasing, put f' (x) = 0, we get {tex}12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x= 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12x(x^2 - x - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12 x[x^2 - 2x + x - 2] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12x(x + 1)(x - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 0, -1\ or\ 2{/tex} Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x) x<-1 (-)(-)(-) -ve -1<x<0 (-)(+)(-) +ve 0< x < 2 (+)(+)(-) -ve x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve A function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing, if f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0. So, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing on the intervals (-1, 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) strictly decreasing on the intervals (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , -1) and (0, 2)
solution: Given function is {tex}f(x) = 3x^4 - 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 5{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}f'(x) = 12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x{/tex} For strictly increasing or strictly decreasing, put f' (x) = 0, we get {tex}12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x= 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12x(x^2 - x - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12 x[x^2 - 2x + x - 2] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12x(x + 1)(x - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 0, -1\ or\ 2{/tex} Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x) x<-1 (-)(-)(-) -ve -1<x<0 (-)(+)(-) +ve 0< x < 2 (+)(+)(-) -ve x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve A function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing, if f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0. So, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing on the intervals (-1, 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) strictly decreasing on the intervals (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , -1) and (0, 2)
solution_html:
Given function is {tex}f(x) = 3x^4 - 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 5{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, 
{tex}f'(x) = 12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x{/tex}
For strictly increasing or strictly decreasing, put f' (x) = 0, we get
{tex}12x^3 -12x^2 - 24x= 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}12x(x^2 - x - 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12 x[x^2 - 2x + x - 2] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}12x(x + 1)(x - 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 0, -1\ or\ 2{/tex}

Interval f'(x) = 12x(x + 1)(x - 2) Sign of f'(x)
x<-1 (-)(-)(-) -ve
-1<x<0 (-)(+)(-) +ve
0< x < 2 (+)(+)(-) -ve
x>2 (+)(+)(+) +ve

A function f(x) is said to be strictly increasing, if f'(x) > 0 and it is said to be strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0. So, the given function f(x) is

  1. strictly increasing on the intervals (-1, 0) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex})
  2. strictly decreasing on the intervals (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, -1) and (0, 2)

question_html: Of all the closed right circular cylindrical cans of volume 128{tex} \pi{/tex} cm3 ,find the dimensions of the can which has minimum surface area.
question_text: Of all the closed right circular cylindrical cans of volume 128 {tex} \pi{/tex} cm 3 ,find the dimensions of the can which has minimum surface area.
explanation: Let r cm be the radius of base and h cm be the height of the cylindrical can Let its volume be V and S be its total surface area. Then, V = 128 {tex} \pi{/tex} cm 3 [given] {tex} \Rightarrow \pi r ^ { 2 } h = 128 \pi \Rightarrow h = \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(i) Now, surface area of cylindrical can, {tex} S = 2 \pi r^ 2 + 2 \pi r h{/tex} .....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \pi r \left( \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) [ \text { using } \mathrm { Eq } . ( \mathrm { i } ) ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + \frac { 256 \pi } { r }{/tex} ....(iii) Therefore, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. r, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 4 \pi r - \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(iv) For maxima, put {tex} \frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi r = \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = \frac { 256 } { 4 } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = 64{/tex} Taking cube root on both sides, we get {tex}r = ( 64 ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \Rightarrow r = 4 \mathrm { cm }{/tex} Again, on differentiating Eq.(iv) w.r.t.r, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = 4 \pi + \frac { 512 \pi } { r ^ { 3 } }{/tex} At r=4, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 512 \pi } { 64 } + 4 \pi = 8 \pi + 4 \pi = 12 \pi > 0{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} at r = 4 Therefore, the surface area is minimum, when the radius of cylinder is 4 cm. Therefore, on putting the value of r in Eq. (i), we get {tex} h = \frac { 128 } { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 128 } { 16 } = 8 \mathrm { cm }{/tex} Hence, for the minimum surface area of can, the dimensions of the cylindrical can are r = 4 cm and h = 8 cm.
solution: Let r cm be the radius of base and h cm be the height of the cylindrical can Let its volume be V and S be its total surface area. Then, V = 128 {tex} \pi{/tex} cm 3 [given] {tex} \Rightarrow \pi r ^ { 2 } h = 128 \pi \Rightarrow h = \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(i) Now, surface area of cylindrical can, {tex} S = 2 \pi r^ 2 + 2 \pi r h{/tex} .....(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \pi r \left( \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) [ \text { using } \mathrm { Eq } . ( \mathrm { i } ) ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + \frac { 256 \pi } { r }{/tex} ....(iii) Therefore, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. r, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 4 \pi r - \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(iv) For maxima, put {tex} \frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi r = \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = \frac { 256 } { 4 } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = 64{/tex} Taking cube root on both sides, we get {tex}r = ( 64 ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \Rightarrow r = 4 \mathrm { cm }{/tex} Again, on differentiating Eq.(iv) w.r.t.r, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = 4 \pi + \frac { 512 \pi } { r ^ { 3 } }{/tex} At r=4, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 512 \pi } { 64 } + 4 \pi = 8 \pi + 4 \pi = 12 \pi > 0{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} at r = 4 Therefore, the surface area is minimum, when the radius of cylinder is 4 cm. Therefore, on putting the value of r in Eq. (i), we get {tex} h = \frac { 128 } { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 128 } { 16 } = 8 \mathrm { cm }{/tex} Hence, for the minimum surface area of can, the dimensions of the cylindrical can are r = 4 cm and h = 8 cm.
solution_html:
Let r cm be the radius of base and h cm be the height of the cylindrical can Let its volume be V and S be its total surface area.

Then, V = 128{tex} \pi{/tex}cm3 [given]
{tex} \Rightarrow \pi r ^ { 2 } h = 128 \pi \Rightarrow h = \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}....(i)
Now,  surface area of cylindrical can,
{tex} S = 2 \pi r^ 2 + 2 \pi r h{/tex}.....(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + 2 \pi r \left( \frac { 128 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) [ \text { using } \mathrm { Eq } . ( \mathrm { i } ) ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow S = 2 \pi r ^ { 2 } + \frac { 256 \pi } { r }{/tex}....(iii)
Therefore, on differentiating both sides of Eq. (iii) w.r.t. r,
we get
{tex}\frac { d S } { d r } = 4 \pi r - \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}....(iv)
For maxima,  put {tex} \frac { d S } { d r } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \pi r = \frac { 256 \pi } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = \frac { 256 } { 4 } \Rightarrow r ^ { 3 } = 64{/tex}
Taking cube root on both sides, we get 
{tex}r = ( 64 ) ^ { 1 / 3 } \Rightarrow r = 4 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
Again,  on differentiating Eq.(iv) w.r.t.r, we get
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = 4 \pi + \frac { 512 \pi } { r ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
At r=4,
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 512 \pi } { 64 } + 4 \pi = 8 \pi + 4 \pi = 12 \pi > 0{/tex}
Thus, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d r ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex}at r = 4
Therefore, the surface area is minimum, when the radius of cylinder is 4 cm.
Therefore, on putting the value of r in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} h = \frac { 128 } { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 128 } { 16 } = 8 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
Hence, for the minimum surface area of can, the dimensions of the cylindrical can are r = 4 cm and h = 8 cm.
