question_html: Prove that the semi-vertical angle of the right circular cone of given volume and least curved surface area is {tex} \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }.{/tex}
question_text: Prove that the semi-vertical angle of the right circular cone of given volume and least curved surface area is {tex} \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }.{/tex}
explanation: Let r be the radius of the base, h be the height, V be the volume, S be the surface area of the cone,slant height= AC = l and {tex}\theta{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle. Then, {tex} V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 V = \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 } \quad \text { [on squaring both sides } ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } }{/tex} .....(i) and curved surface area, {tex} S = \pi r l{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = \pi r \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + h ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because l = \sqrt { h ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {S^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2})[{\text{ on squaring both sides }}]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } } + r ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 }{/tex} .......(ii) When S is least, then S 2 is also least. Now, {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(iii) For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 18 V ^ { 2 } = 4 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 }{/tex} ....(iv) Again, on differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t.r, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^ { 2 } } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 54 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 4 } } + 12 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } > 0{/tex} At {tex} r = \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 6 } , \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^2} \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) > 0{/tex} So, S 2 or S is minimum, when {tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex} On putting {tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex} in Eq. (i) we get {tex} 2 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 6 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 r ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { r } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cot \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \quad \left[ \text { from the figure, } \cot \theta = \frac { h } { r } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} Hence, the semi-vertical angle of the right circular cone of given volume and least cured surface area is {tex} \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }.{/tex}
solution: Let r be the radius of the base, h be the height, V be the volume, S be the surface area of the cone,slant height= AC = l and {tex}\theta{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle. Then, {tex} V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 V = \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 } \quad \text { [on squaring both sides } ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } }{/tex} .....(i) and curved surface area, {tex} S = \pi r l{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = \pi r \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + h ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because l = \sqrt { h ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {S^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2})[{\text{ on squaring both sides }}]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } } + r ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 }{/tex} .......(ii) When S is least, then S 2 is also least. Now, {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 }{/tex} ....(iii) For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 18 V ^ { 2 } = 4 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 }{/tex} ....(iv) Again, on differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t.r, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^ { 2 } } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 54 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 4 } } + 12 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } > 0{/tex} At {tex} r = \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 6 } , \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^2} \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) > 0{/tex} So, S 2 or S is minimum, when {tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex} On putting {tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex} in Eq. (i) we get {tex} 2 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 6 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 r ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { r } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cot \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \quad \left[ \text { from the figure, } \cot \theta = \frac { h } { r } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} Hence, the semi-vertical angle of the right circular cone of given volume and least cured surface area is {tex} \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }.{/tex}
solution_html:
Let r be the radius of the base, h be the height, V be the volume, S be the surface area of the cone,slant height= AC = l and {tex}\theta{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle.

Then, {tex} V = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 V = \pi r ^ { 2 } h{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 } \quad \text { [on squaring both sides } ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad h ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } }{/tex} .....(i)
and curved surface area, {tex} S = \pi r l{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad S = \pi r \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + h ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because l = \sqrt { h ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad {S^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2})[{\text{ on squaring both sides }}]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } } + r ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 }{/tex} .......(ii)
When S is least, then S2 is also least.
Now, {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 }{/tex}....(iii)
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d } { d r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 18 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 18 V ^ { 2 } = 4 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 9 V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 }{/tex}....(iv)
Again, on differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t.r, we get
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^ { 2 } } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 54 V ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 4 } } + 12 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } > 0{/tex}
At  {tex} r = \left( \frac { 9 V ^ { 2 } } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 6 } , \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d r ^2} \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) > 0{/tex}
So, S2 or S is minimum, when
{tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^{ 2 } r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex}
On putting {tex} V ^ { 2 } = 2 \pi ^2 r ^ { 6 } / 9{/tex} in Eq. (i) we get
{tex} 2 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 6 } = \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } h ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 r ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad h = \sqrt { 2 } r{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { r } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cot \theta = \sqrt { 2 } \quad \left[ \text { from the figure, } \cot \theta = \frac { h } { r } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \theta = \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
Hence, the semi-vertical angle of the right circular cone of

given volume and least cured surface area is {tex} \cot ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { 2 }.{/tex}

question_html: Find the point P on the curve y2=4ax, which is nearest to the point (11a, 0).
question_text: Find the point P on the curve y 2 =4ax, which is nearest to the point (11a, 0).
explanation: Let P(x,y) be any point on y 2 =4ax.....(i) Then, distance between (x,y) and (11a, 0) is given by {tex}D = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - 0 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } \quad [ \text { from Eq. } ( \text { i } ) ]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 x - 22 a + 4 a } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex} Put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow x - 9 a = 0 \Rightarrow x = 9 a{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d D } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }\right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } - ( x - 9 a ) \cdot \frac { 1 \cdot [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ax } } } { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { x = 9a } = \frac { \sqrt { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \cdot 9 a }\ - 0 } { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \times 9 a }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } } { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 40 a ^ { 2 } } } > 0{/tex} So, at (x=9a), D is minimum. Now, y 2 =36a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow y = \pm 6 a{/tex} Hence, required points are (9a,6a) and (9a,-6a).
solution: Let P(x,y) be any point on y 2 =4ax.....(i) Then, distance between (x,y) and (11a, 0) is given by {tex}D = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - 0 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } \quad [ \text { from Eq. } ( \text { i } ) ]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 x - 22 a + 4 a } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex} Put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow x - 9 a = 0 \Rightarrow x = 9 a{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d D } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }\right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } - ( x - 9 a ) \cdot \frac { 1 \cdot [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ax } } } { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { x = 9a } = \frac { \sqrt { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \cdot 9 a }\ - 0 } { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \times 9 a }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } } { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 40 a ^ { 2 } } } > 0{/tex} So, at (x=9a), D is minimum. Now, y 2 =36a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow y = \pm 6 a{/tex} Hence, required points are (9a,6a) and (9a,-6a).
solution_html:
Let P(x,y) be any point on y2=4ax.....(i)

Then, distance between (x,y) and (11a, 0) is given by
{tex}D = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - 0 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } \quad [ \text { from Eq. } ( \text { i } ) ]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { 2 x - 22 a + 4 a } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d D } { d x } = \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { \left( x - 11 a { } \right)^2 + 4 a x } }{/tex}
Put {tex}\frac { d D } { d x } = 0 \Rightarrow x - 9 a = 0 \Rightarrow x = 9 a{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d D } { d x } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x - 9 a } { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } }\right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x } - ( x - 9 a ) \cdot \frac { 1 \cdot [ 2 ( x - 11 a ) + 4 a ] } { 2 \sqrt { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ax } } } { ( x - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a x }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } D } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { x = 9a } = \frac { \sqrt { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \cdot 9 a }\ - 0 } { ( 9 a - 11 a ) ^ { 2 } + 4 a \times 9 a }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } } { 4 a ^ { 2 } + 36 a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 40 a ^ { 2 } } } > 0{/tex}
So, at (x=9a), D is minimum.
Now, y2=36a2{tex}\Rightarrow y = \pm 6 a{/tex}
Hence, required points are (9a,6a) and (9a,-6a).

question_html: The sides of an equilateral triangle are increasing at the rate of 2 cm/s. Find the rate at which the area increases when the side is 10 cm?
question_text: The sides of an equilateral triangle are increasing at the rate of 2 cm/s. Find the rate at which the area increases when the side is 10 cm?
explanation: Let the side of the triangle be a and A be its area at any instant of time t. {tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex} = 2 cm/s Area of an equilateral triangle, A = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} Differentiating both sides with respect to t, we get {tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} 2 a{/tex} {tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2a {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = {tex}\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]_{\mathrm{a}=10}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10 {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the rate at which the area increases is 10 {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution: Let the side of the triangle be a and A be its area at any instant of time t. {tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex} = 2 cm/s Area of an equilateral triangle, A = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} Differentiating both sides with respect to t, we get {tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} 2 a{/tex} {tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2a {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = {tex}\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]_{\mathrm{a}=10}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10 {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the rate at which the area increases is 10 {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cm 2 /s
solution_html:
Let the side of the triangle be a and A be its area at any instant of time t.
{tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex} = 2 cm/s 
Area of an equilateral triangle,
A = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex}
Differentiating both sides with respect to t, we get
{tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} ={tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} 2 a{/tex} {tex}\frac{da}{dt}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{dA}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2a {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = {tex}\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]_{\mathrm{a}=10}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
Thus, the rate at which the area increases is 10{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cm2/s

question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = {tex}\frac {3 } { 2 }{/tex}x4 - 4x3 - 45x2 + 51 is

  1. strictly increasing
  2. strictly decreasing
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = {tex}\frac {3 } { 2 }{/tex} x 4 - 4x 3 - 45x 2 + 51 is strictly increasing strictly decreasing
explanation: The Function f(x) is f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} x^{4}-4 x^{3}-45 x^{2}+51{/tex} To find the intervals in which the function is increasing or decreasing, we have to differentiate the function first. So, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 4 x^{3}-4 \times 3 x^{2}-45 \times 2 x{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex} Now, put f'(x) = 0 {tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex} = 0 6x( {tex}x^{2}-2 x-15)=0{/tex} 6x(x +3)(x - 5) = 0 Thus, x = -3 and x = 5 and x = 0 x= 0, -3, 5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Critical points Draw the number line and mark -3, 0 and 5 on it. And check where the function is increasing or decreasing substitute real numbers between these points {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 lies between 0 and 5, as you will see that, f'(1) < 0. So in this interval f(x) is decreasing. Similarly, you can check and find the intervals where the function is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing. Strictly increasing = (-3, 0) {tex}\cup{/tex} (5, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) Strictly decreasing = (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , -3) {tex}\cup{/tex} (0, 5)
solution: The Function f(x) is f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} x^{4}-4 x^{3}-45 x^{2}+51{/tex} To find the intervals in which the function is increasing or decreasing, we have to differentiate the function first. So, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 4 x^{3}-4 \times 3 x^{2}-45 \times 2 x{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex} Now, put f'(x) = 0 {tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex} = 0 6x( {tex}x^{2}-2 x-15)=0{/tex} 6x(x +3)(x - 5) = 0 Thus, x = -3 and x = 5 and x = 0 x= 0, -3, 5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Critical points Draw the number line and mark -3, 0 and 5 on it. And check where the function is increasing or decreasing substitute real numbers between these points {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 lies between 0 and 5, as you will see that, f'(1) < 0. So in this interval f(x) is decreasing. Similarly, you can check and find the intervals where the function is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing. Strictly increasing = (-3, 0) {tex}\cup{/tex} (5, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) Strictly decreasing = (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , -3) {tex}\cup{/tex} (0, 5)
solution_html:
The Function f(x) is
f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} x^{4}-4 x^{3}-45 x^{2}+51{/tex}
To find the intervals in which the function is increasing or decreasing, we have to differentiate the function first. So,
f'(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 4 x^{3}-4 \times 3 x^{2}-45 \times 2 x{/tex}
f'(x) = {tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex}
Now, put f'(x) = 0
{tex}6 x^{3}-12 x^{2}-90 x{/tex} = 0
6x({tex}x^{2}-2 x-15)=0{/tex}
6x(x +3)(x - 5) = 0
Thus, x = -3 and x = 5 and x = 0
x= 0, -3, 5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Critical points
Draw the number line and mark -3, 0 and 5 on it. And check where the function is increasing or decreasing substitute real numbers between these points 
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 lies between 0 and 5, as you will see that, f'(1) < 0. So in this interval f(x) is decreasing.
Similarly, you can check and find the intervals where the function is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing.
Strictly increasing = (-3, 0) {tex}\cup{/tex} (5, {tex}\infty{/tex})
Strictly decreasing = (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, -3) {tex}\cup{/tex} (0, 5)

question_html: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = x2 - x + 1 is neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on (-1, 1).
question_text: Prove that the function f given by f(x) = x 2 - x + 1 is neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on (-1, 1).
explanation: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - x + 1{/tex} f(x) = x 2 - x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x - 1 f(x) is strictly increasing if f'(x) < 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2x - 1 > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x > \frac{1}{2}{/tex} i.e., increasing on the interval {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{2},1} \right){/tex} f(x) is strictly decreasing if f'(x) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 1 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x < \frac{1}{2}{/tex} i.e., decreasing on the interval {tex}\left( { - 1,\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} hence, f(x) is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing on the interval (-1, 1).
solution: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - x + 1{/tex} f(x) = x 2 - x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x - 1 f(x) is strictly increasing if f'(x) < 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2x - 1 > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x > \frac{1}{2}{/tex} i.e., increasing on the interval {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{2},1} \right){/tex} f(x) is strictly decreasing if f'(x) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 1 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x < \frac{1}{2}{/tex} i.e., decreasing on the interval {tex}\left( { - 1,\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} hence, f(x) is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing on the interval (-1, 1).
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - x + 1{/tex}f(x) = x2 - x + 1

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x - 1

f(x) is strictly increasing if f'(x) < 0

{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}2x - 1 > 0

{tex}\Rightarrow x > \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

i.e., increasing on the interval {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{2},1} \right){/tex} 

f(x) is strictly decreasing if f'(x) < 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 1 < 0

{tex}\Rightarrow x < \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

i.e., decreasing on the interval {tex}\left( { - 1,\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} 

hence, f(x) is neither strictly increasing nor decreasing on the interval (-1, 1).

explanation: Let ABCD be the rectangle which is inscribed in a fixed circle whose centre is O and radius b. Let AB = 2x and BC = 2y. In right-angled {tex}\Delta{/tex} OPA by Pythagoras theorem, we have AP 2 + OP 2 = OA 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) Let A be the area of the rectangle. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = (2x) (2y) [ {tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( 4 x ){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { - 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot 4{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} x cannot be negative] Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4\left[ { - 4x{{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 1/2}} + \left( {{b^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right){{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 3/2}}( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { - 4 x } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { x \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} On putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } + \frac { \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } +0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 16 < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0{/tex} . Therefore, A is maximum at {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} Now, on putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = y = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow 2 x = 2 y = \sqrt { 2 } b{/tex} Thus, area of rectangle is maximum, when 2x = 2y, i.e. when rectangle is a square

2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4\left[ { - 4x{{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 1/2}} + \left( {{b^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right){{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 3/2}}( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { - 4 x } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { x \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} On putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } + \frac { \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } +0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 16 < 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0{/tex} . solution_html:
Let ABCD be the rectangle which is inscribed in a fixed circle whose centre is O and radius b. Let AB = 2x and BC = 2y.

In right-angled {tex}\Delta{/tex}OPA by Pythagoras theorem,
we have
AP2 + OP2 = OA2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i)
Let A be the area of the rectangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = (2x) (2y) [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A = 4 x \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \quad \left[ \because \quad y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( 4 x ){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 x \cdot \frac { - 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \cdot 4{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d A } { d x } = 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 4 \left( \frac { b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} x cannot be negative]
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d A } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 4 \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4\left[ { - 4x{{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 1/2}} + \left( {{b^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right){{\left( {{b^2} - {x^2}} \right)}^{ - 3/2}}( - 2x)} \right]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { - 4 x } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { x \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
On putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } + \frac { \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left( b ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) } { \left( b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \left[ \frac { \frac { - 4 b } { \sqrt { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } +0\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - 16 < 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0{/tex}. Therefore, A is maximum at {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, on putting {tex}x = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } - \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x = y = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 2 } } \Rightarrow 2 x = 2 y = \sqrt { 2 } b{/tex}
Thus, area of rectangle is maximum, when 2x = 2y, i.e. when rectangle is a square

question_html:
The amount of pollution content added in air in a city due to x diesel vehicles is given by P(x) = 0.005x3 + 0.02x2 + 30x.
Find the marginal increase in pollution content when 3 diesel vehicles are added and write which value is indicated in the above question?
question_text: The amount of pollution content added in air in a city due to x diesel vehicles is given by P(x) = 0.005x 3 + 0.02x 2 + 30x. Find the marginal increase in pollution content when 3 diesel vehicles are added and write which value is indicated in the above question?
explanation: Given, P(x) = 0.005x 3 + 0.02x 2 + 30x On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( x ) = 3 \times 0.005 x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 0.02 x + 30{/tex} On putting x = 3 we get {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( 3 ) = 3 \times 0.005 \times 9 + 2 \times 0.02 \times 3 + 30{/tex} = 0.135+ 0.12 + 30 = 30.255 Therefore, the marginal increase in pollution content when 3 diesel vehicles are added is 30.255. Pollution content in the city increases with the increase in number of diesel vehicles.
solution: Given, P(x) = 0.005x 3 + 0.02x 2 + 30x On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( x ) = 3 \times 0.005 x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 0.02 x + 30{/tex} On putting x = 3 we get {tex}P ^ { \prime } ( 3 ) = 3 \times 0.005 \times 9 + 2 \times 0.02 \times 3 + 30{/tex} = 0.135+ 0.12 + 30 = 30.255 Therefore, the marginal increase in pollution content when 3 diesel vehicles are added is 30.255. Pollution content in the city increases with the increase in number of diesel vehicles.
solution_html:
Given, P(x) = 0.005x3 + 0.02x2 + 30x
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}P ^ { \prime } ( x ) = 3 \times 0.005 x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 0.02 x + 30{/tex}
On putting x = 3 we get
{tex}P ^ { \prime } ( 3 ) = 3 \times 0.005 \times 9 + 2 \times 0.02 \times 3 + 30{/tex}
= 0.135+ 0.12 + 30
= 30.255

Therefore, the marginal increase in pollution content when 3 diesel vehicles are added is 30.255.

Pollution content in the city increases with the increase in number of diesel vehicles.

question_html:
The total cost C(x) associated with provision of free mid-day meals to x students of a school in primary classes is given by C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 50.
If the marginal cost is given by rate of change {tex}\frac { d C } { d x }{/tex} of total cost, then write the marginal cost of food for 300 students. What value is shown here?
question_text: The total cost C(x) associated with provision of free mid-day meals to x students of a school in primary classes is given by C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 50. If the marginal cost is given by rate of change {tex}\frac { d C } { d x }{/tex} of total cost, then write the marginal cost of food for 300 students. What value is shown here?
explanation: Given, C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 50 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.005 \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) - 0.02 ( 2 x ) + 30{/tex} = 0.015x 2 - 0.04x + 30 On putting x = 300, we get {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.015 ( 300 ) ^ { 2 } - 0.04 ( 300 ) + 30{/tex} = 1350 - 12 + 30 = 1368 Therefore,the marginal cost of food for 300 students is Rs 1368. By providing free mid-day meals to the students of primary classes, care and concern is shown towards their health and nutritional status.
solution: Given, C(x) = 0.005x 3 - 0.02x 2 + 30x + 50 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.005 \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) - 0.02 ( 2 x ) + 30{/tex} = 0.015x 2 - 0.04x + 30 On putting x = 300, we get {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.015 ( 300 ) ^ { 2 } - 0.04 ( 300 ) + 30{/tex} = 1350 - 12 + 30 = 1368 Therefore,the marginal cost of food for 300 students is Rs 1368. By providing free mid-day meals to the students of primary classes, care and concern is shown towards their health and nutritional status.
solution_html:
Given, C(x) = 0.005x3 - 0.02x2 + 30x + 50
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.005 \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) - 0.02 ( 2 x ) + 30{/tex}
= 0.015x2 - 0.04x + 30
On putting x = 300, we get
{tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0.015 ( 300 ) ^ { 2 } - 0.04 ( 300 ) + 30{/tex}
= 1350 - 12 + 30 = 1368
Therefore,the marginal cost of food for 300 students is Rs 1368.
By providing free mid-day meals to the students of primary classes, care and concern is shown towards their health and nutritional status.

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x,0 \leq x \leq 2\pi {/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x,0 \leq x \leq 2\pi {/tex} is increasing or decreasing.
explanation: The given function is: {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = -\sin x + \cos x{/tex} Now, let f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos x - \sin x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{{\cos x}}{{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan x = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}{/tex} {tex}0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2\pi {/tex} int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} +ve Increase {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} -ve Decrease {tex}\left( {\frac{{5\pi }}{4},2\pi } \right){/tex} +ve increase
solution: The given function is: {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = -\sin x + \cos x{/tex} Now, let f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos x - \sin x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{{\cos x}}{{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan x = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}{/tex} {tex}0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2\pi {/tex} int Sign of f’(x) Result {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} +ve Increase {tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} -ve Decrease {tex}\left( {\frac{{5\pi }}{4},2\pi } \right){/tex} +ve increase
solution_html:
The given function is: {tex}f(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f'(x) = -\sin x + \cos x{/tex}
Now, let f'(x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos x - \sin x = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{{\cos x}}{{\cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tan x = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}{/tex}
{tex}0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2\pi {/tex}

int Sign of f’(x) Result
{tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} +ve Increase
{tex}\left( {\frac{\pi }{4},\frac{{5\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} -ve Decrease
{tex}\left( {\frac{{5\pi }}{4},2\pi } \right){/tex} +ve increase

question_html: Show that {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2 + x}},x > - 1{/tex} is an increasing function of x throughout its domain.
question_text: Show that {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2 + x}},x > - 1{/tex} is an increasing function of x throughout its domain.
explanation: Given: {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2+x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left[ {\frac{{\left( {2 + x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x} \right) - 2x\frac{d}{{dx}} \left( {2 + x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{{\left( {4 + 2x - 2x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{4}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2 - 4\left( {1 + x} \right)}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right){{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right)\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2}}{/tex} ...(i) Domain of the given function is given to be {tex}x > - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + 1 > 0{/tex} Also {tex}{\left( {2 + x} \right)^2} > 0{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in domain {tex}x > - 1{/tex} and f is an increasing function.
solution: Given: {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2+x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left[ {\frac{{\left( {2 + x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x} \right) - 2x\frac{d}{{dx}} \left( {2 + x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{{\left( {4 + 2x - 2x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{4}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2 - 4\left( {1 + x} \right)}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right){{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right)\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2}}{/tex} ...(i) Domain of the given function is given to be {tex}x > - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + 1 > 0{/tex} Also {tex}{\left( {2 + x} \right)^2} > 0{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in domain {tex}x > - 1{/tex} and f is an increasing function.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \log \left( {1 + x} \right) - \frac{{2x}}{{2+x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left[ {\frac{{\left( {2 + x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {2x} \right) - 2x\frac{d}{{dx}} \left( {2 + x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{{\left( {4 + 2x - 2x} \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + x}} - \frac{4}{{{{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2 - 4\left( {1 + x} \right)}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right){{\left( {2 + x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {1 + x} \right)\left( {2 + {x}} \right)^2}}{/tex}       ...(i)
Domain of the given function is given to be {tex}x > - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x + 1 > 0{/tex}
Also {tex}{\left( {2 + x} \right)^2} > 0{/tex} and {tex}{x^2} \geqslant 0{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for all x in domain {tex}x > - 1{/tex} and f is an increasing function.

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function given by f(x) = x4 - 8x3 + 22x2 - 24x + 21 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function given by f(x) = x 4 - 8x 3 + 22x 2 - 24x + 21 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
explanation: According to the question, f(x) = x 4 - 8x 3 + 22x 2 - 24x + 21 Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = 4x 3 - 24x 2 + 4x - 24 = 4(x 3 - 6x 2 + 11x - 6) = 4(x - 1) (x 2 - 5x + 6) = 4(x - 1) (x - 2) (x -3) Put f'(x)= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(x - 1) (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 , 2, 3 Therefore, the possible intervals are (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1 ), (1, 2 ), (2, 3) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). For interval (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1), f'(x) < 0 For interval (1, 2), f'(x) > 0 For interval (2, 3), f'(x) < 0 For interval (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f'(x) > 0. Also, as f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is contiuous at x = 1, 2, 3. Hence, function increases in [ 1, 2] and [ 3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). function decreases in (- {tex}\infty{/tex} . 1 ] and [ 2, 3].
solution: According to the question, f(x) = x 4 - 8x 3 + 22x 2 - 24x + 21 Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = 4x 3 - 24x 2 + 4x - 24 = 4(x 3 - 6x 2 + 11x - 6) = 4(x - 1) (x 2 - 5x + 6) = 4(x - 1) (x - 2) (x -3) Put f'(x)= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(x - 1) (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 , 2, 3 Therefore, the possible intervals are (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1 ), (1, 2 ), (2, 3) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). For interval (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1), f'(x) < 0 For interval (1, 2), f'(x) > 0 For interval (2, 3), f'(x) < 0 For interval (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ), f'(x) > 0. Also, as f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is contiuous at x = 1, 2, 3. Hence, function increases in [ 1, 2] and [ 3, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). function decreases in (- {tex}\infty{/tex} . 1 ] and [ 2, 3].
solution_html:
According to the question,  f(x) = x4 - 8x3 + 22x2 - 24x + 21
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = 4x3 - 24x2 + 4x - 24
= 4(x3 - 6x2 + 11x - 6)
= 4(x - 1) (x2 - 5x + 6)
= 4(x - 1) (x - 2) (x -3)
Put f'(x)= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(x - 1) (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 , 2, 3
Therefore, the possible intervals are (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 1 ), (1, 2 ), (2, 3) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex}).
For interval (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 1), f'(x) < 0
For interval (1, 2), f'(x) > 0
For interval (2, 3), f'(x) < 0
For interval (3, {tex}\infty{/tex}), f'(x) > 0.
Also, as f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is contiuous at x = 1, 2, 3.
Hence,

  1. function increases in [ 1, 2] and [ 3, {tex}\infty{/tex}).
  2. function decreases in (- {tex}\infty{/tex}. 1 ] and [ 2, 3].

question_html: An open box with a square base is to be made out of a given quantity of cardboard of area c2. Show that the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cubic units.
question_text: An open box with a square base is to be made out of a given quantity of cardboard of area c 2 . Show that the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cubic units.
explanation: Let the dimensions of the box be x and y. Also, let V denote its volume and S denotes its total surface area. Now, S = x 2 + 4xy Given, x 2 + 4xy = C 2 {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x }{/tex} ...(i) Also, volume of the box is given by V = x 2 y {tex} \Rightarrow V = x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x } \right){/tex} [From Eq.(i)] {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac{dV}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C 2 = 3x 2 {tex}\therefore x = C / \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d V } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 6 x } { 4 } = \frac { - 3 x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left. \frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right| _ { a t x = C / \sqrt { 3 } } < 0{/tex} This implies V is maximum. Now, maximum volume at x = {tex} \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} is {tex}V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \cdot C ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] \left[ \text { put } x = \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } - \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { 3 C ^ { 3 } -C^3} { 3 \sqrt3}\right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \times \frac { 2 C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } = \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Hence, the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cu units.
solution: Let the dimensions of the box be x and y. Also, let V denote its volume and S denotes its total surface area. Now, S = x 2 + 4xy Given, x 2 + 4xy = C 2 {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x }{/tex} ...(i) Also, volume of the box is given by V = x 2 y {tex} \Rightarrow V = x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x } \right){/tex} [From Eq.(i)] {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac{dV}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C 2 = 3x 2 {tex}\therefore x = C / \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d V } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 6 x } { 4 } = \frac { - 3 x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left. \frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right| _ { a t x = C / \sqrt { 3 } } < 0{/tex} This implies V is maximum. Now, maximum volume at x = {tex} \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} is {tex}V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \cdot C ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] \left[ \text { put } x = \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } - \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { 3 C ^ { 3 } -C^3} { 3 \sqrt3}\right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \times \frac { 2 C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } = \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Hence, the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cu units.
solution_html:
Let the dimensions of the box be x and y. Also, let V denote its volume and S denotes its total surface area.
Now, S = x2 + 4xy
Given, x2 + 4xy = C2
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x }{/tex}    ...(i)
Also, volume of the box is given by
V = x2y
{tex} \Rightarrow V = x ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { 4 x } \right){/tex}[From Eq.(i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d V } { d x } = \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac{dV}{dx}{/tex} = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C2 = 3x2
{tex}\therefore x = C / \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d V } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { C ^ { 2 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 6 x } { 4 } = \frac { - 3 x } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left. \frac { d ^ { 2 } V } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right| _ { a t x = C / \sqrt { 3 } } < 0{/tex}
This implies V is maximum.
Now, maximum volume at x ={tex} \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} is
{tex}V = \frac { x C ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \cdot C ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] \left[ \text { put } x = \frac { C } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } - \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \frac { 3 C ^ { 3 } -C^3} { 3 \sqrt3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \times \frac { 2 C ^ { 3 } } { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } = \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Hence, the maximum volume of box is {tex} \frac { C ^ { 3 } } { 6 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} cu units.

question_html: Prove that the area of a right angled triangle of given hypotenuse is maximum, when the triangle is isosceles.
question_text: Prove that the area of a right angled triangle of given hypotenuse is maximum, when the triangle is isosceles.
explanation: Let a and b be the sides of right-angled triangle and c be the hypotenuse. From {tex} \Delta{/tex} ABC, we have c 2 = a 2 + b 2 Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, (A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \cdot b = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. a, we get {tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot 1 \cdot \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot a \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 a ) } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c 2 - a 2 - a 2 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c 2 = 2a 2 Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { - a } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \left\lfloor \frac { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { c ^ { 2 } a } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } < 0{/tex} Therefore, Area of triangle ABC is maximum and b = {tex} \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a^{ 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 a^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = a Hence, the triangle is isosceles.
solution: Let a and b be the sides of right-angled triangle and c be the hypotenuse. From {tex} \Delta{/tex} ABC, we have c 2 = a 2 + b 2 Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, (A) = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \cdot b = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. a, we get {tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot 1 \cdot \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot a \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 a ) } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c 2 - a 2 - a 2 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c 2 = 2a 2 Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { - a } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \left\lfloor \frac { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { c ^ { 2 } a } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } < 0{/tex} Therefore, Area of triangle ABC is maximum and b = {tex} \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a^{ 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 a^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = a Hence, the triangle is isosceles.
solution_html:
Let a and b be the sides of right-angled triangle and c be the hypotenuse.

From {tex} \Delta{/tex}ABC, we have
c2 = a2 + b2
Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, (A) ={tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \cdot b = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. a, we get
{tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot 1 \cdot \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot a \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 a ) } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d a } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } - \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c2 - a2 - a2 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} c2 = 2a2
Now, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { - a } { \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } a \left\lfloor \frac { c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { c ^ { 2 } a } { \left( c ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } < 0{/tex}
Therefore, Area of triangle ABC is maximum and
b = {tex} \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } - a^{ 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 a^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = a
Hence, the triangle is isosceles.

question_html:
The money to be spent for the welfare of the employees of a firm is proportional to the rate of change of its total revenue (marginal revenue).
If the total revenue (in Rs.) received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x2 + 36x + 5, then find the marginal revenue, when x = 5 and write which value does the question indicate?
question_text: The money to be spent for the welfare of the employees of a firm is proportional to the rate of change of its total revenue (marginal revenue). If the total revenue (in Rs.) received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x 2 + 36x + 5, then find the marginal revenue, when x = 5 and write which value does the question indicate?
explanation: By definition of marginal revenue we have, marginal revenue {tex}( M R ) = \frac { d R } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } + 36 x + 5) = 6 x + 36\right.{/tex} When x = 5, then MR = 6(5) + 36 = 30 + 36 = 66 Hence, the required marginal revenue is Rs. 66. More amount of money spent for the welfare of the employees with the increase in marginal revenue show affinity and care for employees.
solution: By definition of marginal revenue we have, marginal revenue {tex}( M R ) = \frac { d R } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } + 36 x + 5) = 6 x + 36\right.{/tex} When x = 5, then MR = 6(5) + 36 = 30 + 36 = 66 Hence, the required marginal revenue is Rs. 66. More amount of money spent for the welfare of the employees with the increase in marginal revenue show affinity and care for employees.
solution_html:
By definition of marginal revenue we have,
marginal revenue {tex}( M R ) = \frac { d R } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } + 36 x + 5) = 6 x + 36\right.{/tex}
When x = 5, then
MR = 6(5) + 36 = 30 + 36 = 66
Hence, the required marginal revenue is Rs. 66.
More amount of money spent for the welfare of the employees with the increase in marginal revenue show affinity and care for employees.

question_html: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
question_text: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
explanation: {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} When x = 4 {tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3 {tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} When x = 4 {tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3 {tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} 
{tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} 
{tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
When x = 4
{tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3
{tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} 
{tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} 
{tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}

question_html: Show that the height of a closed right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is equal to diameter of base.
question_text: Show that the height of a closed right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is equal to diameter of base.
explanation: Let VAB be the cone of base radius r, height h and radius of base of the inscribed cylinder be x. Now, we observe that {tex}\Delta V O B \sim \Delta B ^ { \prime } D B \Rightarrow \frac { V O } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { O B } { D B }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} if the triangles are similar, then their sides are proportional] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { r } { r - x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B ^ { \prime } D = \frac { h ( r - x ) } { r }{/tex} Let C be the curved surface area of cylinder. Then, C = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (OC)(B'D) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 2 \pi x h ( r - x ) } { r } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } \left( rx - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ){/tex} For Maxima or Minima, put {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r - 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow r = 2 x{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, radius of cylinder is half of that of cone. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } C } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 2 \pi h ( r - 2 x ) } { r } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( - 2 ) = \frac { - 4 \pi h } { r } < 0 \text { as } h , r > 0{/tex} Therefore, C is maximum or greatest. Hence, C is greatest at x = {tex}\frac r2{/tex}
solution: Let VAB be the cone of base radius r, height h and radius of base of the inscribed cylinder be x. Now, we observe that {tex}\Delta V O B \sim \Delta B ^ { \prime } D B \Rightarrow \frac { V O } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { O B } { D B }{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} if the triangles are similar, then their sides are proportional] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { r } { r - x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B ^ { \prime } D = \frac { h ( r - x ) } { r }{/tex} Let C be the curved surface area of cylinder. Then, C = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (OC)(B'D) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 2 \pi x h ( r - x ) } { r } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } \left( rx - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ){/tex} For Maxima or Minima, put {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r - 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow r = 2 x{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, radius of cylinder is half of that of cone. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } C } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 2 \pi h ( r - 2 x ) } { r } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( - 2 ) = \frac { - 4 \pi h } { r } < 0 \text { as } h , r > 0{/tex} Therefore, C is maximum or greatest. Hence, C is greatest at x = {tex}\frac r2{/tex}
solution_html:
Let VAB be the cone of base radius r, height h and radius of base of the inscribed cylinder be x.

Now, we observe that
{tex}\Delta V O B \sim \Delta B ^ { \prime } D B \Rightarrow \frac { V O } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { O B } { D B }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} if the triangles are similar, then their sides are proportional]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { h } { B ^ { \prime } D } = \frac { r } { r - x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B ^ { \prime } D = \frac { h ( r - x ) } { r }{/tex}
Let C be the  curved surface area of cylinder. Then,
C = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(OC)(B'D)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 2 \pi x h ( r - x ) } { r } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } \left( rx - x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ){/tex}
For Maxima or Minima, put {tex}\frac { d C } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( r - 2 x ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r - 2 x = 0 \Rightarrow r = 2 x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore, radius of cylinder is half of that of cone.
Also,{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } C } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ \frac { 2 \pi h ( r - 2 x ) } { r } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 \pi h } { r } ( - 2 ) = \frac { - 4 \pi h } { r } < 0 \text { as } h , r > 0{/tex}
Therefore, C is maximum or greatest.

Hence, C is greatest at x = {tex}\frac r2{/tex}

question_html: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex} times the radius of the base.
question_text: Show that the right circular cone of least curved surface and given volume has an altitude equal to {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex} times the radius of the base.
explanation: Volume of the cone is: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi {r^2}h{/tex} Let {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} Where k is constant {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} {tex}h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}{s^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}{l^2}{/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2}){/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}\left[ {{r^2} + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^4}}}} \right]\left[ {\because h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}z = {\pi ^2}{r^4} + {\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}} = 12{\pi ^2}{r^2} + 6{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - \frac{{2{\pi ^2}{k^2}}}{{{r^3}}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^6} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{k^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} Put {tex}k = {r^2}h{/tex} we get {tex}{r^4}{h^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{h^2} = 2{r^2}{/tex} {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex} {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right)_{h = \sqrt 2 r}}{/tex} is negative z is maximum at {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex}
solution: Volume of the cone is: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi {r^2}h{/tex} Let {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} Where k is constant {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} {tex}h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} {tex}s = \pi rl{/tex} {tex}{s^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}{l^2}{/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2}){/tex} {tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}\left[ {{r^2} + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^4}}}} \right]\left[ {\because h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}z = {\pi ^2}{r^4} + {\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}} = 12{\pi ^2}{r^2} + 6{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - \frac{{2{\pi ^2}{k^2}}}{{{r^3}}} = 0{/tex} {tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^6} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{k^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} Put {tex}k = {r^2}h{/tex} we get {tex}{r^4}{h^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} {tex}{h^2} = 2{r^2}{/tex} {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex} {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right)_{h = \sqrt 2 r}}{/tex} is negative z is maximum at {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex}
solution_html:
Volume of the cone is: {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
Let {tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} 
Where k is constant
{tex}{r^2}h = k{/tex} 
{tex}h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} 
{tex}s = \pi rl{/tex} 
{tex}{s^2} = {\pi ^2}{r^2}{l^2}{/tex} 
{tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}({r^2} + {h^2}){/tex} 
{tex} = {\pi ^2}.{r^2}\left[ {{r^2} + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^4}}}} \right]\left[ {\because h = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}z = {\pi ^2}{r^4} + {\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 3}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}} = 12{\pi ^2}{r^2} + 6{\pi ^2}{k^2}{r^{ - 4}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dz}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^3} - \frac{{2{\pi ^2}{k^2}}}{{{r^3}}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}4{\pi ^2}{r^6} - 2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}2{\pi ^2}{k^2} = 4{\pi ^2}{r^6}{/tex} 
{tex}{k^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} 
Put {tex}k = {r^2}h{/tex} we get
{tex}{r^4}{h^2} = 2{r^6}{/tex} 
{tex}{h^2} = 2{r^2}{/tex} 
{tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex} 
{tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}z}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right)_{h = \sqrt 2 r}}{/tex} is negative
z is maximum at {tex}h = \sqrt 2 r{/tex}

question_html: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum value and of given slant height is {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2{/tex}
question_text: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of the maximum value and of given slant height is {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2{/tex}
explanation: Let x be the radius, y be the height, l be the slant height of given cone and {tex}\theta {/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of cone. {tex}\therefore {l^2} = {x^2} + {y^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = {l^2} - {y^2}{/tex} …(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cone {tex}\left( V \right) = \frac{1}{3}\pi {x^2}y{/tex} …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {{l^2} - {y^2}} \right)y{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2}y - {y^3}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{y^2}}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( { - 6y} \right) = - 2\pi y{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} - 3{y^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{y^2} = {l^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} At {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2\pi \left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 2\pi l}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} [Negative] {tex}\therefore {/tex} V is maximum at {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}{x^2} = {l^2} - \frac{{{l^2}}}{3} = \frac{{2{l^2}}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Semi-vertical angle, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \sqrt 2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2 {/tex}
solution: Let x be the radius, y be the height, l be the slant height of given cone and {tex}\theta {/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of cone. {tex}\therefore {l^2} = {x^2} + {y^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = {l^2} - {y^2}{/tex} …(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cone {tex}\left( V \right) = \frac{1}{3}\pi {x^2}y{/tex} …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {{l^2} - {y^2}} \right)y{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2}y - {y^3}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{y^2}}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( { - 6y} \right) = - 2\pi y{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} - 3{y^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{y^2} = {l^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} At {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2\pi \left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 2\pi l}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} [Negative] {tex}\therefore {/tex} V is maximum at {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}{x^2} = {l^2} - \frac{{{l^2}}}{3} = \frac{{2{l^2}}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Semi-vertical angle, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \sqrt 2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2 {/tex}
solution_html:
Let x be the radius, y be the height, l be the slant height of given cone and {tex}\theta {/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of cone.

{tex}\therefore {l^2} = {x^2} + {y^2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = {l^2} - {y^2}{/tex} …(i)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cone {tex}\left( V \right) = \frac{1}{3}\pi {x^2}y{/tex} …(ii)

{tex} \Rightarrow V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {{l^2} - {y^2}} \right)y{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2}y - {y^3}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{y^2}}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\left( { - 6y} \right) = - 2\pi y{/tex} 

Now {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{3}\left( {{l^2} - 3{y^2}} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} - 3{y^2} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 3{y^2} = {l^2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

At {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = - 2\pi \left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{ - 2\pi l}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} [Negative]

{tex}\therefore {/tex}  V is maximum at {tex}y = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  From eq. (i), {tex}{x^2} = {l^2} - \frac{{{l^2}}}{3} = \frac{{2{l^2}}}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Semi-vertical angle, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \sqrt 2 {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt 2 {/tex}

question_html: Find the point on the curve y2 = 2x which is at a minimum distance from the point (1, 4).
question_text: Find the point on the curve y 2 = 2x which is at a minimum distance from the point (1, 4).
explanation: The given equation of curve is y 2 = 2x and the given point is Q (1, 4). Let P(x, y) be any point on the curve. Now, distance between points P and Q is given by {tex} P Q = \sqrt { ( 1 - x ) ^ { 2 } + ( 4 - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} using distance formula, {tex} \left[ {d = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2}} ]} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q = \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 16 + y ^ { 2 } - 8 y }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 x - 8 y + 17 }{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get PQ 2 = x 2 + y 2 - 2x - 8y + 17 {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) - 8 y + 17{/tex} {tex} \left[ \text { given, } y ^ { 2 } = 2 x \Rightarrow x = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + y ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} Let PQ 2 = Z Then, {tex} Z = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get {tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = \frac { 4 y ^ { 3 } } { 4 } - 8 = y ^ { 3 } - 8{/tex} For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 3 } - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow y ^ { 3 } = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = 2{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( y ^ { 3 } - 8 \right) = 3 y ^ { 2 }{/tex} On putting y = 2, we get {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { y = 2 } = 3 ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } = 12 > 0{/tex} {tex} \because \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} Z is minimum and therefore PQ is also minimum as Z = PQ 2 . On putting y = 2 in the given equation, i.e. y 2 = 2x, we get (2) 2 = 2x {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 = 2 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2{/tex} Hence, the point which is at a minimum distance from point (1, 4) is P (2, 2).
solution: The given equation of curve is y 2 = 2x and the given point is Q (1, 4). Let P(x, y) be any point on the curve. Now, distance between points P and Q is given by {tex} P Q = \sqrt { ( 1 - x ) ^ { 2 } + ( 4 - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} using distance formula, {tex} \left[ {d = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2}} ]} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q = \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 16 + y ^ { 2 } - 8 y }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 x - 8 y + 17 }{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get PQ 2 = x 2 + y 2 - 2x - 8y + 17 {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) - 8 y + 17{/tex} {tex} \left[ \text { given, } y ^ { 2 } = 2 x \Rightarrow x = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + y ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} Let PQ 2 = Z Then, {tex} Z = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get {tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = \frac { 4 y ^ { 3 } } { 4 } - 8 = y ^ { 3 } - 8{/tex} For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 3 } - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow y ^ { 3 } = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = 2{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( y ^ { 3 } - 8 \right) = 3 y ^ { 2 }{/tex} On putting y = 2, we get {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { y = 2 } = 3 ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } = 12 > 0{/tex} {tex} \because \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} Z is minimum and therefore PQ is also minimum as Z = PQ 2 . On putting y = 2 in the given equation, i.e. y 2 = 2x, we get (2) 2 = 2x {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 = 2 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2{/tex} Hence, the point which is at a minimum distance from point (1, 4) is P (2, 2).
solution_html:
The given equation of curve is y2 = 2x and the given point is Q (1, 4).

Let P(x, y) be any point on the curve.
Now, distance between points P and Q is given by
{tex} P Q = \sqrt { ( 1 - x ) ^ { 2 } + ( 4 - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
using distance formula, {tex} \left[ {d = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2}} ]} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q = \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 16 + y ^ { 2 } - 8 y }{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 x - 8 y + 17 }{/tex}
On squaring both sides, we get
PQ2 = x2 + y2 - 2x - 8y + 17
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) - 8 y + 17{/tex} {tex} \left[ \text { given, } y ^ { 2 } = 2 x \Rightarrow x = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + y ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } - 8 y + 17{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P Q ^ { 2 } = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex}
Let PQ2 = Z
Then, {tex} Z = \frac { y ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - 8 y + 17{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get
{tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = \frac { 4 y ^ { 3 } } { 4 } - 8 = y ^ { 3 } - 8{/tex}
For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d Z } { d y } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 3 } - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow y ^ { 3 } = 8{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = 2{/tex}
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( y ^ { 3 } - 8 \right) = 3 y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
On putting y = 2, we get
{tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { y = 2 } = 3 ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } = 12 > 0{/tex}
{tex} \because \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d y ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} Z is minimum and therefore PQ is also minimum as Z = PQ2.
On putting y = 2 in the given equation, i.e. y2 = 2x, we get
(2)2 = 2x
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 = 2 x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2{/tex}
Hence, the point which is at a minimum distance from point (1, 4) is P (2, 2).
