question_html: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of 12cm3/s. the falling sand forms a cone on the ground in such a way that the height of the cone is always one-sixth of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing when the height is 4 cm?
question_text: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of 12cm 3 /s. the falling sand forms a cone on the ground in such a way that the height of the cone is always one-sixth of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing when the height is 4 cm?
explanation: Let radius of the cone = r Height of the cone = h Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given) {tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given) {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
solution: Let radius of the cone = r Height of the cone = h Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given) {tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given) {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
solution_html:
Let radius of the cone = r
Height of the cone = h
Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given)
{tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given)
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 2x3 + 9x2 + 12x + 20 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 2x 3 + 9x 2 + 12x + 20 is (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
explanation: Given function is {tex}f(x) = 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 12x + 20{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}f'(x) = 6x^2 + 18x + 12{/tex} Put f'(x) = 0, we get {tex}6x^2 + 18x + 12 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6(x^2 + 3x + 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x + 1 = 0 \quad or\quad x + 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}x = - 2\ or - 1{/tex} Sign of f'(x) x<-2 (+)(-)(-) +ve -2<x<-1 (+)(-)(+) -ve x>-1 (+)(+)(+) +ve A function f(x) is said to be an strictly increasing function, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} ,-2) and (-1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ) and decreasing on interval (-2 -1) Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = -1, - 2. {tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , - 2] and [-1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ). decreasing on interval [-2, -1].
solution: Given function is {tex}f(x) = 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 12x + 20{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}f'(x) = 6x^2 + 18x + 12{/tex} Put f'(x) = 0, we get {tex}6x^2 + 18x + 12 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6(x^2 + 3x + 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x + 1 = 0 \quad or\quad x + 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}x = - 2\ or - 1{/tex} Sign of f'(x) x<-2 (+)(-)(-) +ve -2<x<-1 (+)(-)(+) -ve x>-1 (+)(+)(+) +ve A function f(x) is said to be an strictly increasing function, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} ,-2) and (-1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ) and decreasing on interval (-2 -1) Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = -1, - 2. {tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , - 2] and [-1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ). decreasing on interval [-2, -1].
solution_html:
Given function is {tex}f(x) = 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 12x + 20{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, 
 {tex}f'(x) = 6x^2 + 18x + 12{/tex}
Put f'(x) = 0, we get
{tex}6x^2 + 18x + 12 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6(x^2 + 3x + 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x + 2) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} x + 1 = 0 \quad or\quad x + 2 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}x = - 2\ or - 1{/tex}

    Sign of f'(x)
x<-2 (+)(-)(-) +ve
-2<x<-1 (+)(-)(+) -ve
x>-1 (+)(+)(+) +ve

A function f(x) is said to be an strictly increasing function, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing, if f'(x) < 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is increasing on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex},-2) and (-1, {tex} \infty{/tex}) and decreasing on interval (-2 -1)
Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = -1, - 2.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} given function is

  1. increasing on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex}, - 2] and [-1, {tex} \infty{/tex}).
  2. decreasing on interval [-2, -1].

question_html: Show that of all the rectangles with a given perimeter, the square has the largest area.
question_text: Show that of all the rectangles with a given perimeter, the square has the largest area.
explanation: Let x and y be the lengths of two sides of a rectangle. Again, let P denotes its perimeter and 'A' be the area of rectangle Then, P = 2 (x + y) [ {tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle = 2 (l + b)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P = 2 x + 2 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 }{/tex} ...(i) We know that, area of rectangle is given by A = xy {tex}\Rightarrow A = x \left( \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 } \right){/tex} [by using Eq. (i)] On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow P = 4 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 2 y = 4 x \quad [ \because P = 2 x + 2 y ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y{/tex} So, the rectangle is a square. Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 4 } { 2 } = - 2 < 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A is maximum. Hence, the area is maximum, when the rectangle is a square.
solution: Let x and y be the lengths of two sides of a rectangle. Again, let P denotes its perimeter and 'A' be the area of rectangle Then, P = 2 (x + y) [ {tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle = 2 (l + b)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P = 2 x + 2 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 }{/tex} ...(i) We know that, area of rectangle is given by A = xy {tex}\Rightarrow A = x \left( \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 } \right){/tex} [by using Eq. (i)] On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 }{/tex} For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow P = 4 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 2 y = 4 x \quad [ \because P = 2 x + 2 y ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y{/tex} So, the rectangle is a square. Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 4 } { 2 } = - 2 < 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A is maximum. Hence, the area is maximum, when the rectangle is a square.
solution_html:
Let x and y be the lengths of two sides of a rectangle. Again, let P denotes its perimeter and 'A' be the area of rectangle
Then, P = 2 (x + y) [{tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle = 2 (l + b)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P = 2 x + 2 y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 }{/tex} ...(i)
We know that, area of rectangle is given by
A = xy
{tex}\Rightarrow A = x \left( \frac { P - 2 x } { 2 } \right){/tex} [by using Eq. (i)]
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d A } { d x } = \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima put {tex} \frac { d A } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow P = 4 x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x + 2 y = 4 x \quad [ \because P = 2 x + 2 y ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y{/tex}
So, the rectangle is a square.
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { P - 4 x } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 4 } { 2 } = - 2 < 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A is maximum.
Hence, the area is maximum, when the rectangle is a square.

question_html: Show that for all the rectangles of given area, the square has the smallest perimeter.
question_text: Show that for all the rectangles of given area, the square has the smallest perimeter.
explanation: Let x and y be the lengths of sides of a rectangle. Again, let A denotes its area and P be the perimeter. Now, area of rectangle, A = xy {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { A } { x }{/tex} ...(i) And P = 2 (x + y) [ {tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle= 2 (l + b)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = 2 \left( x + \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because y = \frac { A } { x } , \text { from Eq. (i) } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 = \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x ^ { 2 } \quad [ \because A = x y ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = y{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] = 2 \left( \frac { 2 A } { x ^ { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 4 A } { x ^ { 3 } } > 0{/tex} Here, x and A being the side and area of rectangle can never be negative. So, P is minimum. Hence, perimeter of rectangle is minimum, when rectangle is a square.
solution: Let x and y be the lengths of sides of a rectangle. Again, let A denotes its area and P be the perimeter. Now, area of rectangle, A = xy {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { A } { x }{/tex} ...(i) And P = 2 (x + y) [ {tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle= 2 (l + b)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = 2 \left( x + \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because y = \frac { A } { x } , \text { from Eq. (i) } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 = \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x ^ { 2 } \quad [ \because A = x y ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad x = y{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] = 2 \left( \frac { 2 A } { x ^ { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 4 A } { x ^ { 3 } } > 0{/tex} Here, x and A being the side and area of rectangle can never be negative. So, P is minimum. Hence, perimeter of rectangle is minimum, when rectangle is a square.
solution_html:
Let x and y be the lengths of sides of a rectangle.
Again, let A denotes its area and P be the perimeter.
Now, area of rectangle, A = xy
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { A } { x }{/tex} ...(i)
And P = 2 (x + y) [{tex} \because{/tex} perimeter of rectangle= 2 (l + b)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = 2 \left( x + \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because y = \frac { A } { x } , \text { from Eq. (i) } \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w. r. t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex}.
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 = \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x ^ { 2 } \quad [ \because A = x y ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x = y{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left[ 2 \left( 1 - \frac { A } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] = 2 \left( \frac { 2 A } { x ^ { 3 } } \right) = \frac { 4 A } { x ^ { 3 } } > 0{/tex}
Here, x and A being the side and area of rectangle can never be negative. So, P is minimum.
Hence, perimeter of rectangle is minimum, when rectangle is a square.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A window has the shape of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle. If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, then find the dimensions of the rectangle that will produce the largest area of the window.
question_text: A window has the shape of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle. If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, then find the dimensions of the rectangle that will produce the largest area of the window.
explanation: Let ABCD be the rectangle which is surmounted by an equilateral {tex} \Delta{/tex} EDC. Now, given that Perimeter of window = 12m {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2y + y = 12 {tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y{/tex} ...(i) Let A denotes the combined area of the window. Then, A = area of rectangle + area of equilateral triangle {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = y \left( 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \right) + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = 6 y - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get, {tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } - 3 \right) = - 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( \frac { d A } { d y } \right) = \frac { d } { d y } \left( 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y \right){/tex} {tex}= - 3 + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - 6 + \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } < 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} A is maximum. Now, on putting {tex} y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \Rightarrow x = \frac { 36 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } - 18 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Hence, the area of the window is largest when the dimensions of the window are {tex} x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \text { and } y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
solution: Let ABCD be the rectangle which is surmounted by an equilateral {tex} \Delta{/tex} EDC. Now, given that Perimeter of window = 12m {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2y + y = 12 {tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y{/tex} ...(i) Let A denotes the combined area of the window. Then, A = area of rectangle + area of equilateral triangle {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = y \left( 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \right) + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = 6 y - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get, {tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } - 3 \right) = - 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( \frac { d A } { d y } \right) = \frac { d } { d y } \left( 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y \right){/tex} {tex}= - 3 + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - 6 + \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } < 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} A is maximum. Now, on putting {tex} y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \Rightarrow x = \frac { 36 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } - 18 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Hence, the area of the window is largest when the dimensions of the window are {tex} x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \text { and } y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let ABCD be the rectangle which is surmounted by an equilateral {tex} \Delta{/tex}EDC.

Now, given that
Perimeter of window = 12m
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2y + y = 12
{tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y{/tex} ...(i)
Let A denotes the combined area of the window.
Then, A = area of rectangle + area of equilateral triangle
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = x y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = y \left( 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \right) + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = 6 y - \frac { 3 } { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. y, we get,
{tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d A } { d y } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } - 3 \right) = - 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Now, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } A } { d y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d y } \left( \frac { d A } { d y } \right) = \frac { d } { d y } \left( 6 - 3 y + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } y \right){/tex}
{tex}= - 3 + \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - 6 + \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } < 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} A is maximum.
Now, on putting {tex} y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} x = 6 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \Rightarrow x = \frac { 36 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } - 18 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Hence, the area of the window is largest when the dimensions of the window are

{tex} x = \frac { 18 - 6 \sqrt { 3 } } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } } \text { and } y = \frac { 12 } { 6 - \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}

question_html: Prove that y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}.
question_text: Prove that y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} .
explanation: According to the question,We have to prove that y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} . Now, y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} ........(i) We shall use definition of increasing function to prove the required result. {tex}{/tex} On differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta }{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{\left[ {(2 + \cos \theta ) \times \frac{d}{{d\theta }}(4\sin \theta ) - 4\sin \theta \times \frac{d}{{d\theta }}(2 + \cos \theta )} \right]}}{{{{(2 + \;\cos \;\theta )}^2}}}{/tex} - 1 [By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ( 4 \cos \theta ) - 4 \sin \theta ( 0 - \sin \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } - 1{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \cos \theta + 4 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { - ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \cos \theta + 4 \left( \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) } { - \left( 4 + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \cos \theta \right) } \end{array} \right] } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 8 \cos \theta + 4 - 4 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 4 \cos \theta } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 4 \cos \theta - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d \theta } = \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, as {tex} \cos \theta > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} and {tex} ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} being a perfect square is always positive for all {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Also, for {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} , we know that {tex} 0 < \cos \theta < 1{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad 4 - \cos \theta > 0 \text { for all } \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Thus, we conclude that {tex} \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d \theta } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Therefore, y is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Hence proved

1 [By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ( 4 \cos \theta ) - 4 \sin \theta ( 0 - \sin \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } - 1{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \cos \theta + 4 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d \theta } = \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, as {tex} \cos \theta > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} and {tex} ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} being a perfect square is always positive for all {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Also, for {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} , we know that {tex} 0 < \cos \theta < 1{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad 4 - \cos \theta > 0 \text { for all } \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Thus, we conclude that {tex} \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d \theta } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Therefore, y is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
According to the question,We have to prove that y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}.

Now, y = {tex} \frac { 4 \sin \theta } { 2 + \cos \theta } - \theta{/tex} ........(i)
We shall use definition of increasing function to prove the required result.{tex}{/tex}
On differentiating both sides of Eq. (i) w.r.t. {tex} \theta{/tex}, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta }{/tex}={tex} \frac{{\left[ {(2 + \cos \theta ) \times \frac{d}{{d\theta }}(4\sin \theta ) - 4\sin \theta \times \frac{d}{{d\theta }}(2 + \cos \theta )} \right]}}{{{{(2 + \;\cos \;\theta )}^2}}}{/tex}- 1 [By using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex} = \frac { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ( 4 \cos \theta ) - 4 \sin \theta ( 0 - \sin \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } - 1{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \cos \theta + 4 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { - ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \cos \theta + 4 \left( \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) } { - \left( 4 + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + 4 \cos \theta \right) } \end{array} \right] } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 8 \cos \theta + 4 - 4 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 4 \cos \theta } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 4 \cos \theta - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d \theta } = \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, as {tex} \cos \theta > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
and {tex} ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} being a perfect square is always positive for all {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
Also, for {tex} \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}, we know that {tex} 0 < \cos \theta < 1{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad 4 - \cos \theta > 0 \text { for all } \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
Thus, we conclude that 
{tex} \frac { \cos \theta ( 4 - \cos \theta ) } { ( 2 + \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d \theta } > 0 , \forall \theta \in \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
Therefore, y is an increasing function in {tex} \left( 0 , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
Hence proved

question_html: Prove the volume of the largest cone that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
question_text: Prove the volume of the largest cone that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R is {tex}\frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of the volume of the sphere.
explanation: Volume of cone , {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{r} = \sqrt {{R^2} - {x^2}} } \right]{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right).\left( {R + x} \right){/tex} {tex}Now ,\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - x} \right) - 2x\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left[ {R - x - 2x} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - 3x} \right){/tex} ...(1) Put {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} Now R + x= 0 {tex}\implies R = - x\left( {neglect} \right){/tex} Now , R-3x=0 {tex}\implies{/tex} R = 3x {tex}\frac{R}{3} = x{/tex} On again differentiate equation (1) {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3x} \right)\left( 1 \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{R}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + \frac{R}{3}} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3.\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{4R}}{3} \times - 3 + 0} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\pi 4R{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} < 0{/tex} Hence maximum Now {tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[( {{R^2} - {{\left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)}^2})\left( {R + \left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{8{R^2}}}{9} \times \frac{{4R}}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)\pi {R^3}{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} Value of sphere Value of cone {tex}= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of value of sphere.
solution: Volume of cone , {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{r} = \sqrt {{R^2} - {x^2}} } \right]{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right).\left( {R + x} \right){/tex} {tex}Now ,\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - x} \right) - 2x\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left[ {R - x - 2x} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - 3x} \right){/tex} ...(1) Put {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} Now R + x= 0 {tex}\implies R = - x\left( {neglect} \right){/tex} Now , R-3x=0 {tex}\implies{/tex} R = 3x {tex}\frac{R}{3} = x{/tex} On again differentiate equation (1) {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3x} \right)\left( 1 \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{R}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + \frac{R}{3}} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3.\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{4R}}{3} \times - 3 + 0} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\pi 4R{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} < 0{/tex} Hence maximum Now {tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[( {{R^2} - {{\left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)}^2})\left( {R + \left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{8{R^2}}}{9} \times \frac{{4R}}{3}} \right]{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)\pi {R^3}{/tex} {tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} Value of sphere Value of cone {tex}= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} of value of sphere.
solution_html:
Volume of cone , {tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{r} = \sqrt {{R^2} - {x^2}} } \right]{/tex}

{tex}V = \frac{1}{3}\pi .\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right).\left( {R + x} \right){/tex}

{tex}Now ,\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 2x} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - x} \right) - 2x\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left[ {R - x - 2x} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left( {R + x} \right)\left( {R - 3x} \right){/tex} ...(1)

Put {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex}

Now R + x= 0

{tex}\implies R = - x\left( {neglect} \right){/tex}

Now , R-3x=0

{tex}\implies{/tex}R = 3x

{tex}\frac{R}{3} = x{/tex}

On again differentiate equation (1)

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + x} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3x} \right)\left( 1 \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = \frac{R}{3}}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {R + \frac{R}{3}} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( {R - 3.\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{4R}}{3} \times - 3 + 0} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\pi 4R{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} < 0{/tex} Hence maximum

Now {tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\left( {{R^2} - {x^2}} \right)\left( {R + x} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}V= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[( {{R^2} - {{\left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)}^2})\left( {R + \left( {\frac{R}{3}} \right)} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\pi \left[ {\frac{{8{R^2}}}{9} \times \frac{{4R}}{3}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)\pi {R^3}{/tex}

{tex}V= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex} Value of sphere

Value of cone {tex}= \frac{8}{{27}}{/tex}of value of sphere.

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 20- 9x + 6x2 - x3 is (i) strictly increasing. (ii) strictly decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f(x) = 20- 9x + 6x 2 - x 3 is (i) strictly increasing. (ii) strictly decreasing.
explanation: Given, f(x) = 20 - 9x + 6x 2 - x 3 . On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = - 9 + 12x - 3x 2 On putting f'(x) = 0, we get -9 + 12x - 3x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3(x 2 - 4x + 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3(x - 1) (x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = 0 or x - 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 or 3 Now, we find intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) =-3(x - 1) (x - 3) Sign of f'(x) x<1 (-) (-) (-) -ve 1 < x < 3 (-) (+) (-) +ve x > 3 (-) (+) (+) -ve Therefore, by definition, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing on the interval (1, 3) and strictly decreasing on the intervals (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} )
solution: Given, f(x) = 20 - 9x + 6x 2 - x 3 . On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = - 9 + 12x - 3x 2 On putting f'(x) = 0, we get -9 + 12x - 3x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3(x 2 - 4x + 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3(x - 1) (x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = 0 or x - 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 or 3 Now, we find intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) =-3(x - 1) (x - 3) Sign of f'(x) x<1 (-) (-) (-) -ve 1 < x < 3 (-) (+) (-) +ve x > 3 (-) (+) (+) -ve Therefore, by definition, the given function f(x) is strictly increasing on the interval (1, 3) and strictly decreasing on the intervals (- {tex}\infty{/tex} , 1) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex} )
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = 20 - 9x + 6x2 - x3.
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = - 9 + 12x - 3x2
On putting f'(x) = 0, we get
-9 + 12x - 3x2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3(x2 - 4x + 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-3(x - 1) (x - 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)(x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = 0 or x - 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 or 3
Now, we find intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) =-3(x - 1) (x - 3) Sign of f'(x)
x<1 (-) (-) (-) -ve
1 < x < 3 (-) (+) (-) +ve
x > 3 (-) (+) (+) -ve

 Therefore, by definition, the given function f(x) is

  1. strictly increasing on the interval (1, 3) and
  2. strictly decreasing on the intervals (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, 1) and (3, {tex}\infty{/tex})

question_html: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet, so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the total surface area is least when depth of the tank is half its width.
question_text: An open tank with a square base and vertical sides is to be constructed from a metal sheet, so as to hold a given quantity of water. Show that the total surface area is least when depth of the tank is half its width.
explanation: Let the length, breadth and depth of the open tank be x, and y, respectively. Length and breadth are same because given tank has a square base. Again, let V denotes its volume and S denotes its surface area. Now, V = x 2 y, which is constant ...(i) Also, we know that the total surface area of the open tank is given by S = x 2 + 4xy ...(ii) [ {tex} \because{/tex} S = area of square base + area of the four walls] On putting {tex} y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex} S = x ^ { 2 } + 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 V } { x }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 V = 2 x ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 x ^ { 2 } y = 2 x ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = x ^ { 2 } y , \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 y = x \Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} So, depth of tank is half of its width. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d S } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 \cdot x ^ { 2 } y } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 y } { x } > 0{/tex} , as x > 0 and y > 0 Thus, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d x ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S is minimum. Hence, total surface area of the tank is least, when depth is half of its width.
solution: Let the length, breadth and depth of the open tank be x, and y, respectively. Length and breadth are same because given tank has a square base. Again, let V denotes its volume and S denotes its surface area. Now, V = x 2 y, which is constant ...(i) Also, we know that the total surface area of the open tank is given by S = x 2 + 4xy ...(ii) [ {tex} \because{/tex} S = area of square base + area of the four walls] On putting {tex} y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex} S = x ^ { 2 } + 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 V } { x }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 V = 2 x ^ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 x ^ { 2 } y = 2 x ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = x ^ { 2 } y , \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 y = x \Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} So, depth of tank is half of its width. Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d S } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 \cdot x ^ { 2 } y } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] {tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 y } { x } > 0{/tex} , as x > 0 and y > 0 Thus, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d x ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S is minimum. Hence, total surface area of the tank is least, when depth is half of its width.
solution_html:
Let the length, breadth and depth of the open tank be x, and y, respectively. Length and breadth are same because given tank has a square base. Again, let V denotes its volume and S denotes its surface area.
Now,
V = x2y, which is constant ...(i)
Also, we know that the total surface area of the open tank is given by
S = x2 + 4xy ...(ii)
[{tex} \because{/tex} S = area of square base + area of the four walls]
On putting {tex} y = \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex} S = x ^ { 2 } + 4 x \cdot \frac { V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad S = x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 V } { x }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex}\frac { d S } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 V = 2 x ^ { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 x ^ { 2 } y = 2 x ^ { 3 } \quad \left[ \because V = x ^ { 2 } y , \text { from } \mathrm { Eq } . \text { (i) } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 y = x \Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex}
So, depth of tank is half of its width.
Also, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d S } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 x - \frac { 4 V } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 V } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 \cdot x ^ { 2 } y } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}= 2 + \frac { 8 y } { x } > 0{/tex}, as x > 0 and y > 0
Thus, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d x ^ { 2 } } > 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S is minimum.
Hence, total surface area of the tank is least, when depth is half of its width.

question_html: Show that the cone of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a given sphere is such that three times its altitude is twice the diameter of the sphere. Find the volume of the largest cone inscribed in a sphere of radius R.
question_text: Show that the cone of greatest volume which can be inscribed in a given sphere is such that three times its altitude is twice the diameter of the sphere. Find the volume of the largest cone inscribed in a sphere of radius R.
explanation: Suppose R be the radius of the given sphere with centre O, and suppose V be the volume of the inscribed cone, h be its height and r be the radius of the base. In the above given figure, we have OD = AD - AO = (h-R) {tex}\therefore{/tex} R 2 = (h - R ) 2 + r 2 or r 2 = h(2R-h) ... (i) Now, {tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{2}(2 R-h){/tex} [using (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R-2 \pi h\right){/tex} For a maxima or minima, we have {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 0 or (4R - 3h) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R \quad[\because h \neq 0]{/tex} And, {tex}\left[\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}\right]_{h=(4 / 3) R}=-\frac{4 \pi R}{3}<0{/tex} Therefore, V is maximum when {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R{/tex} , i.e., when 3h = 2(2R) i.e., 3 times the height = 2 times the diameter. Volume of the largest cone = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi \times \frac{16 R^{2}}{9} \times\left(2 R-\frac{4 R}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{32 \pi R^{3}}{81}{/tex}
solution: Suppose R be the radius of the given sphere with centre O, and suppose V be the volume of the inscribed cone, h be its height and r be the radius of the base. In the above given figure, we have OD = AD - AO = (h-R) {tex}\therefore{/tex} R 2 = (h - R ) 2 + r 2 or r 2 = h(2R-h) ... (i) Now, {tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{2}(2 R-h){/tex} [using (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R-2 \pi h\right){/tex} For a maxima or minima, we have {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 0 or (4R - 3h) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R \quad[\because h \neq 0]{/tex} And, {tex}\left[\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}\right]_{h=(4 / 3) R}=-\frac{4 \pi R}{3}<0{/tex} Therefore, V is maximum when {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R{/tex} , i.e., when 3h = 2(2R) i.e., 3 times the height = 2 times the diameter. Volume of the largest cone = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi \times \frac{16 R^{2}}{9} \times\left(2 R-\frac{4 R}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{32 \pi R^{3}}{81}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose R be the radius of the given sphere with centre O, and suppose V be the volume of the inscribed cone, h be its height and r be the radius of the base.

In the above given figure, we have OD = AD - AO = (h-R)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R2 = (h - R )2 + r2 or r2 = h(2R-h) ... (i)
Now, {tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{2}(2 R-h){/tex} [using (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h){/tex}
and {tex}\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R-2 \pi h\right){/tex}
For a maxima or minima, we have {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d V}{d h}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h(4 R-3 h)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 0 or (4R - 3h) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R \quad[\because h \neq 0]{/tex}
And, {tex}\left[\frac{d^{2} V}{d h^{2}}\right]_{h=(4 / 3) R}=-\frac{4 \pi R}{3}<0{/tex}
Therefore, V is maximum when {tex}h=\frac{4}{3} R{/tex}, i.e., when 3h = 2(2R)
i.e., 3 times the height = 2 times the diameter.
Volume of the largest cone = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi \times \frac{16 R^{2}}{9} \times\left(2 R-\frac{4 R}{3}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{32 \pi R^{3}}{81}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = 2x3 - 15x2 + 36x + 6 is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = 2x 3 - 15x 2 + 36x + 6 is increasing decreasing.
explanation: Here,it is given the function f(x) = 2x 3 - 15x 2 + 36x + 6 f'(x) = 6x 2 - 30x + 36 = 6(x 2 - 5x + 6) = 6(x - 3)(x - 2) ... (i) f(x) is increasing {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} f'(x) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 6(x - 3)(x - 2) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 ... question_html:
Find the intervals of function f(x) = x3 - 12x2 + 36x + 17 is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals of function f(x) = x 3 - 12x 2 + 36x + 17 is increasing decreasing.
explanation: f (x) = x 3 - 12x 2 + 36x + 17 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 24x + 36 f’(x) = 3(x 2 - 8x + 12) = 3(x - 6)(x - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x) =3(x - 6)(x - 2)= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 or x = 6 Function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [2, 6] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty, 2) \cup(6, \infty){/tex}
solution: f (x) = x 3 - 12x 2 + 36x + 17 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 24x + 36 f’(x) = 3(x 2 - 8x + 12) = 3(x - 6)(x - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x) =3(x - 6)(x - 2)= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 or x = 6 Function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [2, 6] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty, 2) \cup(6, \infty){/tex}

f (x) = x3 - 12x2 + 36x + 17
f’(x) = 3x2 - 24x + 36
f’(x) = 3(x2 - 8x + 12)
= 3(x - 6)(x - 2) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f’(x) =3(x - 6)(x - 2)= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 or x = 6

Function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [2, 6] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty, 2) \cup(6, \infty){/tex}

question_html: Manufacturer can sell x items at a price of rupees {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. The cost price of x items is Rs {tex} \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}. Find the number of items he should sell to earn maximum profit.
question_text: Manufacturer can sell x items at a price of rupees {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. The cost price of x items is Rs {tex} \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} . Find the number of items he should sell to earn maximum profit.
explanation: Given manufacturer sells x items at a price of Rs. {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. {tex} \therefore{/tex} Total revenue obtained {tex} = Rs. \left[ x \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right) \right] = Rs. \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right){/tex} Also, cost price of x item {tex} = Rs. \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} Let P(x) be the profit function. We know that, Profit = Revenue - Cost {tex} \therefore \quad P = \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right) - \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } + \frac { 24 x } { 5 } - 500{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } = 0 \Rightarrow x = 240{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d P } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \frac { 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { 100 } = - \frac { 1 } { 50 } < 0{/tex} Thus, at x = 240, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0 \Rightarrow P{/tex} is maximum. Hence, number of items sold to have maximum profit is 240.
solution: Given manufacturer sells x items at a price of Rs. {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each. {tex} \therefore{/tex} Total revenue obtained {tex} = Rs. \left[ x \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right) \right] = Rs. \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right){/tex} Also, cost price of x item {tex} = Rs. \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} Let P(x) be the profit function. We know that, Profit = Revenue - Cost {tex} \therefore \quad P = \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right) - \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } + \frac { 24 x } { 5 } - 500{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 }{/tex} For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } = 0 \Rightarrow x = 240{/tex} Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d P } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \frac { 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 2 } { 100 } = - \frac { 1 } { 50 } < 0{/tex} Thus, at x = 240, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0 \Rightarrow P{/tex} is maximum. Hence, number of items sold to have maximum profit is 240.
solution_html:
Given manufacturer sells x items at a price of Rs. {tex} \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right){/tex} each.
{tex} \therefore{/tex} Total revenue obtained {tex} = Rs. \left[ x \left( 5 - \frac { x } { 100 } \right) \right] = Rs. \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right){/tex}
Also, cost price of x item {tex} = Rs. \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}
Let P(x) be the profit function.
We know that,
Profit = Revenue - Cost
{tex} \therefore \quad P = \left( 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } \right) - \left( \frac { x } { 5 } + 500 \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { 100 } + \frac { 24 x } { 5 } - 500{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 }{/tex}
For maxima or minima, put {tex} \frac { d P } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } = 0 \Rightarrow x = 240{/tex}
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d P } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \frac { 2 x } { 100 } + \frac { 24 } { 5 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = - \frac { 2 } { 100 } = - \frac { 1 } { 50 } < 0{/tex}
Thus, at x = 240, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d x ^ { 2 } } < 0 \Rightarrow P{/tex} is maximum.
Hence, number of items sold to have maximum profit is 240.

question_html: Find the interval in function f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2)2 is increasing or decreasing.
question_text: Find the interval in function f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 is increasing or decreasing.
explanation: Given, f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = (x - 1) {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1){/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 · 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) [2x - 2 + x - 2] {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) (3x - 4) On putting f'(x) = 0, we get (x - 2) (3x - 4) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = 0 or 3x - 4 = 0 Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) =(x - 2)(3x - 4) Sign of f'(x) x< {tex} \frac 43{/tex} (-)(-) +ve {tex} \frac 43{/tex} <x<2 (-)(+) -ve x > 2 (+)(+) +ve We know that, a fuction f(x) is sad to be a strictly increasing fuction, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing function when f'(x) < 0. So, f(x) is increasing on {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) \text { and } ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right){/tex} . Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = 4/3 and 2. Hence, given function is increasing on interval {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right] \text { and } [ 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on interval {tex} \left[ \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right]{/tex} .
solution: Given, f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get f'(x) = (x - 1) {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1){/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 · 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2) 2 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) [2x - 2 + x - 2] {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) (3x - 4) On putting f'(x) = 0, we get (x - 2) (3x - 4) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = 0 or 3x - 4 = 0 Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) =(x - 2)(3x - 4) Sign of f'(x) x< {tex} \frac 43{/tex} (-)(-) +ve {tex} \frac 43{/tex} <x<2 (-)(+) -ve x > 2 (+)(+) +ve We know that, a fuction f(x) is sad to be a strictly increasing fuction, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing function when f'(x) < 0. So, f(x) is increasing on {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) \text { and } ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right){/tex} . Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = 4/3 and 2. Hence, given function is increasing on interval {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right] \text { and } [ 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on interval {tex} \left[ \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right]{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2)2

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
f'(x) = (x - 1){tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - 1){/tex}[ by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2)2 · 1
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - 1)(x - 2) + (x - 2)2
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) [2x - 2 + x - 2]
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = (x - 2) (3x - 4)
On putting f'(x) = 0, we get
(x - 2) (3x - 4) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = 0 or 3x - 4 = 0
Now, we find the intervals in which f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) =(x - 2)(3x - 4) Sign of f'(x)
x<{tex} \frac 43{/tex} (-)(-) +ve
{tex} \frac 43{/tex}<x<2 (-)(+) -ve
x > 2 (+)(+) +ve

We know that, a fuction f(x) is sad to be a strictly increasing fuction, if f'(x) > 0 and strictly decreasing function when f'(x) < 0.  So, f(x) is increasing on {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) \text { and } ( 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on {tex} \left( \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right){/tex}. 
Since, f(x) is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at x = 4/3 and 2.
Hence, given function is increasing on interval {tex} \left( - \infty , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right] \text { and } [ 2 , \infty ){/tex} and decreasing on interval {tex} \left[ \frac { 4 } { 3 } , 2 \right]{/tex}.

question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} is x (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f given by f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} is x (i) increasing. (ii) decreasing.
explanation: f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} On putting f'(x) = 0, we get, {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 - 3 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x 6 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm{/tex} 1 Now, we find intervals in whch f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = {tex} \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Sign of f'(x) x<-1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +ve -1<x<1,x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex}\frac { ( - ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} -ve x > 1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +v So, f(x) is strictly increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1) and (1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ) and it is strictly decreasing on (-1, 1)-{0}. Also, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 , -1. Hence, f(x) is increasig on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1 ] and [1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ). decreasing on interval [-1, 1]- {0}.
solution: f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, f'(x) = {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} On putting f'(x) = 0, we get, {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 - 3 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 6 = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x 6 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm{/tex} 1 Now, we find intervals in whch f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. Interval f'(x) = {tex} \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Sign of f'(x) x<-1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +ve -1<x<1,x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex}\frac { ( - ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} -ve x > 1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +v So, f(x) is strictly increasing on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1) and (1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ) and it is strictly decreasing on (-1, 1)-{0}. Also, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 , -1. Hence, f(x) is increasig on intervals (- {tex} \infty{/tex} , -1 ] and [1, {tex} \infty{/tex} ). decreasing on interval [-1, 1]- {0}.
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex} x ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 3 } } , x \neq 0{/tex} 

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
f'(x) = {tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
On putting f'(x) = 0, we get,
{tex} 3 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x6 - 3 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}3x6 = 3
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x6 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex} \pm{/tex}1
Now, we find intervals in whch f(x) is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Interval f'(x) ={tex} \frac { 3 x ^ { 6 } - 3 } { x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Sign of f'(x)
x<-1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +ve
-1<x<1,x{tex}\ne{/tex}0 {tex}\frac { ( - ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} -ve
x > 1 {tex}\frac { ( + ) } { ( + ) }{/tex} +v

So, f(x) is strictly increasing on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex}, -1) and (1, {tex} \infty{/tex}) and it is strictly decreasing on (-1, 1)-{0}.
 Also, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 , -1.
 Hence, f(x) is

  1. increasig on intervals (-{tex} \infty{/tex}, -1 ] and [1, {tex} \infty{/tex}).
  2. decreasing on interval [-1, 1]- {0}.

question_html: An inverted cone has a depth of 10 cm and a base of radius 5 cm. Water is poured into it at the rate of {tex}\frac{3 }{2}{/tex} c.c. per minute. Find the rate at which the level of water in the cone is rising when the depth is 4 cm.
question_text: An inverted cone has a depth of 10 cm and a base of radius 5 cm. Water is poured into it at the rate of {tex}\frac{3 }{2}{/tex} c.c. per minute. Find the rate at which the level of water in the cone is rising when the depth is 4 cm.
explanation: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone VAB whose height VO is 10 cm and radius OB = 5 cm. Then, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the cone VA'B' after time t minutes and h be the height of water. Then, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(O' B^{\prime}\right)^{2}\left(V O^{\prime}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} ....[ {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} O'B' = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} h^{3}{/tex} .....[tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} 3 h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi h^{2}}{4}. \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} .... {tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \text { minute (given) }\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dh}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi h^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi(4)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8 \pi}{/tex} cm/min.
solution: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone VAB whose height VO is 10 cm and radius OB = 5 cm. Then, tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the cone VA'B' after time t minutes and h be the height of water. Then, V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(O' B^{\prime}\right)^{2}\left(V O^{\prime}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} ....[ {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} O'B' = h tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} h^{3}{/tex} .....[tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} 3 h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi h^{2}}{4}. \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} .... {tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \text { minute (given) }\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dh}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi h^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi(4)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8 \pi}{/tex} cm/min.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the semi-vertical angle of the cone VAB whose height VO is 10 cm and radius OB = 5 cm. Then,
tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Let V be the volume of the water in the cone i.e. the volume of the cone VA'B' after time t minutes and h be the height of water. Then,
V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(O' B^{\prime}\right)^{2}\left(V O^{\prime}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha{/tex} ....[{tex}\because{/tex} tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{V O^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{O^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} O'B' = h tan{tex}\alpha{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} h^{3}{/tex}   .....[tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{12} 3 h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4} h^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi h^{2}}{4}. \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} ....{tex}\left[\because \frac{d V}{d t}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \text { minute (given) }\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dh}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi h^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d h}{d t}\right)_{h=4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\pi(4)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8 \pi}{/tex} cm/min.

question_html: Show that f(x) = {tex}\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right){/tex} is decreasing [-1, 1].
question_text: Show that f(x) = {tex}\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right){/tex} is decreasing [-1, 1].
explanation: f(x) = x 3 + x -3 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 3x ‑4 = {tex}3(x^2-\frac{1}{x^4}){/tex} {tex}=3\left(\frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{2}} \cdot \frac{x^{3}+1}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(x+1)\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)}{x^{4}}{/tex} Root of f’(x) = 1 and -1 x= -1 and x =1 Here we can clearly see that f’(x) is decreasing in [-1, 1] So, f(x) is decreasing in interval [-1, 1]
solution: f(x) = x 3 + x -3 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 3x ‑4 = {tex}3(x^2-\frac{1}{x^4}){/tex} {tex}=3\left(\frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{2}} \cdot \frac{x^{3}+1}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(x+1)\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)}{x^{4}}{/tex} Root of f’(x) = 1 and -1 x= -1 and x =1 Here we can clearly see that f’(x) is decreasing in [-1, 1] So, f(x) is decreasing in interval [-1, 1]
solution_html:
f(x) = x3 + x-3
f’(x) = 3x2 - 3x‑4
= {tex}3(x^2-\frac{1}{x^4}){/tex}
{tex}=3\left(\frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{2}} \cdot \frac{x^{3}+1}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(x+1)\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)}{x^{4}}{/tex}
Root of f’(x) = 1 and -1   x= -1 and x =1

Here we can clearly see that f’(x) is decreasing in [-1, 1]
So, f(x) is decreasing in interval [-1, 1]

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the intervals of function f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2)2 is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals of function f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 is increasing decreasing.
explanation: Given function is f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 = x 2 - 4x + 4 (x - 1) = x 3 - 4x 2 + 4x - x 2 + 4x - 4 f(x) = x 3 - 5x 2 + 8x - 4 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 10x + 8 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 6x - 4x + 8 = 3x(x - 2) -4(x - 2) = (3x - 4)(x - 2) Function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [4/3, 2] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty, 4 / 3) \cup(2, \infty){/tex} .
solution: Given function is f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2) 2 = x 2 - 4x + 4 (x - 1) = x 3 - 4x 2 + 4x - x 2 + 4x - 4 f(x) = x 3 - 5x 2 + 8x - 4 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 10x + 8 f’(x) = 3x 2 - 6x - 4x + 8 = 3x(x - 2) -4(x - 2) = (3x - 4)(x - 2) Function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [4/3, 2] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty, 4 / 3) \cup(2, \infty){/tex} .
solution_html:
Given function is f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 2)2 = x2 - 4x + 4 (x - 1)
= x3 - 4x2 + 4x - x2 + 4x - 4
f(x) = x3 - 5x2 + 8x - 4
f’(x) = 3x2 - 10x + 8
f’(x) = 3x2 - 6x - 4x + 8
= 3x(x - 2) -4(x - 2)
= (3x - 4)(x - 2)

Function f(x) is decreasing for x{tex}\in{/tex}[4/3, 2] and increasing in x{tex}\in{/tex}{tex}(-\infty, 4 / 3) \cup(2, \infty){/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the intervals of function f(x) = (2x3 + 9x2 + 12x + 15) is

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing.
question_text: Find the intervals of function f(x) = (2x 3 + 9x 2 + 12x + 15) is increasing decreasing.
explanation: Given,f(x) = 2x 3 + 9x 2 + 12x + 15 f’(x) = 6x 2 + 18x + 12 f’(x) = 6(x 2 + 3x + 2) = 6(x + 2)(x + 1) Therefore,function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, -2] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty,-1) \cup(-2, \infty){/tex}
solution: Given,f(x) = 2x 3 + 9x 2 + 12x + 15 f’(x) = 6x 2 + 18x + 12 f’(x) = 6(x 2 + 3x + 2) = 6(x + 2)(x + 1) Therefore,function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, -2] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty,-1) \cup(-2, \infty){/tex}
solution_html:
Given,f(x) = 2x3 + 9x2 + 12x + 15
f’(x) = 6x2 + 18x + 12
f’(x) = 6(x2 + 3x + 2)
= 6(x + 2)(x + 1)

Therefore,function f(x) is decreasing for x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, -2] and increasing in x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}(-\infty,-1) \cup(-2, \infty){/tex}

question_html: The two equal sides of an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} with fixed base b are decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/s. How fast is the area decreasing when the two equal sides are equal to the base?
question_text: The two equal sides of an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} with fixed base b are decreasing at the rate of 3 cm/s. How fast is the area decreasing when the two equal sides are equal to the base?
explanation: Let at any time t, the length of each equal side be x cm and area of triangle be A.Then. {tex}A = \frac{1}{2}BC \times AD = \frac{1}{2}b\sqrt {{x^2} - \frac{{{b^2}}}{4}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{b}{4} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }} \times 8x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\left( {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}} \right)_{x = b}} = \frac{{3{b^2}}}{{\sqrt {4{b^2} - {b^2}} }} = \sqrt 3 b{/tex} cm 2 /sec
solution: Let at any time t, the length of each equal side be x cm and area of triangle be A.Then. {tex}A = \frac{1}{2}BC \times AD = \frac{1}{2}b\sqrt {{x^2} - \frac{{{b^2}}}{4}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{b}{4} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }} \times 8x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\left( {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}} \right)_{x = b}} = \frac{{3{b^2}}}{{\sqrt {4{b^2} - {b^2}} }} = \sqrt 3 b{/tex} cm 2 /sec
solution_html:
Let at any time t, the length of each equal side be x cm and area of triangle be A.Then.
{tex}A = \frac{1}{2}BC \times AD = \frac{1}{2}b\sqrt {{x^2} - \frac{{{b^2}}}{4}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{b}{4} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }} \times 8x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3bx}}{{\sqrt {4{x^2} - {b^2}} }}\left( {\because \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3cm/\sec } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dA}}{{dt}}} \right)_{x = b}} = \frac{{3{b^2}}}{{\sqrt {4{b^2} - {b^2}} }} = \sqrt 3 b{/tex} cm2/sec

question_html: Find the largest possible area of a right-angled triangle whose hypotenuse is 5 cm long.
question_text: Find the largest possible area of a right-angled triangle whose hypotenuse is 5 cm long.
explanation: ABC is a right angled triangle. Hypotenuse h = AC = 5cm. Let x and y one the other two side of the triangle. {tex}\because{/tex} x 2 = y 2 = 25 ... (i) {tex}\because{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} B C \times A B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy ... (ii) From equation(i) and equation(ii) S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\times \sqrt{25-x}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sqrt{25-x^{2}}-\frac{2 x^{2}}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[25-x^{2}-x^{2}\right]}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} For maxima and minima, {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}5 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\sqrt{25-x^{2}} \times(-4 x)+\frac{\left(25-2 x^{2}\right) 2 x}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}}{\left(25-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[-\frac{25}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}+0\right]}{\frac{25}{2}}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} < 0 {tex}\because{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is a point local maxima, {tex}\because{/tex} The largest possible area {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy Area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Area = {tex}\frac{25}{4}{/tex} cm 2 .
solution: ABC is a right angled triangle. Hypotenuse h = AC = 5cm. Let x and y one the other two side of the triangle. {tex}\because{/tex} x 2 = y 2 = 25 ... (i) {tex}\because{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} B C \times A B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy ... (ii) From equation(i) and equation(ii) S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\times \sqrt{25-x}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sqrt{25-x^{2}}-\frac{2 x^{2}}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[25-x^{2}-x^{2}\right]}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} For maxima and minima, {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}5 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\sqrt{25-x^{2}} \times(-4 x)+\frac{\left(25-2 x^{2}\right) 2 x}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}}{\left(25-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} At x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[-\frac{25}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}+0\right]}{\frac{25}{2}}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} < 0 {tex}\because{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is a point local maxima, {tex}\because{/tex} The largest possible area {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy Area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Area = {tex}\frac{25}{4}{/tex} cm 2 .
solution_html:
ABC is a right angled triangle. Hypotenuse h = AC = 5cm.
Let x and y one the other two side of the triangle.
{tex}\because{/tex} x2 = y2 = 25   ... (i)
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} B C \times A B{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy    ... (ii)
From equation(i) and equation(ii)
S = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\times \sqrt{25-x}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sqrt{25-x^{2}}-\frac{2 x^{2}}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[25-x^{2}-x^{2}\right]}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex}
For maxima and minima,
{tex}\frac{d s}{d x}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{25-2 x^{2}}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}5 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\sqrt{25-x^{2}} \times(-4 x)+\frac{\left(25-2 x^{2}\right) 2 x}{2 \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}}{\left(25-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
At  x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[-\frac{25}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}+0\right]}{\frac{25}{2}}{/tex}
= - {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} < 0
{tex}\because{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is a point local maxima,
{tex}\because{/tex} The largest possible area {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} xy
Area = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Area = {tex}\frac{25}{4}{/tex}cm2.
