question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 7 cm3 /sec. How fast is its surface area increasing at the instant when the length of an edge of the cube is 12 cm?
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 7 cm 3 /sec. How fast is its surface area increasing at the instant when the length of an edge of the cube is 12 cm?
explanation: At any instant t, let the length of each edge of the cube be x, V be its volume and S be its surface area. Then, {tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = 7 cm 3 / sec ... (given) ... (i) Now, V = x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dV}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{3}\right) \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} .... [ {tex}\because{/tex} V = x 3 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 6x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dS}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dS}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(6 x^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(12 x \times \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{28}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{dS}{dt}\right]_{x=12}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{28}{12}\right){/tex} cm 2 /sec = {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex} Hence, the surface area of the cube is increasing at the rate of {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex} at the instant when its edge is 12 cm.
solution: At any instant t, let the length of each edge of the cube be x, V be its volume and S be its surface area. Then, {tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = 7 cm 3 / sec ... (given) ... (i) Now, V = x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dV}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{3}\right) \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} .... [ {tex}\because{/tex} V = x 3 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 6x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dS}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dS}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(6 x^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(12 x \times \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{28}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{dS}{dt}\right]_{x=12}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{28}{12}\right){/tex} cm 2 /sec = {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex} Hence, the surface area of the cube is increasing at the rate of {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex} at the instant when its edge is 12 cm.
solution_html:
At any instant t, let the length of each edge of the cube be x, V be its volume and S be its surface area. Then,
{tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = 7 cm3 / sec ... (given) ... (i)
Now, V = x3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dV}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dV}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{3}\right) \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} .... [{tex}\because{/tex} V = x3]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}3 x^{2} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 6x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dS}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dS}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{d}{dx}\left(6 x^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(12 x \times \frac{7}{3 x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{28}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{dS}{dt}\right]_{x=12}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{28}{12}\right){/tex} cm2 /sec = {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex}
Hence, the surface area of the cube is increasing at the rate of {tex}2 \frac{1}{3} \;{cm}^{2} /{sec}{/tex} at the instant when its edge is 12 cm.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A point source of light along a straight road is at a height of a metres. A boy b metres in height is walking along the road. How fast is his shadow increasing if he is walking away from the light at the rate of c metres per minute?
question_text: A point source of light along a straight road is at a height of a metres. A boy b metres in height is walking along the road. How fast is his shadow increasing if he is walking away from the light at the rate of c metres per minute?
explanation: Suppose AB be the lamp post, the lamp being at B. Then, AB = a metres. At any instant t, suppose CD be the position of the boy and CE be his shadow. Then, CD = b metres. Let AC = x metres and CD = y metres. Given that {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = c metres /min Clearly, {tex}\triangle B A E \sim \triangle D C E{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{A B}{C D}=\frac{A E}{C E}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b)y = bx {tex}\Rightarrow(a-b) \frac{dy}{dt}=b \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}=b c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{dx}{dt}=c\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}=\frac{b c}{(a-b)} {/tex} metres/min Therefore ,the shadow is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{b c}{(a-b)}{/tex} metres/min
solution: Suppose AB be the lamp post, the lamp being at B. Then, AB = a metres. At any instant t, suppose CD be the position of the boy and CE be his shadow. Then, CD = b metres. Let AC = x metres and CD = y metres. Given that {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = c metres /min Clearly, {tex}\triangle B A E \sim \triangle D C E{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{A B}{C D}=\frac{A E}{C E}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b)y = bx {tex}\Rightarrow(a-b) \frac{dy}{dt}=b \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}=b c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{dx}{dt}=c\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}=\frac{b c}{(a-b)} {/tex} metres/min Therefore ,the shadow is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{b c}{(a-b)}{/tex} metres/min
solution_html:
Suppose AB be the lamp post, the lamp being at B. Then, AB = a metres. At any instant t, suppose CD be the position of the boy and CE be his shadow.
Then, CD = b metres.
Let AC = x metres and CD = y metres.
Given that {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex}= c metres /min

Clearly, {tex}\triangle B A E \sim \triangle D C E{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{A B}{C D}=\frac{A E}{C E}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{x+y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(a - b)y = bx
{tex}\Rightarrow(a-b) \frac{dy}{dt}=b \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}=b c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{dx}{dt}=c\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}=\frac{b c}{(a-b)} {/tex}metres/min
Therefore ,the shadow is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{b c}{(a-b)}{/tex}metres/min

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Using differentials, find the approximate value of {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
question_text: Using differentials, find the approximate value of {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}y=x^{1 / 4}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{16}{81}\right){/tex} and {tex}\Delta x=\frac{1}{81}{/tex} Then, {tex}\Delta y=(x+\Delta x)^{\frac{1}{4}}-x^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{2}{3}+\Delta y{/tex} Now, dy is approximately equal to {tex}\Delta y{/tex} and is given by {tex}d y=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \Delta x=\frac{1}{4(x)^{\frac{3}{4}}}(\Delta x){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}}\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)=\frac{27}{4 \times 8}=\frac{1}{32 \times 3}=\frac{1}{96}=0.010{/tex} Therefore, the approximate value of {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2}{3}+0.010=0.677{/tex} .
solution: Let {tex}y=x^{1 / 4}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{16}{81}\right){/tex} and {tex}\Delta x=\frac{1}{81}{/tex} Then, {tex}\Delta y=(x+\Delta x)^{\frac{1}{4}}-x^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{2}{3}+\Delta y{/tex} Now, dy is approximately equal to {tex}\Delta y{/tex} and is given by {tex}d y=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \Delta x=\frac{1}{4(x)^{\frac{3}{4}}}(\Delta x){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}}\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)=\frac{27}{4 \times 8}=\frac{1}{32 \times 3}=\frac{1}{96}=0.010{/tex} Therefore, the approximate value of {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2}{3}+0.010=0.677{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let   {tex}y=x^{1 / 4}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{16}{81}\right){/tex} and {tex}\Delta x=\frac{1}{81}{/tex} 
Then, {tex}\Delta y=(x+\Delta x)^{\frac{1}{4}}-x^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{2}{3}+\Delta y{/tex} 
Now, dy is approximately equal to {tex}\Delta y{/tex} and is given by
{tex}d y=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \Delta x=\frac{1}{4(x)^{\frac{3}{4}}}(\Delta x){/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{1}{4\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}}\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)=\frac{27}{4 \times 8}=\frac{1}{32 \times 3}=\frac{1}{96}=0.010{/tex} 
Therefore, the approximate value of {tex}\left(\frac{17}{81}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2}{3}+0.010=0.677{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The surface area of a spherical bubble is increasing at the rate of 2 cm2/s. When the radius of the bubble is 6 cm, at what rate is the volume of the bubble increasing?
question_text: The surface area of a spherical bubble is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /s. When the radius of the bubble is 6 cm, at what rate is the volume of the bubble increasing?
explanation: We are given that, {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=2 \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} {tex}\text { To find } \frac{d V}{d t} \text { at } r=6 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} {tex}A=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}2=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{1}{4 \pi r} \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} {tex}\text { Now, } \quad V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=4 \pi r^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi r}\right){/tex} = r {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} So, volume of bubble is increasing at the rate of 6 cm 3 sec
solution: We are given that, {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=2 \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} {tex}\text { To find } \frac{d V}{d t} \text { at } r=6 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} {tex}A=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}2=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{1}{4 \pi r} \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} {tex}\text { Now, } \quad V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=4 \pi r^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi r}\right){/tex} = r {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex} So, volume of bubble is increasing at the rate of 6 cm 3 sec
solution_html:
We are given that,

 {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=2 \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}
{tex}\text { To find } \frac{d V}{d t} \text { at } r=6 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}A=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}2=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{1}{4 \pi r} \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Now, } \quad V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}=4 \pi r^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi r}\right){/tex}
= r
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6 \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}
So, volume of bubble is increasing at the rate of 6 cm3 sec

question_html: The function f(x) = sin4 x + cos4 x increases if
question_text: The function f(x) = sin 4 x + cos 4 x increases if

options.explanation: f(x) = sin 4 x + cos 4 x = f(x) = 4 sin 3 x cos x - 4 cos 3 x sin x f(x) is increasing if f'(x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 sin 3 x cos x - 4 cos 3 x sin x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 sin x cos x (sin 2 x - cos 2 x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2(2 sin x cos x) (cos 2 x - sin 2 x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 sin 2 x cos 2x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -sin 4x > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} -sin 4x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 4x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {\pi}{/tex} < 4x < 2 {tex}\pi \Rightarrow \frac {\pi}4{/tex} < x {tex}\frac {\pi} 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {\pi}4{/tex} < x < {tex}\frac {3\pi}8{/tex}

explanation: f(x) = sin 4 x + cos 4 x = f(x) = 4 sin 3 x cos x - 4 cos 3 x sin x f(x) is increasing if f'(x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 sin 3 x cos x - 4 cos 3 x sin x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 sin x cos x (sin 2 x - cos 2 x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2(2 sin x cos x) (cos 2 x - sin 2 x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 sin 2 x cos 2x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -sin 4x > 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} -sin 4x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 4x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {\pi}{/tex} < 4x < 2 {tex}\pi \Rightarrow \frac {\pi}4{/tex} < x {tex}\frac {\pi} 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {\pi}4{/tex} < x < {tex}\frac {3\pi}8{/tex}
